The small image separation and the emission line suggest that if the system is a lens, the deflector is a late-type galaxy, and thus not one of the more common early-type deflectors considered in our forecasts.

Based on the analysis of 0 4 seeing Gemini-S images of the candidate quad DESJ2346-5203, and we conclude that this is not a quadruply imaged quasar in a classic configuration. If it is a multiply imaged quasar the morphology requires a complex deflector or extreme flux ratio anomalies.

gmw_00009415.name: SRGA J183220.1 103508
gmw_00009415.overview: SRGA J183220.1 103508 is an extragalactic object identified as a radio-loud active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2402.07290]. The source is located within a galaxy cluster at a redshift of approximately 0.121 [arXiv:2402.07290].

sources.summary: The paper studies SRGA J183220.1 103508 as an extended extragalactic X-ray source with a bright radio counterpart, concluding it is a radio-loud AGN in a galaxy cluster.

quotes.quote: The second source -- SRGA J183220.1 103508 -- is located approximately at 20 from SNR 21.5-0.9, which has been repeatedly observed previously with different X-ray telescopes as a calibration target. In the Chandra 2016ApJS..224...40W and XMM-Newton 2020A&A...641A.136W catalogs this source, known under the name CXOGSG J183220.8 103510, is marked as extended. In addition, it coincides with a bright (1.1 Jy) radio source. The set of these characteristics and the available observational data determine the heightened interest in this object and allow its nature to be studied in more detail.

Thus, the galactic nature of the source can be ruled out and, hence, it is an extragalactic object. In that case, it could be assumed that the point source is an AGN at z 0.08 , while the emission line is the fluorescent Fe K , line whose equivalent width in Compton-thick AGNs can exceed 1 keV 2010ApJ...725.2381L .
Thus, based on the available data, we can draw the preliminary conclusion that SRGA J183220.1 103508 is a radio-loud AGN located in a galaxy cluster at z=0.121 .

gmw_00009416.name: NGC 7603B
gmw_00009416.overview: NGC 7603B is a spiral galaxy located 59 arcseconds to the southeast of the larger galaxy NGC 7603 [arXiv:astro-ph/0509630]. It has a higher redshift of z=0.0569 and lies along an apparently luminous filament that connects the two objects [arXiv:astro-ph/0509630].

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 7603B as a higher-redshift spiral companion near NGC 7603, lying along a luminous filament and considered part of a possible anomalous-redshift association.

quotes.quote: A fact that has attracted attention is the proximity of NGC 7603B, a spiral galaxy with higher redshift ( z=0.0569 ) located 59 arcseconds to the SE of NGC 7603 . The angular proximity of both galaxies and the apparently luminous connection between them makes the system an important example of a possible anomalous redshift association. Arp has claimed that the compact member, NGC 7603B, was somehow ejected from the bigger object. Moreover, there are also two objects overimposed on the filament apparently connecting both galaxies. We identified several emission lines in the spectra of the two knots, and from the emission lines of H , H , OII, doublet OIII, NII-6584 \ we determined their redshifts to be 0.394 0.002 and 0.245 0.002 for the objects closest to and farthest from NGC 7603 respectively .

gmw_00009417.name: MCG 7-25-46
gmw_00009417.overview: MCG 7-25-46 is a galaxy system featuring a main galaxy connected to a smaller companion by a bridge [arXiv:astro-ph/0509630]. This bridge shares the redshift of the main galaxy and terminates where the emission of the discordant, higher-redshift companion begins [arXiv:astro-ph/0509630].

sources.summary: The paper describes MCG 7-25-46 as a bridge-connected two-galaxy system with discordant redshifts, H-alpha emission mapped in two dimensions, and a filament aligned toward the companion.

quotes.quote: MCG 7-25-46 (or UGC 7175) (Fig. Fig:UGC7175 ) was also analysed by Arp : a system with two galaxies connected by a bridge and with different redshift: z=0.003 for the main galaxy and z=0.098 for the small one. From our analysis, it is relevant that the bridge has the same redshift as the main galaxy (see Fig. Fig:UGC7175v ). This had already been observed by , but they took a single slit spectrum, and we could produce a 2D map of the H _ emission. also observed perturbation, due possibly to interaction, in the higher redshift object. As in NGC 7603, one could ask why MCG 7-25-46 ejects a filament/bridge in the direction of the discordant redshift companion and not in other directions. There is the further interesting observation that the H _ emission at z=0.003 finishes exactly where the H _ emission of the galaxy with z=0.098 begins (which is supposed to be in the background), there is no overlap in the two emissions.

gmw_00009418.name: GC 0248+430
gmw_00009418.overview: GC 0248+430 is a disturbed, merging galaxy pair at z=0.0519 consisting of a spiral galaxy and an elliptical galaxy [arXiv:1901.04683]. The system features a Seyfert nucleus and a LINER nucleus [arXiv:1901.04683], with a tidal tail extending toward a background quasar [arXiv:1901.04683, arXiv:astro-ph/0509630].

sources.summary: The paper describes G0248+430 as a z=0.0519 pair of merging active galaxies whose tidal tail produces absorption toward a background quasar and whose foreground galaxies emit CO.

quotes.quote: We report here the detection with NOEMA of CO(1-0) and CN(1-0) lines in absorption, and the confirmation of CO emission in the quasar/galaxy pair Q0248+430/G0248+430. The system G0248+430 corresponds to two merging galaxies (a Seyfert and a LINER) at z=0.0519 with a tidal tail just on the line of sight to the background quasar Q0248+430 at z = 1.313. Optical (CaII, NaI), , and OH-1667 MHz absorption lines associated with the tidal tail of the foreground system have previously been detected toward the quasar, while four CO lines at different rotation J levels have been detected in emission from the foreground galaxies.

In this paper we report the discovery of CO absorption at z 0.05, in front of the quasar Q0248 at z=1.31 Kuehr1977 . The absorption comes from a foreground pair of galaxies called G0248 Junkkarinen1987 , consisting of the two sources G1 and G2. A tidal tail connects, in projection, G2 with the background quasar.

sources.summary: The paper describes GC 0248+430 as a disturbed double-nucleus galaxy whose nuclei, gas extension, and nearby quasars are closely aligned, yielding a low-probability apparent association.

quotes.quote: Fig:GC0248 shows the double nucleus galaxy GC 0248+430 and the field surrounding it. The first quasar with z=1.311 , m_R=17.45 was studied previously (our photometry gave m_ ' =18.80 for observation on 2002 December 3; the difference is possibly due to variability), and the remarkable coincidence was that the double nucleus of the galaxy and the QSO lie along a line. Moreover, the gas extends in the direction of this QSO and beyond it, and the galaxy is disturbed, due presumably to some interaction.

The position angle of the first QSO with respect to the major nucleus of the galaxy is -73 ^ at a distance of 14.4" from the major nucleus; and the position angle of the second QSO is -68 ^ at a distance of 108 '' from the major nucleus. The position angle of the line which joins the two nuclei of the galaxy is -77 18 ^ (the error bar is large because both nuclei are very close---2.7 '' --and it was not possible to determine the position of the second centre accurately). Even if we forget the second nucleus and the gas ejection, all in this direction , given the low density of expected background quasars, the coincidence of the near-alignment (the difference is 5 degrees) seems unlikely.

sources.summary: The paper identifies G1 as one component of the foreground G0248 pair, with nonthermal nuclear activity, LINER excitation, systematic rotation, and spiral-galaxy spectra.

quotes.quote: The associated pair of galaxies G0248 is a violent starburst, according to its IRAS far-IR (FIR) flux density measurements, corresponding to L _ FIR = 4.3 10 ^ 11 L _ , and a star formation rate (SFR) of 74 M _ /yr (see Table tab:G0248 ), and the G1 and G2 nuclei (separated by 3.5'' = 3.5 kpc) both show nonthermal activity Borgeest1991, Kollatschny1991 . Indeed, G1 is a LINER and G2 a Seyfert 2, and based on their optical emission line excitation both are located in the AGN region of the Baldwin-Phillips-Terlevich (BPT) diagram Baldwin1981 , plotting [ OIII ] /H as a function of [ NII ] /H .

determine the kinematics of the two merging galaxies, the LINER G1 and Seyfert G2; the former shows systematic rotation, but the latter does not. With respect to G2 (z=0.0507), a constant velocity of 100 \ is observed all across the tidal tail. Furthermore, from their spectra, they conclude that G1 is a spiral galaxy and G2 an elliptical.

gmw_00009419.name: B2 1637+29
gmw_00009419.overview: The system B2 1637+29 is characterized either as a double-nucleus galaxy or as two separate galaxies [arXiv:astro-ph/0509630]. It is associated with a poor group of galaxies and displays a peculiar head-tail radio morphology featuring twin bent jets and oscillating tails [arXiv:0908.4391].

sources.summary: The paper describes B2 1637+29 as either a double-nucleus galaxy or two galaxies, with a newly found aligned emission-line galaxy and a very low calculated projection probability.

quotes.quote: Fig:B21637 shows the system B2 1637+29, either a galaxy with two nuclei separated by 4100 km/s or two separate galaxies, and the field surrounding it. We have serendipitously discovered another galaxy with z=0.568 , m_ u' =19.94 , m_ g' =20.75 , m_ ' =20.56 (SDSS). We detected the emission lines of OII, NeIII, OIII, and MgII (broad?, it is not clear; the classification as QSO/Seyfert 1 is not discarded although, in principle, we simply claim that it is an emission line galaxy; from the images it looks like an extended object); see Fig. Fig:spectra . There is a fainter object which connects the isophote of this object with those of B2 1637+39, but it was too faint to derive its redshift.

The position angle of the line which joins the two nuclei of the galaxy is -22.0 ^ and the separation is 6.6 '' . The position angle of the second galaxy with respect to the major nucleus of the galaxy is -22.6 ^ at a distance of 22.4 '' from the major nucleus, so there is a difference of only 0.6 degrees (from SDSS astrometry, the number increases to 1.1 degrees). Indeed, the detection of this new object was totally fortuitous: we placed the slit to take the spectra of both objects separated by 4100 km/s and by chance we took the spectrum of this object.

sources.summary: The paper describes 1637+29 as a peculiar head-tail source in a poor group, with twin bent jets, oscillating tails, and an unresolved VLBA component.

quotes.quote: 1637+29 -- This peculiar source displays a head-tail morphology associated with a poor group of galaxies ( ). The radio source consists of twin bent jets, with the main jet ending in a very bright lobe and two radio tails showing conspicuous oscillations. Our VLBA image shows an unresolved component with total flux density S_5 =7.8 mJy. The ratio between the VLBA correlated flux and the arcsecond core flux density at 5 GHz is 60 .

gmw_00009420.name: UGC 6514 E
gmw_00009420.overview: UGC 6514 E is a blue emission-line galaxy that is classified as either a blue compact, a spiral, or a LINER [arXiv:astro-ph/0509630]. It is a discordant-redshift member of the VV172 group, showing evidence of a halo interaction and a possible high-velocity outflow [arXiv:astro-ph/0509630].

sources.summary: The paper identifies UGC 6514 E as the discordant-redshift, blue emission-line member of VV172, with evidence for a halo interaction and a possible high-velocity outflow.

quotes.quote: Fig:VV172 , it consists of a chain of 5 galaxies in which one has a discordant redshift (UGC 6514 E with z=0.123 instead of z=0.052-0.054 for the other galaxies). The object with the discordant redshift is an emission line galaxy, either an HII galaxy or a LINER, according to our spectrum (why is it that most of the galaxies in scenarios of discordant redshift are emission-line galaxies?); it is also very blue ( B-V=0.89 , plus 0.42 for the K-correction). It is either a blue compact or a spiral, perhaps a spiral because some structure was observed in their images. It has been analysed previously and was a matter of discussion during the '70s and early '80s; later on it was apparently forgotten.

The extent and the smoothness of its enveloping halo, especially in the vicinity of the discordant galaxy, was the strongest direct evidence for interaction . The halo could not be explained by the simple overlapping of four or five normal galaxy envelopes, suggesting that a significant part of the halo is composed of stars governed by the overall gravitational potential of the group .

All this information indicates that the lines are duplicated (we do not see all of them because there is superposition of the two sets of lines) and we have two components with a difference of velocity of +700 km/s in the same galaxy, possibly a high velocity outflow: the main galaxy is ejecting some material, which is precisely what we are observing with an excess of 700 km/s. This would indicate that ejection of matter is taking place in this galaxy.

gmw_00009421.name: VDES J2325-5229
gmw_00009421.overview: VDES J2325-5229 is a high-redshift lensed quasar system with a source redshift of 2.739 [arXiv:1607.01391]. The system features two blue images separated by 2.9 arcseconds, which are gravitationally lensed by a foreground early-type galaxy at a redshift of 0.400 [arXiv:1607.01391].

sources.summary: The paper identifies VDES J2325-5229 as a high-redshift lensed quasar system with two blue images, a bright lensing galaxy, source redshift 2.739, lens redshift 0.400, and 52-day delay.

quotes.quote: Colour composites made of DES g , , and i bands were generated and VDES J2325-5229, shown in Fig. fig:residuals , stood out. The separation between the two blue sources, is 2.9". Despite clearly having three main components, J2325-5229 is only deblended into a double source in the DES catalogue and appears as a single source in the VHS catalogue.

Spectroscopic follow-up with NTT and the use of an archival AAT spectrum show that the point sources can be identified as a lensed quasar with an emission line redshift of z=2.739 0.003 and a foreground early type galaxy with z=0.400 0.002 . We model the system as a single isothermal ellipsoid and find the Einstein radius _E 1.47'' , enclosed mass M_ enc 4 10^ 11 M _ and a time delay of 52 days.

We have identified a high redshift lensed quasar, VDES J2325-5229, by applying GMM supervised machine learning to select the candidates in a colour space defined by DES+VHS+WISE photometric bands. Since the selection does not depend on the u band, we are capable of selecting candidates at higher redshifts. For comparison, amongst the 49 new lenses found by SQLS, only 6 have source redshifts greater than J2325, 3 of which were serendipitously discovered Johnston+2003,Pindor+2004,McGreer+2010 .

gmw_00009422.name: FCC032
gmw_00009422.overview: FCC032 is a Fornax dwarf elliptical galaxy that contains a large, extended ionized gas complex [arXiv:astro-ph/0406543]. This complex is composed of multiple emission clouds, a superbubble, and a filament that suggest recent star formation [arXiv:astro-ph/0406543].

sources.summary: The paper describes FCC032 as a Fornax dwarf elliptical with the richest ionized gas in the sample, including multiple emission clouds, a superbubble, and a filament suggestive of recent star formation.

quotes.quote: FCC032 harbours a large ionized gas complex, consisting of several individual clouds, a superbubble and a filament that extends away from the galaxy centre. The ionized gas structures observed in FCC032 bear a strong resemblance to those observed in more gas-rich and more fiercely star-forming dwarf galaxies.

FCC032 FCC032 is the most gas-rich galaxy in our sample and contains an extended ionized gas complex, about 10 arcsec ( 850 pc) across. The bulk of the H emission in FCC032 is distributed over 4 different emission clouds, 2 of which are clearly visible in the R band.

The diameter of the bubble in FCC032 is comparable to those found in hydrodynamical simulations of supernova remnants (SNRs) db01 and those observed in irregular dwarf galaxies by martin98 , who measures (super)bubble diameters of 100 to 2000 pc.

gmw_00009423.name: FCC206
gmw_00009423.overview: FCC206 is a non-nucleated dwarf elliptical galaxy with a very low surface brightness [arXiv:astro-ph/0406543]. It contains four blue star clusters that may be merging to form a central nucleus, as well as distinct emission regions [arXiv:astro-ph/0406543].

sources.summary: The paper presents FCC206 as a very low surface brightness, non-nucleated dwarf elliptical with separated emission clouds, blue central clusters, and possible nucleus formation by merging clusters.

quotes.quote: FCC206, a very low surface brightness dE, contains one faint extended emission region, and two compact clouds.
Inside FCC206, four star clusters appear to be on the verge of merging to form a nucleus in this presently non-nucleated dE.

Formation of the central nucleus FCC206 is a non-nucleated dE but it contains 4 non-resolved star clusters, two of which show H emission. These knots have blue colours and luminosities comparable to globular clusters, which tempted caldwell87 to suggest we are witnessing the formation of a nucleus. This corroborates one currently popular hypothesis concerning the formation of nuclei in dEs, namely the merger of globular clusters that have been driven inward by dynamical friction ( ol00 , lotz01 ).

gmw_00009424.name: FCCB729
gmw_00009424.overview: FCCB729 is a nucleated dwarf galaxy located in the Fornax Cluster [arXiv:astro-ph/0406543]. It features a disky structure with an exponential surface brightness profile and an extended central region of ionized gas emission that coincides with its stellar nucleus [arXiv:astro-ph/0406543].

sources.summary: The paper identifies FCCB729 as a true Fornax dwarf galaxy with a nucleus, disky structure, and central ionized emission coincident with the stellar nucleus.

quotes.quote: In FCCB729, the only nucleated galaxy in this sample, one of the ionized gas clouds coincides with the stellar nucleus.

FCCB729 Due to its relatively high surface brightness, FCCB729 was originally classified as a background galaxy ferguson89 . However, redshift measurements by drink01 identify FCCB729 as a true Fornax Cluster member. Moreover, FCCB729 has a nearly exponential surface brightness profile, typical of a genuine dwarf galaxy.

FCCB729 FCCB729 has an extended emission region in the centre, with faint extensions towards the west, along the galaxy's major axis, and towards the north, along its minor axis. The emission peak coincides with the galaxy's central nucleus.

gmw_00009425.name: Pox 186
gmw_00009425.overview: Pox 186 is a local blue compact dwarf galaxy located at a redshift of approximately 0.0040705 [arXiv:2307.00059]. It is an ultra-compact galaxy still in the process of formation, with most of its star formation concentrated in a central star cluster [arXiv:2307.00059].

sources.summary: The paper probes Pox 186 as a blue compact dwarf EoR analogue with extreme carbon lines, high FIR line ratio, high ionizing escape fraction, and compact star formation.

quotes.quote: We probe the ionized carbon content of a blue compact dwarf galaxy Pox 186 using the UV, optical, mid-infrared and FIR data taken with telescopes in space (Hubble, Spitzer, Herschel) and on the ground (Gemini). This local (z 0.0040705) galaxy is likely an analogue of EoR galaxies, as revealed by its extreme FIR emission line ratio, 88 / 157 ( > 10). The UV spectra reveal extreme 1907, 1909 emission with the strongest equivalent width (EW) = 35.85 0.73 detected so far in the local (z 0) Universe, a relatively strong 1548, 1550 emission with EW = 7.95 0.45 , but no 1640 detection.

Pox 186 was originally discovered in Kunth1981 , and was thought to be a protogalaxy. Corbin2002 later shows that Pox186 is an ultra-compact galaxy still in the process of formation with a majority of star-formation concentrated in the central star cluster of mass 10 ^5 M _ .

gmw_00009426.name: 1ES 0715-259
gmw_00009426.overview: 1ES 0715-259 is a steep-spectrum radio quasar located at a redshift of z=0.465 [arXiv:astro-ph/0306617]. It features a point source surrounded by an elongated host galaxy and exhibits a clear FR II radio morphology [arXiv:astro-ph/0306617].

sources.summary: The paper reclassifies 1ES 0715-259 as a steep-spectrum radio quasar, describing its FR II radio morphology, elongated host galaxy, strong emission lines, and redshift z=0.465.

quotes.quote: 1ES 0715-259 belongs to the Einstein Slew Survey (Elvis et al. 1992) and was classified as a BL Lac Object by Perlman et al. (1996). They assumed that a VLA radio source could be identified with the corresponding Slew Survey source if it fell within 80 '' of the X-ray position. However in this case they reported an offset in the radio-X-ray position of > 200 '' . The VLA radio map of 1ES 0715-259 (Perlman et al. 1996) shows a clear FR II morphology.

The ratio logF _ x /F _ =-5.2 places 1ES 0715-259 near the borderline between radio-selected and X-ray-selected BL Lac objects. There are no polarimetric data for this source. HST (Urry et al. 2000) clearly resolved 1ES0715-259 into a point source surrounded by a small, rather elongated host galaxy: both disk and de Vaucouleurs models can fit the data giving for the host galaxy an apparent magnitude of R=21.1 and R=20.0 respectively. We took an optical spectrum of the counterpart of the radio source. This clearly shows strong emission lines (see figure 1) and the identification, as reported in Table 2, of the [ OII ] , [ NeIII ] , HeII blended with H _ , H _ , [ OIII ] N1 and [ OIII ] N2 yields a redshift <z> =0.465 0.002.

gmw_00009427.name: SDSS J1557
gmw_00009427.overview: SDSS J1557 is a dual-core composite galaxy with a projected core separation of 4.9 kiloparsecs [arXiv:2212.05753]. The system exhibits a velocity offset of 458 km/s between its emission and absorption lines, which is unlikely to be caused by an AGN-driven outflow [arXiv:2212.05753].

sources.summary: The paper presents SDSS J1557 as a dual-core composite galaxy with 4.9 kpc core separation and a significant emission-line velocity offset unlikely to be AGN-driven outflow.

quotes.quote: This paper presents a detection of significant velocity offset between emission and absorption lines for a dual core system in SDSS J155708.82+273518.74 (= SDSS J1557). The photometric image of SDSS J1557 exhibits clear two cores with a projected separation of 2.2 arcseconds (4.9 kpc) determined by GALFIT . Based on the applications of the commonly accepted pPXF code with 636 theoretical SSP templates, the host galaxy contribution can be well determined. Then, the emission line features of SDSS J1557 can be well measured after subtraction of host starlight.

It is found that the velocity offset of emission lines with respect to absorption lines reaches 458 13 . According to the Baldwin-Phillips-Terlevich (BPT) diagram, SDSS J1557 is a composite galaxy. In addition, SDSS J1557 can well fit the M_ BH _ relation of bulges and the galaxy merger would not change this relation. Two reasonable models (say, AGN-driven outflow vs. dual core system) have been discussed to explain this velocity offset. The model of AGN-driven outflow fails to interpret the systematic redshift of emission lines and similar velocity offsets for various emission lines in SDSS J1557.

gmw_00009428.name: SDSS J1054+2733
gmw_00009428.overview: SDSS J1054+2733 is a likely early-type lens galaxy [arXiv:0912.1462]. It acts as a strong gravitational lens with a measured image separation of 1.269 arcseconds and a background source redshift of 1.452 [arXiv:0912.1462].

sources.summary: The paper describes SDSS J1054+2733 as a likely early-type lens galaxy fit in I band, with R-band modeling tied to that profile and measured image separation and source redshift.

quotes.quote: SDSS J1054+2733. The best-fit S ersic index to the galaxy is 3.7 in the I band, and the lens galaxy is likely to be early-type. For the R band analysis, we fixed the galaxy profile to that from the I band fit. Although there remain some residuals in the 2PSFs model of the V band image, we could not fit the residuals well because of a lack of bright enough PSF templates to fit the weak residuals. The image separation is =1 269 0 004 and the source redshift is z_s=1.452 0.002 .

gmw_00009429.name: SDSS J1055+4628
gmw_00009429.overview: SDSS J1055+4628 is a radio-quiet quasar located at a redshift of approximately 1.25 [arXiv:1811.07932, arXiv:0912.1462]. The system is gravitationally lensed by a foreground galaxy, producing two lensed images with a separation of 1.15 arcseconds and a low-frequency radio luminosity consistent with star formation [arXiv:1811.07932, arXiv:0912.1462].

sources.summary: The paper detects lensed radio-quiet quasar SDSS J1055+4628 with LoTSS and interprets its low-frequency radio luminosity as consistent with star formation.

quotes.quote: SDSS J1055+4628 (SDSS J105545.45+462839.4) was discovered in the Sloan Digital Sky Survey (SDSS) Quasar Lens Search (SQLS) by . The quasar is at z_s=1.25 and is gravitationally lensed by a foreground galaxy at z_l=0.39 . The separation between the two lensed images is 1.15 . This system was undetected in Herschel /SPIRE, implying a limit on the lensing-corrected SFR of <44 M_ \ yr^ 1 , assuming the median fitted dust temperature of the parent sample and a magnification factor of 10.

Here, we present the detection of the gravitationally-lensed radio-quiet quasars SDSS J1055+4628, SDSS J1313+5151 and SBS 1520+530 at 144 MHz that fall in the HETDEX Spring Field targeted in the LOFAR Two-metre Sky Survey (LoTSS) first full data release. We compare their radio and far-infrared luminosities relative to the radio--infrared correlation and find that their radio luminosities can be explained by star formation. The implied star formation rates derived from their radio and infrared luminosities are between 20 and 300 M _ yr ^ 1 .

sources.summary: The paper reports SDSS J1055+4628 as a lens system with two quasars, a Sersic-fit lens galaxy, nearby extended objects, image separation, source redshift, and SQLS statistical-sample inclusion.

quotes.quote: SDSS J1055+4628. The FOCAS R band image was used for astrometric measurements because it is the deepest. In addition to the two quasars (A and B) and the lens galaxy (G1 with a best-fit S ersic index of 5.4), we also found two extended objects located 3 5 to the north and 4'' to the south of the system. The northern object has R-I 1.0 , which is similar to the lens galaxy G1 ( R-I=0.87 ), but the southern one is much bluer than the two galaxies. In the V band image we could not find any galaxy components. The image separation is =1 146 0 004 and the source redshift is z_s=1.249 0.001 .

gmw_00009430.name: SDSS J1131+1915
gmw_00009430.overview: SDSS J1131+1915 is a strong gravitational lensing system with a source redshift of 2.915 [arXiv:0912.1462]. The lens galaxy has been successfully fit with a Sersic profile in the I band, and the system exhibits an image separation of 1.462 arcseconds [arXiv:0912.1462].

sources.summary: The paper says SDSS J1131+1915 has an I-band Sersic-fit lens galaxy, faint V-band residuals, no R-band imaging, measured image separation, and a source redshift outside the SQLS selection criteria.

quotes.quote: SDSS J1131+1915. The lens galaxy was successfully fit in the I band image with the S ersic profile (the best-fit index is 2.4), but the V band residuals after subtracting two PSFs are faint and we could not estimate the parameters of the galaxy. We did not take R band images. The image separation is =1 462 0 007 and the source redshift is z_s=2.915 0.001 . This system is not included in the SQLS statistical sample because the redshift is outside of our selection criteria ( z_s<2.2 ).

gmw_00009431.name: SDSS J1349+1227
gmw_00009431.overview: SDSS J1349+1227 is a gravitational lens system with a source redshift of 1.722 [arXiv:0912.1462]. It contains a faint lens galaxy located very close to the fainter quasar component, exhibiting an image separation of 3.002 arcseconds [arXiv:0912.1462].

sources.summary: The paper reinterprets SDSS J1349+1227 as a gravitational lens, detecting a faint lens galaxy near the fainter quasar and reporting its Sersic fit, separation, redshift, and sample exclusion.

quotes.quote: SDSS J1349+1227. Although this system had been reported as a binary quasar in hennawi06 , our new observations suggest that it is a gravitational lens rather than a binary quasar. We detected a lens galaxy very close to the fainter quasar component with a best-fit S ersic index of 1.7. Because the galaxy is faint in the R and V bands, we fixed the galaxy parameters to the best-fit values from the I band. The image separation is =3 002 0 004 and the source redshift is z_s=1.722 0.002 .

gmw_00009432.name: SDSS J1455+1447
gmw_00009432.overview: SDSS J1455+1447 is a gravitationally lensed system with a source redshift of 1.424 [arXiv:0912.1462]. The system has an image separation of 1.727 arcseconds and features a lens galaxy with a Sersic index of 3.5 [arXiv:0912.1462].

sources.summary: The paper fits SDSS J1455+1447 with two PSFs and a lens galaxy, fixes the R and V profiles to the I-band fit, measures its separation and redshift, and includes it in SQLS DR7.

quotes.quote: SDSS J1455+1447. We fit the I band image with two PSFs and a galaxy, and the best-fit S ersic index was 3.5. As in the case of SDSS J1349+1227, we fixed the galaxy profile in R and V to the best-fit values from the I band. The image separation is =1 727 0 004 and the source redshift is z_s=1.424 0.001 . This system will be included in the SQLS DR7 statistical sample (N. Inada et al. 2010, in preparation).

gmw_00009433.name: SDSS J1620+1203
gmw_00009433.overview: SDSS J1620+1203 is a strong gravitational lensing system with a source redshift of 1.158 [arXiv:0912.1462]. The bright lens galaxy, which has a redshift of 0.398, is located close to the faint quasar and exhibits a Sersic index of 6.62 [arXiv:0912.1462].

sources.summary: The paper describes SDSS J1620+1203 as a lens with a bright galaxy near the faint quasar, spectral absorption lines giving the lens redshift, and measured separation, source redshift, and Sersic index.

quotes.quote: SDSS J1620+1203. The lens galaxy is bright and close to the faint quasar, and the spectral features of the galaxy are seen in the spectrum of the faint quasar. In fact, the fainter quasar image was classified as a galaxy in the SDSS due to the bright lens galaxy. Ca 2 H K , Mg, and Na galactic absorption lines are observed, giving a lens galaxy redshift of z_l=0.398 0.001 . The image separation is =2 765 0 011 and the source redshift is z_s=1.158 0.002 . The best-fit S ersic index of 6.62 2.53 is larger than is expected blanton05 , but we do not take it seriously because of the large error.

gmw_00009434.name: SDSS J102906.69+555625.2
gmw_00009434.overview: SDSS J102906.69+555625.2 is an active galactic nucleus at a redshift of z = 0.451 [arXiv:2312.02326]. The source has been detected in single-dish radio observations at 15 and 37 GHz, but it remains undetected by radio interferometry [arXiv:2312.02326].

sources.summary: The paper reports that J1029+5556 is detected at 37 and 15 GHz but not by radio interferometry, has the highest sample redshift, lacks morphology information, and may have changed activity.

quotes.quote: SDSS J102906.69+555625.2 So far J1029+5556 has been detected at 37 GHz at MRO and at 15 GHz at OVRO (Table tab:j1029singledish ). It has not been detected in any radio interferometric observations (see Table tab:jterf ). J1029+5556 has the highest redshift, z = 0.451, in this sample, and due to this it is also the only source that is missing the host galaxy morphology information. Interestingly, it was detected at MRO only three times in 2016-2017, with moderate flux densities around 500 mJy and below, and has not been detected after that, though it has not been observed very frequently in the past few years.

gmw_00009435.name: SDSS J150916.18+613716.7
gmw_00009435.overview: SDSS J150916.18+613716.7 is an active galactic nucleus that exhibits extreme radio variability of four orders of magnitude [arXiv:2312.02326]. The source is generally undetected in radio bands, including LOFAR and JVLA, and lacks detectable radio emission from star formation [arXiv:2312.02326].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J1509+6137 as highly variable at 37 GHz, absent in JVLA and LOFAR detections except for flares, and lacking detectable radio emission from star formation.

quotes.quote: SDSS J150916.18+613716.7 J1509+6137 is an intriguing source as it has clearly the highest detection percentage at 37 GHz -- 25.3 per cent -- but it has not been detected in any JVLA band. The MRO detections have an average luminosity of L_ = 2.5 10 ^ 43 erg s ^ 1 . The light curves are shown in Figs. j1509lc and fig:J1509lc-ul , and the radio data are given in Tables tab:jterf and tab:j1509singledish . The brightest MRO flares exceed 1 Jy, indicating extreme variability of four orders of magnitude.

J1509+6137 was not detected by LOFAR, but based on the LOFAR LoTSS DR2 there are two other radio sources within the MRO beam. Neither of these sources have optical counterparts, and both of them are faint, around 0.4 and 0.8 mJy. They are not seen in the JVLA data. J1509+6137 seems to be totally absent in radio -- except during the 37 GHz flares -- and does not even show detectable amounts of radio emission from star formation.

gmw_00009436.name: SDSS J151020.06+554722.0
gmw_00009436.overview: SDSS J151020.06+554722.0 is a galaxy with a high detection rate at 37 GHz [arXiv:2312.02326]. Its radio spectrum below 10 GHz is consistent with star-forming galaxies, and it appears marginally resolved in LOFAR observations [arXiv:2312.02326].

sources.summary: The paper says J1510+5547 had frequent 37 GHz detections, earlier low-band JVLA detections but recent higher-band non-detections, LOFAR marginal resolution, and a low-frequency spectrum consistent with star-forming galaxies.

quotes.quote: SDSS J151020.06+554722.0 J1510+5547 has a high detection percentage of 17.6 per cent at 37 GHz (Table tab:j1510singledish and Figs. j1510lc and fig:J1510lc-ul ). It was last detected in 2019 even if the number of annual observations has stayed roughly the same. The mean luminosity at 37 GHz is L_ = 8.7 10 ^ 42 erg s ^ 1 . It was detected in L, C, and X bands in our previous JVLA observations, but remained a non-detection in all bands, X through Q, in the recent observations (Table tab:jterf ).

The radio spectrum of J1510+5547 is shown in Fig. j1510spect . The X band upper limit is very close to the earlier X band detection flux density, and considering that the central frequencies of the two observations differ by 1 GHz, it is likely that the recent non-detection is due to the source being very close to the detection limit. This source is also detected by LOFAR and seems to be marginally resolved. The radio spectrum below 10 GHz is consistent with that of star forming galaxies with the characteristic spectral turnover seen toward lower frequencies.

gmw_00009437.name: No.18
gmw_00009437.overview: No.18 is a unique Lyman-alpha blob that features extremely extended Lyman-alpha nebulae with a very large equivalent width [arXiv:1209.1253]. The galaxy contains two bright plume structures and two lower surface brightness tails, which resemble tidal tails and suggest an ongoing merger process [arXiv:1209.1253].

sources.summary: The paper identifies No.18 as a uniquely extended Ly blob with extremely large equivalent width, bright Ly plume structures, and tidal-tail-like features suggesting an ongoing merger.

quotes.quote: 53W002 , which is originally found as a weak radio source W91 , has the smallest isophotal area and major-axis among the four LABs. p t No.18 lies in the northwest direction from 53W002, and it is the most unique object with several very interesting features. First it is notable that No.18 has an extremely large EW_0 and very extended Ly nebulae in spite of a substantial amount of dust (see later description). We show the pseudo-color image of No.18 ( NB413 for green, B for blue and red) as well as their Ly surface brightness in Figure fig8 . It clearly has two bright structures in Ly ( plume1 and plume2 ). The unique shape of plume1 appears like a bow-shock front with a jet expelled from the core.
No.18 also has two tails with lower surface brightness ( tail1 and tail2 ). They resemble tidal tails, which may indicate that No.18 is undergoing a merger process.

gmw_00009438.name: No.19
gmw_00009438.overview: No.19 is a variable quasar located at a redshift of z = 2.397 [arXiv:1209.1253]. It features faint, clumpy Lyman-alpha tails spanning 88.5 kiloparsecs and is believed to be interacting with the nearby galaxy No.18 [arXiv:1209.1253].

sources.summary: No.19 is described as a variable QSO near No.18, with newly detected faint clumpy Ly tails, a large physical extent, AGN dominance, and possible interaction with No.18.

quotes.quote: No.19, which lies close by No.18, was found as a variable QSO and spectroscopically confirmed to be at z = 2.397 P96a . In the Ly image it shows faint and clumpy tails that is detected for the first time. Including the tails we estimated the size as 88.5 kpc in physical scale, which is relatively large among the LABs in other fields. Unlike No.18, however, we consider that No.19 is physically dominated by AGN because of its small EW_0 .

Especially for No.18 and No.19, we consider that galaxy - galaxy mergers may have triggered their AGN activity and induced their morphological features like tail or jet because the merging mechanism becomes effective in outer regions of clusters of galaxies at least in the local universe treu03 . The HST images of No.18, which shows the double component having different colors and Ly brightness in the core unresolved in our images, strengthen the merging hypothesis. No.19, which lies at the distance of only 80 kpc (physical) from No.18, can be considered as to interact with No.18.

gmw_00009439.name: NGC 528
gmw_00009439.overview: NGC 528 is a massive, inclined lenticular galaxy with a stellar mass of approximately 1.5 × 10^{11} solar masses [arXiv:1612.01957]. Spectral decomposition reveals a symmetric, rotating bulge and a well-behaved rotating disc with a rotation speed exceeding 300 km/s [arXiv:1612.01957].

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC 528 as the detailed test case for spectral bulge-disc decomposition, finding a massive inclined S0 whose spectra separate into coherent rotating disc and bulge components.

quotes.quote: In order to fully determine the strengths and limitations of the method, we have chosen to explore the decomposition of one galaxy, NGC 528, in particular detail. At an inclination of 70 degrees, it clearly displays both bulge and disc, while still ensuring that enough of any rotational motion is projected into the line of sight to be detected. It was also chosen for its brightness: it has a K-band absolute magnitude of M_K 24.36 Skrutskie2006 ; using eq.(2) of Cappellari2013 this luminosity can be converted into a large stellar mass of M_ 1.5 10^ 11 M_ , ensuring that any kinematic structure will be well resolved with the CALIFA spectral resolution.

It is apparent from this figure that this process decomposes the galaxy into remarkably coherent individual components, with a well-behaved symmetrically-rotating disc that has a low dispersion at all radii, and a symmetric high-dispersion rotating bulge with a dispersion profile that decreases in an orderly manner with radius. Note that the symmetric appearance of the kinematics is in no way imposed on the results: each spatial bin is decomposed independently, so the coherence of the map is a direct indicator of the consistency and reproducibility of the results, even down to the level where the component is only a 30 contributor to the total light. The derived velocity dispersion of the bulge is high, but quite consistent with the central dispersion of 264 km s ^ 1 found by Scodeggio1998 , and the expected properties of such a massive galaxy (see Section sec:data ). The mass of the galaxy is also reflected in the rotation speed of the disc, which, after correcting for inclination, implies a value of a little over 300 km s ^ 1 .

gmw_00009440.name: NGC 7671
gmw_00009440.overview: NGC 7671 is a lenticular galaxy characterized by a low-Sersic-index bulge [arXiv:1612.01957]. Structural and kinematic decompositions of the galaxy reveal two coherent components consisting of a cold rotating disc and a hot rotating bulge [arXiv:1612.01957].

sources.summary: The paper analyzes NGC 7671 with NGC 6427 as CALIFA S0 test cases, finding low-Sersic-index bulges and coherent decompositions into cold rotating discs and hot rotating bulges.

quotes.quote: NGC 7671 and NGC 6427 Two other galaxies in this initial test, NGC 7671 and NGC 6427, were analysed in the same way. The galaxy magnitudes, masses and inclinations are shown in Table tab:galpar and the parameters of the GALFIT photometric decomposition are shown in Table tab:GALFIT2 . Similar to NGC 528, both galaxies also possess low-S e rsic-index bulges, allowing us to gain further insight into these systems.

Figure fig:kin7671 and Figure fig:kin6427 show the resulting decomposed kinematic components. Again, the decompositions result in two very coherent components which match the photometric decomposition very well, and, as for NGC 528, both show cold rotating discs and hot rotating bulges. Comparing Figures fig:bulger , fig:kin7671 and fig:kin6427 there is a clear similarity in the kinematic components of these three galaxies as well as the photometric components.

gmw_00009441.name: NGC 6427
gmw_00009441.overview: NGC 6427 is a lenticular galaxy [arXiv:1612.01957]. Photometric and kinematic analyses reveal that the galaxy possesses a low-Sersic-index bulge, structured into a cold rotating disc and a hot rotating bulge [arXiv:1612.01957].

sources.summary: The paper analyzes NGC 6427 with NGC 7671 as CALIFA S0 test cases, finding low-Sersic-index bulges and coherent decompositions into cold rotating discs and hot rotating bulges.

quotes.quote: NGC 7671 and NGC 6427 Two other galaxies in this initial test, NGC 7671 and NGC 6427, were analysed in the same way. The galaxy magnitudes, masses and inclinations are shown in Table tab:galpar and the parameters of the GALFIT photometric decomposition are shown in Table tab:GALFIT2 . Similar to NGC 528, both galaxies also possess low-S e rsic-index bulges, allowing us to gain further insight into these systems.

Figure fig:kin7671 and Figure fig:kin6427 show the resulting decomposed kinematic components. Again, the decompositions result in two very coherent components which match the photometric decomposition very well, and, as for NGC 528, both show cold rotating discs and hot rotating bulges. Comparing Figures fig:bulger , fig:kin7671 and fig:kin6427 there is a clear similarity in the kinematic components of these three galaxies as well as the photometric components.

gmw_00009442.name: Draco dwarf spheroidal galaxy
gmw_00009442.overview: The Draco dwarf spheroidal galaxy has a flattened structure with a nearly constant ellipticity of 0.29 [arXiv:astro-ph/0108100]. Deep multicolor photometry shows no evidence of tidally induced tails or an unbound stellar halo [arXiv:astro-ph/0108100].

sources.summary: The paper maps Draco with SDSS photometry, finding a larger limiting radius, flattened regular structure, and no clear tidal tails or extratidal stellar halo at the survey depth.

quotes.quote: We have investigated the spatial extent and structure of the Draco dwarf spheroidal galaxy using deep wide-field multicolor CCD photometry from the Sloan Digital Sky Survey (SDSS). Our study covers an area of 27 square degrees around the center of the Draco dwarf and reaches two magnitudes below the level of Draco's horizontal branch. The SDSS photometry allows very effective filtering in color-magnitude space. With such filtering the density of the foreground of Galactic field stars is decreased by more than an order of magnitude and the stellar population of the Draco dwarf galaxy thus stands out with much higher contrast than in former investigations.

We show that the spatial distribution of Draco's red giants, red horizontal branch stars and subgiants down to i^* =21.7 mag does not provide evidence for the existence of tidally induced tails or a halo of unbound stars. The projected surface density of the dwarf galaxy is flattened with a nearly constant ellipticity of 0.29 0.02 at position angle 88 3^ . The radial profile can be fit by King models as well as by a generalized exponential. Using the empirical King (1962) profile the core radius and the limiting (or tidal) radius along the major axis are r_c = 7 7 and r_t = 40 1 , respectively; the latter means that the size of the Draco dwarf galaxy is 40 larger than previously measured.

gmw_00009443.name: MCG+11-11-029
gmw_00009443.overview: MCG+11-11-029 is an edge-on galaxy located at a redshift of z = 0.0370 [arXiv:2404.16691]. It is associated with a radio source and is suspected to host an absorbed active galactic nucleus that is hidden behind interstellar matter [arXiv:2404.16691].

sources.summary: The survey identifies MCG+11-11-029 as an edge-on galaxy in an INTEGRAL error circle, with radio emission and IR colors suggesting a possible absorbed AGN.

quotes.quote: In the position error circle of this source the \ instrument distinguishes the edge-on galaxy MCG+11-11-029 at redshift z = 0.0370 . The radio source ILT J085033.04+662916.2 from the LOFAR survey lofar can be associated with it. The infrared (IR) color of the galaxy from the ALLWISE catalog allwise , W1-W2 0.2 , is more typical for ordinary galaxies than for AGNs. Nevertheless, it can be assumed that we are dealing with an absorbed AGN, since its nucleus may be hidden from the observer behind a thick layer of interstellar matter due to the orientation of the galaxy in the sky.

gmw_00009444.name: UGC 6473
gmw_00009444.overview: UGC 6473 is a nearby galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.0214 [arXiv:2404.16691]. It is classified as an active galactic nucleus candidate and exhibits associated radio emission, despite having the infrared colors of an ordinary galaxy [arXiv:2404.16691].

sources.summary: The survey marks UGC 6473 as a galaxy in an INTEGRAL error circle, detects associated radio emission, and classifies it as an AGN candidate despite ordinary IR color.

quotes.quote: The galaxy UGC 6473 ( z=0.0214 ) is distinguished in the \ position error circle. Radio emission (NVSS J112857+730202) is detected from it. Its IR color W1-W2 0.1 corresponds to ordinary galaxies but does not rule out the presence of an active nucleus, given the relative proximity and low luminosity of the object. Therefore, we assign this object to the AGN candidates.

gmw_00009445.name: SDSS J1442+4055
gmw_00009445.overview: SDSS J1442+4055 is a gravitationally lensed quasar that is doubly imaged [arXiv:1903.06505]. Its main lensing galaxy, G1, is an early-type galaxy located at a redshift of 0.284 [arXiv:1903.06505].

sources.summary: The paper follows up the gravitationally lensed quasar SDSS J1442+4055, measuring a time delay, microlensing limits, spectroscopic properties, and constraints for lens modeling.

quotes.quote: de Los Castros s/n, 39005 Santander, Spain We present an -band photometric monitoring of the two images A and B of the gravitationally lensed quasar SDSS J1442+4055 using the Liverpool Telescope (LT). From the LT light curves between 2015 December and 2018 August, we derive at once a time delay of 25.0 1.5 days (1 confidence interval; A is leading) and microlensing magnification gradients below 10 ^ 4 mag day ^ 1 . The delay interval is not expected to be affected by an appreciable microlensing--induced bias, so it can be used to estimate cosmological parameters. This paper also focuses on new Gran Telescopio Canarias (GTC) and LT spectroscopic observations of the lens system.
Current observational constraints also allow us to explore simple mass models for SDSS J1442+4055 , and thus, compare the predicted delays with the measured one.

sources.summary: The paper identifies G1 as the main lensing galaxy near the brightest quasar image, extracts its spectrum, measures z = 0.284, and finds it does not drive the observed extinction.

quotes.quote: We determine the redshift of two bright galaxies around the doubly imaged quasar using LT spectroscopy, while GTC data lead to low--noise individual spectra of A, B, and the main lensing galaxy G1. The G1 spectral shape is accurately matched to an early--type galaxy template at z = 0.284, and it has potential for further relevant studies.

This paper mainly reports on optical follow--up observations of the gravitationally lensed quasar SDSS J1442+4055 , using the GTC and the LT. The main lensing galaxy G1 is only 1 38 from the brightest quasar image A, and its GTC spectra clearly show the Ca ii HK , G band, H , and Mg i b absorption features at z_ G1 = 0.284 0.001. The new spectra of G1 with unprecedented quality might be used (together with IR spectroscopy) to fit stellar population models in the non--local early--type galaxy e.g., bruzual03 .

gmw_00009446.name: G2
gmw_00009446.overview: G2 is a spiral galaxy with a light distribution characterized by a disk and spiral arms [arXiv:1903.06505]. It acts as a secondary lensing galaxy in the field of the quasar SDSS J1442+4055, located at a redshift of approximately 0.22 [arXiv:1903.06505].

sources.summary: The paper treats G2 as a secondary lensing galaxy near G1, models it with a spiral light distribution, measures its spectroscopic redshift, and rules out physical association with G1.

quotes.quote: The lens system basically consists of two quasar images (A and B) having 18 19 mag and separated by 2 156, the main lensing galaxy (G1; 19.5 mag) located 1 38 from the A image, and two additional intervening objects: a secondary galaxy (G2; 19.8 mag) in the vicinity of G1 and an absorber at z = 1.946 2016MNRAS.456.1948S . This paper is dedicated to describe and deeply analyse follow--up observations of SDSS J1442+4055 . In the framework of the Gravitational LENses and DArk MAtter (GLENDAMA) project 2018A&A...616A.118G , in Section sec:data , we present a 2.7--year photometric monitoring with the 2.0 m Liverpool Telescope (LT) in the band and associated light curves for both quasar images, as well as spectroscopic observations with the 10.4 m Gran Telescopio Canarias (GTC) and the LT. The high signal--to--noise ratio (SNR) spectroscopy over a wide wavelength interval with the GTC allows us to accurately identify the primary lensing galaxy G1, while we use the LT spectroscopic data of two bright secondary galaxies (G2 and another object in the field around the quasar) to measure their redshifts.

From this tool, assuming reasonable brightness profiles for the lensing galaxies G1 and G2, one can determine the fluxes of A and B through point--spread function (PSF) fitting. The brightness of the main deflector G1 was modelled as a de Vaucouleurs profile 2016MNRAS.456.1595M , whereas we taken an exponential profile to model the light distribution of the secondary lens G2, since the Hubble Space Telescope ( HST ) archive includes an image of G2 showing the presence of a disc and spiral arms Program Id: 14127, PI: Michele Fumagalli (see the left panel of Figure  fig:f1 ).

Despite G2 is a spiral galaxy (see Section subsec:ioo ), we do not detect any emission line in its visible spectrum. The original SDSS fluxes of G2 and G3 were reduced by 50 to roughly account for slit losses, since the slit width does not cover their entire luminous halo. Although the LT--SPRAT spectra are quite noisy, their shapes indicated that the two secondary galaxies are at similar redshift z_ G2 = z_ G3 = 0.22 0.01. Hence, the photometric redshift of G2 does not correspond to the true value of z_ G2 , and this galaxy is not physically associated with the main deflector.

gmw_00009447.name: G3
gmw_00009447.overview: G3 is a bright field galaxy located near the quasar SDSS J1442+4055 [arXiv:1903.06505]. Spectroscopic observations of the galaxy indicate that it is at a redshift of approximately 0.22 [arXiv:1903.06505].

sources.summary: The paper observes G3 as a bright field galaxy near SDSS J1442+4055, gives its SDSS position, notes its photometric redshift and brightness, and measures z = 0.22.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: The SDSS position of G3 is RA (J2000) = 220 73908 and Dec (J2000) = +40 92188

quotes.quote: To identify G2 and another bright field galaxy (G3), both objects were spectroscopically observed on 2016 June 8. The SDSS position of G3 is RA (J2000) = 220 73908 and Dec (J2000) = +40 92188 (southeast of the quasar images), and thus, A and G3 are separated by 33 9. Moreover, G3 is brighter than G2 ( = 19.1) and has a photometric redshift of 0.188 0.029.

After a primary reduction of frames under the IRAF working environment, the spectra of G2 and G3 were extracted using the task APALL. These spectroscopic data are included in Table tab:t4 and Figure  fig:f4 . In Figure  fig:f4 , we show the two spectra (green and cyan) and two red--shifted SDSS templates of an early--type galaxy (magenta; see Section subsec:osiris ), along with gri fluxes for both galaxies from the SDSS database (circles). Despite G2 is a spiral galaxy (see Section subsec:ioo ), we do not detect any emission line in its visible spectrum. The original SDSS fluxes of G2 and G3 were reduced by 50 to roughly account for slit losses, since the slit width does not cover their entire luminous halo. Although the LT--SPRAT spectra are quite noisy, their shapes indicated that the two secondary galaxies are at similar redshift z_ G2 = z_ G3 = 0.22 0.01.

gmw_00009448.name: 3C 381
gmw_00009448.overview: The active galaxy 3C 381 has historically been classified as a broad-line radio galaxy [arXiv:0904.1323]. However, more recent spectroscopic observations and a high absorbing X-ray column suggest it is instead a narrow-line radio galaxy [arXiv:0904.1323].

sources.summary: The paper argues that 3C 381 is probably not a true BLRG, citing tenuous classification evidence, NLRG spectroscopy, and a high absorbing X-ray column.

quotes.quote: 3C 381 is classed as a BLRG in LRL, but as pointed out by Hardcastle \ (1997) the evidence for this classification is very tenuous. Jackson Rawlings (1997) classify it as a NLRG, based on the spectroscopy of Saunders \ (1989), whose published spectrum shows no evidence for broad H . Our X-ray spectrum gives a well-constrained, high absorbing column. We infer that the classification of this source as a BLRG by LRL is likely to have been incorrect. For consistency, we retain its BLRG classification in the plots and tables in the present paper.

gmw_00009449.name: PKS 0805-07
gmw_00009449.overview: PKS 0805-07 is a high-redshift quasi-periodic blazar candidate [arXiv:1707.05829] that exhibits continuous gamma-ray variability [arXiv:1511.02654]. The quasar hosts a relativistic jet with feature speeds reaching up to 50c [arXiv:1603.03882], which may indicate a binary supermassive black hole system [arXiv:1707.05829].

sources.summary: The paper identifies PKS 0805-07 as a new high-redshift quasi-periodic blazar candidate, measuring a 676-day GLS power peak with 4.7 sigma white-noise significance.

quotes.quote: The four other blazars, 4C +01.28, S5 0716+71, PKS 0805-07, and PKS 2052-47, are new candidates to quasi-periodic blazars. Three of the four candidates are located at high redshifts of z=0.89 (4C +01.28), 1.84 (PKS 0805-07), and 1.49 (PKS 2052-47), and are much more distant than the previously reported quasi-periodic blazars. Taking that binary SMBH systems are expected to be more frequent at high redshifts into account, these distant blazars are potential candidates to binary systems of SMBHs.

We computed the GLS periodograms of these sources and found that their power peaks are at 442 days for 4C +01.28, at 340 days for S5 0716+71, at 676 days for PKS 0805-07, and at 642 days for PKS 2052-47.

Three of these four power peaks are significant at >5 confidence level under a condition of white noise, while the power peak of PKS 0805-07 is significant at 4.7 (corresponding to the chance probability of 3 10^ 4 ) under this condition.

sources.summary: The paper finds 0805-077 continuously variable in gamma rays, with a major 2013.5 activity period whose 95 GHz correlation lags gamma rays by 70 days.

quotes.quote: 0805-077 : The activity of this source in rays is characterized by continuous variability with one major activity period around 2013.5. A high-intensity outburst around 2009.5 with strong variability throughout that year was observed in Paper I. Although the 2013.5 activity is not the brightest feature observed in the rays, it yields a significant correlation with the 95 GHz light curve with the 95 GHz lagging the ray variations by 70 d. Despite the different time window in Paper I, the time lag of 120 d with the 37 GHz emission lagging the rays seems to be in agreement with this result, given the higher observing frequency.

sources.summary: The paper identifies PKS 0805 07 as the survey's fastest quasar jet, with feature speeds near 40-50c and one accelerating component decelerating from 50c to 20c.

quotes.quote: The fastest speed in our survey is 50c , measured in the jet of the quasar PKS 0805 07, and is indicative of a maximum jet Lorentz factor of 50 in the parent population.
The fastest overall jet speed we have measured in our survey to date is feature id = 2 in the quasar PKS 0805 07, with = 503 62 , corresponding to 40c 5c .

These are feature id = 3 in PKS 0805 07, which had an initial speed of 50c in 1996 and subsequently decelerated to 20c , and feature id = 16 in PKS 1510 08, which attained a maximum speed of 42c before fading in 2011.

gmw_00009450.name: HE 0005 2355
gmw_00009450.overview: HE 0005 2355 is a radio-loud quasar [arXiv:astro-ph/0405279] classified as a Population B source [arXiv:astro-ph/0405279]. It has a redshift of approximately 1.41 [arXiv:astro-ph/0405279] and exhibits a general absence of large CIV blueshifts [arXiv:astro-ph/0405279].

sources.summary: The paper classifies HE 0005 2355 as a Population B radio-loud quasar, reporting consistent redshift measurements and no large CIV blueshift.

quotes.quote: Profile shift was measured relative to rest frame measures derived from HE 0005 2355 : We call this a Population B RL source. Espey et al. \ ( ) report z 1.411 consistent with the general absence of large \ blueshifts in Population B sources (we obtain z 1.412 ; Espey et al. \ report z 1.407 for \ with FWHM( ) 5900 ). The more recent HE measure gives z 1.405 but with CIV at the noisy blue edge of the spectrum.

gmw_00009451.name: HE 0122 3759
gmw_00009451.overview: HE 0122 3759 is a Population A radio-quiet quasar [arXiv:astro-ph/0405279]. High-excitation spectroscopy of the source confirms a large CIV blueshift, which is an amplitude characteristic of extreme Population A sources [arXiv:astro-ph/0405279].

sources.summary: The paper classifies HE 0122 3759 as a Population A radio-quiet quasar and highlights a large CIV blueshift confirmed by HE spectroscopy.

quotes.quote: HE 0122 3759 : Population A RQ source. Comparison of \ redshifts (2.173: Carswell et al. \ ; 2.178: Espey et al. \ ) with our \ value (2.200) suggest a large blueshift. Espey et al. \ ( ) derive z=2.207 from \ and z 2.199 from . An HE spectrum (CIV z 2.164 ) confirms the large blueshift C 3400 \ with an amplitude seen only in extreme Population A (i.e. NLSy1) sources.

gmw_00009452.name: HE 0205 3756
gmw_00009452.overview: HE 0205 3756 is a radio-quiet quasar [arXiv:astro-ph/0405279] classified as a Population A source [arXiv:astro-ph/0405279]. It exhibits a large carbon IV emission line blueshift relative to its rest frame [arXiv:astro-ph/0405279].

sources.summary: The paper classifies HE 0205 3756 as a Population A radio-quiet quasar, noting a large CIV blueshift but ambiguous line properties affected by sky residuals.

quotes.quote: HE 0205 3756 : Population A RQ source. A published \ measure yields z 2.395 (Ulrich ). This implies a large blueshift C 3000 \ relative to our rest frame measure z=2.437 (2.412 in Wilkes ). The low measured W( ) (Ulrich ) is also consistent with a Population A source (S00b).
Taken at face value, EW and profile shape for \ appear characteristic of Population B. However the \ red shelf may be a spurious feature caused by residuals from the very strong sky lines.

gmw_00009453.name: host galaxy of GRB 220427A
gmw_00009453.overview: The host galaxy of GRB 220427A is a distant galaxy observed in multiple photometric bands [arXiv:2310.17287]. Spectral energy distribution fitting of these observations indicates that the galaxy has a photometric redshift of approximately 0.82 [arXiv:2310.17287].

sources.summary: The paper follows up the host galaxy of GRB 220427A with GROND photometry, derives a photometric redshift of z=0.82 0.09, and fits its SED with LePhare and CIGALE.

quotes.quote: The host galaxy of GRB 220427A was observed using GROND at the MPG 2.2 m telescope at ESO's La Silla observatory Greiner2008 on 27 May 2022 in 7 bands ( grizJHK _S ). The observation included integration of 3600s in each of the bands. We corrected the resulting photometry of galactic extinction using the Schlegel1998 and the Schlafly2011 corrections.

The spectral energy distribution was used to determine a photometric redshift by fitting it with galaxy models at varying redshifts using LePhare Arnouts2011 . The best fit was obtained for a redshift of z=0.82 0.09 (see figure fig:host220427A ). Using this redshift, we performed a further SED fit with CIGALE Boquien2019 , which delivered similar host galaxy properties to the ones obtained with LePhare. The results obtained with CIGALE are given in Table Table:host220427A . Input parameters for the SED fitting with CIGALE are presented in Table Table:paramshost220427A .

gmw_00009454.name: VCC 781
gmw_00009454.overview: VCC 781 is a dwarf galaxy with a robust far-infrared dust detection [arXiv:1005.3060]. Based on observations across multiple wavelengths, the galaxy has an estimated dust mass of 1.85 x 10^5 solar masses [arXiv:1005.3060].

sources.summary: The paper reports a robust far-infrared dust detection in VCC 781, rejects likely background contamination, and derives a dust mass of 1.85 x 10^5 solar masses.

quotes.quote: On the other hand, VCC 781 and VCC 951 are detected unambiguously in the SPIRE 250 m image at 9.8 and 11.1 , respectively (see Fig. ). Adopting a fair 2 detection cutoff in the other filters, VCC 781 is also detected in the PACS 100 m, PACS 160 m, SPIRE 350 m, and SPIRE 500 m bands.

For VCC 781 , this identifies four SDSS sources within this PSF region that may be contaminating the dwarf emission. The superior resolution in the PACS 160 m image discards three of these optically identified objects, while the remaining source is rejected as a possible background candidate for the IR emission, based on the 24 m image (data from Fadda et al. in prep.), which has an even higher spatial resolution, and is clearly extended. Hence, the coincidence of the position of detections at various wavelengths makes us confident that the IR emission originates in VCC 781 .
We obtain dust masses of 1.85 10^5 M _ and 1.28 10^5 M _ for VCC 781 and VCC 951 , respectively.

gmw_00009455.name: VCC 951
gmw_00009455.overview: VCC 951 is a dwarf galaxy that has been detected in far-infrared observations [arXiv:1005.3060]. However, unresolved contamination from a neighboring source prevents a definitive conclusion regarding the origin of its detected infrared emission [arXiv:1005.3060].

sources.summary: The paper reports VCC 951 as a far-infrared dwarf-galaxy detection, but notes unresolved neighboring-source contamination that prevents a definite conclusion about its SPIRE emission.

quotes.quote: On the other hand, VCC 781 and VCC 951 are detected unambiguously in the SPIRE 250 m image at 9.8 and 11.1 , respectively (see Fig. ). Adopting a fair 2 detection cutoff in the other filters, VCC 781 is also detected in the PACS 100 m, PACS 160 m, SPIRE 350 m, and SPIRE 500 m bands. VCC 951 has additional detections only in both SPIRE 350 m and 500 m images. Unfortunately, it is impossible to measure fluxes for VCC 951 at 350 m and 500 m because VCC 951 and its neighbouring source, SDSS J122655.56+114000.5, are not resolved at these longer wavelengths.

The SDSS DR7 catalog provides us with 5 detections within the PSF region of VCC 951 , three of which are very likely to be artifacts in the source extraction, since we detected them in neither the SDSS nor the UKIDSS images during our visual inspection. The remaining two, morphologically classified as stellar-like objects, are located at distances of 8'' and 10'', and are also detected in the UKIDSS images. The 24 m image (see Fig. ) shows some evidence of emission from both VCC 951 and one of the two aforementioned sources, which is spatially resolved at these wavelengths. Based on these data, we cannot definitely conclude that the IR emission detected by SPIRE comes from our dwarf galaxy, but deeper optical and NIR observations may help us to address the issue.

gmw_00009456.name: ESO 303-IG 011
gmw_00009456.overview: ESO 303-IG 011 is a close, separated major merger consisting of a pair of star-forming galaxies [arXiv:2404.06539]. The system features extended tidal structures, including a loop of hot gas that connects the two nuclei and is actively forming stars [arXiv:2404.06539].

sources.summary: The paper describes ESO 303-IG 011 as a Hubble-imaged star-forming pair, a close separated major merger with tidal structures and a hot-gas loop connecting its nuclei.

quotes.quote: Furthermore, we explored ESO 303-IG 011, a pair of star-forming galaxies captured by the Hubble Space Telescope and SDSS J084905.51+111447.2, a system of three colliding galaxies revealed by Chandra data. These candidates have some interesting common characteristics: (i) all the candidates are close mergers (UGC 12914/5, UGC 813/6, ESO 303-11, VV 114) or appear to have merged already so that their nuclei lie within a massive bulge in the center of the galaxy (NGC 660, NGC 3256). (ii) The few galaxies that are separated (UGC 12914/5, UGC 813/6, ES0 303-11, VV 114) appear to be major mergers, i.e., their galaxy or bulge masses are similar. (iii) All the seven galaxies have extended tidal tails, bridges or loops of hot, magnetized gas that can be clearly distinguished in the blue (or g) band images.
Also, in the case of ESO 303-11, there is a loop of hot gas connecting the two nuclei, and it also appears to be forming stars.

gmw_00009457.name: 5C6.17
gmw_00009457.overview: 5C6.17 is a large double radio source with a probable but weak radio core [arXiv:astro-ph/0011082]. It features an extended near-infrared counterpart and lies in a fairly empty field with no detected companion galaxies [arXiv:astro-ph/0011082].

sources.summary: The paper describes 5C6.17 as a large double radio source with an extended near-infrared counterpart, red colors, SED evidence for z≈1.05, and no detected optical emission line.

quotes.quote: 5C6.17 This is a large (47 arcsec) double radio source with a probable but weak radio core (detected at the 3 level). The presence of extended lobe emission and the large angular size strongly suggest a redshift z 2 (Blundell, Rawlings Willott 1999). The near-IR counterpart is at the core position (within the uncertainties of the registration of the radio and optical astrometry frames, 1 arcsec). This object is clearly extended in the 1 arcsec seeing J and H images. It lies in a fairly empty field with no companion galaxies detected in any of the images within a 10 arcsec radius.

The SED fitting procedure supports this conclusion with a best-fit redshift of z=1.05 . The SED is fit slightly better by a heavily reddened stellar population and a 0.6 Gyr galaxy reddened by E(B-V)=0.5 provides the best-fit, but no reddening and an age of 4 Gyr is still acceptable. There is a secondary minimum in the ^2 contours at z=5 , but we think this redshift extremely unlikely given the radio structure, the K magnitude and the lack of a Lyman- emission line in the optical spectrum.

gmw_00009458.name: 5C6.62
gmw_00009458.overview: 5C6.62 is a double radio galaxy with a weak core and an angular size of 34 arcsec [arXiv:astro-ph/0011082]. The galaxy has an adopted redshift of z=1.45 based on probable H-band emission, and its near-infrared identification is clearly extended [arXiv:astro-ph/0011082].

sources.summary: The paper treats 5C6.62 as a resolved double radio galaxy with a red optical/near-infrared counterpart, probable H-band emission, and an adopted redshift of z=1.45.

quotes.quote: 5C6.62 This is a double radio source with a weak core and angular size of 34 arcsec (Blundell et al., in prep.). The position of the near-IR/optical identification is coincident with the radio core. There is significant extended emission around the hotspots, one of which contains no compact features. These radio properties are suggestive of z 2 . The radio source identification is clearly extended in the 1 arcsec seeing H and K images and there are hints in the J and K images of an extension/companion to the south-east, although better images would be necessary to confirm this.

This is one of the two sources to have probable emission lines in their near-IR spectra. Taking the line to be H gives a redshift of z=1.45 . Note that 5C6.62 is the brightest object at K band out of those studied in this paper, with K=17.5 .

gmw_00009459.name: 5C6.83
gmw_00009459.overview: 5C6.83 is a double radio source spanning 14 arcseconds with no detected radio core [arXiv:astro-ph/0011082]. Its near-infrared counterpart is slightly extended, extremely red, and has a constrained redshift of z < 2.2 [arXiv:astro-ph/0011082].

sources.summary: The paper identifies 5C6.83 as a double radio source in a crowded field whose near-infrared counterpart is slightly extended, extremely red, and best placed near z≈1.8.

quotes.quote: 5C6.83 This is a 14 arcsec double radio source (Blundell et al., in prep.). No radio core is detected, but the assumed near-IR counterpart lies midway between the two hotspots. The radio source lies in a crowded field with many objects visible in the optical and near-IR images. The brightest object is a star, but the next two brightest objects are both extended and would appear to be low redshift galaxies in a group/cluster. It is not possible to tell if all the fainter objects are resolved or not, but it appears that the radio source counterpart is slightly extended in the near-IR images.

The galaxy is undetected in the R and I images and has a colour R-K>6.2 . From the J to K bands the SED is equivalent to that of a galaxy population beyond the 4000   break. This constrains the redshift to z<2.2 .

gmw_00009460.name: 5C6.242
gmw_00009460.overview: 5C6.242 is a compact double radio source with a weak core [arXiv:astro-ph/0011082]. Its resolved near-infrared counterpart exhibits extremely red colors, with a photometric redshift likely below 2.2 [arXiv:astro-ph/0011082].

sources.summary: The paper describes 5C6.242 as a compact double radio source with a resolved near-infrared counterpart, extremely red colors, and a most-likely photometric redshift near 1.9.

quotes.quote: 5C6.242 This is a small (5 arcsec) double radio source with a weak core. The near-IR counterpart is co-incident with the radio core. The Keck-II K band image with 0.6 arcsec resolution shows the counterpart to be resolved but fairly compact. There is another extremely red galaxy 7 arcsec south-west of the radio source. The bright object just north of the radio source is a star.

This is the reddest of all the objects studied in this paper with R-K>7.3 , although this may be because it has the deepest R band data and several other sources have only lower limits on their colours. The shape of the photometry from J to K is similar to that of a galaxy above the 4000   break, implying a redshift z<2.2 . The flux-density then plummets down to the I and R bands.

gmw_00009461.name: 5C7.47
gmw_00009461.overview: 5C7.47 is a compact radio galaxy located in a crowded field [arXiv:astro-ph/0011082]. It has an adopted redshift of approximately z=1.7, exhibits blue relative colors, and shows no clear emission lines in its optical spectrum [arXiv:astro-ph/0011082].

sources.summary: The paper discusses 5C7.47 as an unusual compact radio galaxy in a crowded field, with blue relative colors, no clear emission lines, and an adopted redshift around z=1.7.

quotes.quote: 5C7.47 is unusual in our sample studied here because it is both the bluest galaxy with R-K = 4.0 and the faintest at K band with a magnitude of 19.5 (3 arcsec aperture photometry used). The optical spectrum (60 minutes integration) shows a faint continuum all the way down to 3900 . Hence we can be sure of a redshift below 3.3 because of the absence of any observed Lyman limit. There are no clear emission lines in the spectrum to a 3 limit of 2 10^ 20 W m ^ 2 .

Adopting this assumption we find that the possible range of redshifts for 5C7.47 is 1.5 z 2.5 . Once again weak Ly emission would be required for 1.8 z 2.5 , which would be highly unusual for a powerful radio galaxy with an observed continuum down to virtually the wavelength of redshifted Ly . Therefore we assign a redshift of z=1.7 to 5C7.47.

gmw_00009462.name: 5C7.208
gmw_00009462.overview: 5C7.208 is a double radio source with diffuse lobe emission near its hotspots [arXiv:astro-ph/0011082]. It features two near-infrared components aligned with the radio axis and has an estimated redshift of approximately z=2.1 [arXiv:astro-ph/0011082].

sources.summary: The paper presents 5C7.208 as a double radio source with radio-optical alignment, multiple near-infrared components, red stellar-population colors, and an adopted SED redshift of z≈2.0.

quotes.quote: 5C7.208 This is an 18 arcsec double radio source with some rather diffuse lobe emission near both hotspots (Blundell et al., in prep.). It is the only source discussed in this paper which shows clear evidence for a radio-optical alignment. There are two near-IR components separated by 2.9 arcsec at a position angle (east of north) of 34^ . This is within a couple of degrees of the radio axis (Fig. fig:7p208 ). We define the brighter (at K band) northern component `a' and the fainter southern component `b'.

`a' has R-K=5.6 and is well-fit by a reddened [ E(B-V)=0.4 ] young (0.3 Gyr) galaxy at z=2.1 . Unreddened, older galaxies at slightly higher redshifts are also not ruled out. The formal redshift range allowed is 1.3 z 2.45 .

gmw_00009463.name: 5C7.245
gmw_00009463.overview: 5C7.245 is a small double radio source with a northwest hotspot that exhibits a two-peak intensity structure [arXiv:astro-ph/0011082]. The galaxy is identified as an extended near-infrared source with very red colors and a probable H-alpha emission line at a redshift of 1.61 [arXiv:astro-ph/0011082].

sources.summary: The paper describes 5C7.245 as a small double radio source with an extended near-infrared identification, very red colors, SED evidence for z≈2, and probable H emission at z=1.61.

quotes.quote: 5C7.245 This is another small (12 arcsec) double radio source (Blundell et al., in prep.). The north-west hotspot has a strange structure with two peaks in intensity separated by 3 arcsec. The near-IR identification is very close to the southern hotspot, along the radio axis, and definitely extended in the 0.8 arcsec seeing J and H images. The radio source lies in a fairly blank field with few other galaxies detected on our images. Undetected in the R and I bands, 5C7.245 has R-K>5.8 (5 arcsec apertures used because of the varied seeing of the observations).

As for most of the objects previously discussed, the spectrum is fairly flat between J and K and then drops rapidly to I and R . The SED fitting suggests an unreddened galaxy at z 2 with a formal range of 1.3 z 2.65 . The near-IR spectrum shows a probable emission line which we believe to be H at z=1.61 .

gmw_00009464.name: 5C7.271
gmw_00009464.overview: 5C7.271 is a small, resolved radio galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0011082] with a spectroscopic redshift of 2.224 [arXiv:astro-ph/0011082]. It has a resolved near-infrared counterpart [arXiv:astro-ph/0011082] but remains undetected in R and I band optical images [arXiv:astro-ph/0011082].

sources.summary: The paper uses 5C7.271 as a resolved radio galaxy with a near-infrared counterpart, optical non-detections, red colors, and a spectroscopic redshift validating its SED estimate.

quotes.quote: 5C7.271 This is a very small but resolved ( 1 arcsec) radio source (Blundell et al., in prep.). The near-IR counterpart is co-incident with this radio position and is resolved in the 0.8 arcsec seeing J and H images. The few other objects in the field appear to be relatively low-redshift galaxies. 5C7.271 is undetected in the R and I band images and has a lower limit on its colour of R-K>5.2 (5 arcsec apertures used because of the varied seeing of the observations). The SED fitting gives a wide range of acceptable redshifts ( 0.2 z 3.2 ) with a best-fit value of z=2.3 .

As mentioned in section sample , we acquired a spectroscopic redshift for this source of z=2.224 0.006 after we had obtained this imaging data. Since it has colours similar to the other objects studied here, we have used it to check the reliability of our SED fitting procedure. The similarity of the SED-fit redshift to the spectroscopic one is encouraging.

gmw_00009465.name: ERO J081539+2446.8
gmw_00009465.overview: ERO J081539+2446.8 is an extremely red galaxy located at a likely redshift of 1.200 [arXiv:astro-ph/0011082]. The galaxy is resolved in the K band and is identified as a possible radio-quiet narrow-line active galactic nucleus [arXiv:astro-ph/0011082].

sources.summary: The paper identifies ERO J081539+2446.8 as a resolved extremely red galaxy near 5C7.47, likely at z=1.200 from [O II], possibly a radio-quiet narrow-line AGN.

quotes.quote: The object to the south-east [ which we designate as ERO J081539+2446.8; co-ordinates 08 15 39.01 +24 46 51.2 (J2000.0) ] is slightly redder with 3 arcsec aperture magnitudes of K=18.92 0.06 and R=24.6 0.4 . This object is detected in the J and H images but with insufficient SNR for reliable magnitudes to be determined. The object is clearly resolved at K band with a FWHM of 1.3 arcsec in 1.0 arcsec seeing.

This leaves the most likely explanation that the line is in fact [ OII ] at a redshift of 1.200 . Given the observed K magnitude of ERO J081539+2446.8, we find its luminosity is that of an unevolved 1.6L^ galaxy at z=1.2 (for _ M =1 , _ =0 ; for _ M =0.3 , _ =0.7 the luminosity is 3L^ ).
