Stars in the halo of the spiral galaxy NGC 4407 are quite dense in one corner of the frame, where the distance to the center of the galaxy is 120 , or 8.7 kpc in projection. The stars extend well towards the center. There is some overlap with SW2 lsb31, thus a local background subtraction will be necessary.

An annular region around SW2 lsb31 was chosen to correct the function for that galaxy, though in the end, the correction did not affect either the tip magnitude or the derived distance and metallicity.

gmw_00009531.name: VCC871
gmw_00009531.overview: VCC871 is a nucleated dwarf elliptical galaxy located in the Virgo cluster [arXiv:astro-ph/0607476]. Its extended stellar halo contains resolved stars projected five kiloparsecs from the galaxy's center [arXiv:astro-ph/0607476].

sources.summary: The paper identifies VCC871 as a brighter nucleated dwarf elliptical whose halo lies in the SW2 ACS field, with resolved stars projected 5 kpc from its center and velocity distinct from NGC4407.

quotes.quote: That dwarf lies between two larger galaxies, one an SB spiral (NGC4407) and the other a much brighter, nucleated dE (VCC871). While the centers of those two do not fall on the small ACS field, their extended stellar halos do show up in the images.

In the other corner are found stars from the dE VCC871, 70 from its center, or 5 kpc in projection. It's interesting to note that the velocity of NGC4407 is 102 , while that of VCC871 is 1427. Clearly these two are not associated, so a determination of their distances would be valuable in understanding a kinematic detail of the Virgo cluster.

gmw_00009532.name: SDSS J014124+010306
gmw_00009532.overview: SDSS J014124+010306 is a high-redshift galaxy located at a redshift of z=1.06 [arXiv:2208.05253]. It is identified as a candidate for a central tidal disruption event, which is supported by long-term photometric variabilities and broad Mg II emission lines [arXiv:2208.05253].

sources.summary: The paper reports SDSS J014124+010306/SDSS J0141 as a z=1.06 TDE candidate with broad Mg II emission, SDSS variability, a preferred TDE model, and discrepant virial versus TDE black-hole masses.

quotes.quote: In the Letter, a new candidate for central tidal disruption event (TDE) is reported in SDSS J014124+010306 (=SDSS J0141) with broad Mg  ii line at redshift z=1.06 . Based on long-term photometric ugriz band variabilities from SDSS Stripe82 Database and PHOTOOBJALL database, a central TDE is preferred with a 1.3 M_ main-sequence star tidally disrupted by central black hole (BH) of (14 2) 10^6 M_ in SDSS J0141. Moreover, CAR process has been applied to confirm that the probability is only about 0.4 that the long-term variabilities in SDSS J0141 are not related to TDE but from intrinsic AGN activities.

Meanwhile, based on the apparent broad Mg  ii emission lines, virial BH mass can be estimated as 245 10^6 M_ , 18 times larger than the TDE-model determined BH mass, providing further clues to support a central TDE in SDSS J0141, similar as the case in the TDE candidate SDSS J0159 with virial BH mass two magnitudes larger than M-sigma relation expected BH mass. Among the reported optical TDE candidates, SDSS J0141 is the candidate at the highest redshift. The results in the Letter indicate it should be common to detect TDE candidates in high redshift galaxies with broad Mg  ii lines.

gmw_00009533.name: HELMS RED 3
gmw_00009533.overview: HELMS RED 3 is a high-redshift infrared galaxy that is bright at 500 microns [arXiv:1601.02665]. Photometry measurements of the source yield an observed modified-blackbody temperature of 11.10 K [arXiv:1601.02665].

sources.summary: The paper treats HELMS RED 3 as a 500-micron-bright HeLMS red source; CSO/MUSIC and SPIRE photometry gave acceptable modified-blackbody fits and an observed temperature of 11.10 K.

quotes.quote: We observed four of our 500-micron-bright sources (HELMS RED 3, HELMS RED 4, HELMS RED 6 and HELMS RED 7) at the Caltech Submillimeter Observatory using the Multiwavelength Submillimeter Inductance Camera MUSIC, sayers2014 .

The results of the measurements are listed in Table tbl:music . In Fig. fig:musicfits we show modified blackbody fits to the SPIRE and MUSIC photometry measurements for these four sources. We apply Gaussian priors on and _0(1+z) , as discussed in Section sec:tobs , thus we effectively fit for two parameters, T_ obs and S_ 500 (except in the case of HELMS RED 3, where attaching an S_ ^ power law onto the short wavelength side of the peak results in a better fit to our flux densities). The ^2 values are acceptable for all fits, and the observed temperatures are 11.10 1.18 K , 10.85 0.95 K , 10.79 1.32 K and 11.28 0.80 K for HELMS RED 3, HELMS RED 4, HELMS RED 6, and HELMS RED 7, respectively.

gmw_00009534.name: HELMS RED 4
gmw_00009534.overview: HELMS RED 4 is a high-redshift infrared galaxy located at a redshift of z = 5.162 [arXiv:1601.02665]. Spectroscopic observations of the galaxy have detected CO(5-4) and CO(6-5) transitions, revealing a warm dust temperature of 67 K [arXiv:1601.02665].

sources.summary: The paper confirms HELMS RED 4 as a high-redshift HeLMS red source, detecting CO(5-4) and CO(6-5) with ALMA at z=5.162 and inferring a warm dust temperature.

quotes.quote: We carried out spectroscopic observations for two of our red sources, HELMS RED 4 and HELMS RED 31, using the Atacama Large Millimeter/submillimeter Array (ALMA) during the Cycle 2 operational phase, to determine their redshifts. These two objects have photometric redshift estimates of z_ phot =5.27 0.12 and z_ phot =5.28 0.27 , respectively, based on the SPIRE flux densities and additional millimetre-wave interferometric follow-up observations (the photo- z estimation method will be presented in Clements et al. in prep.).

In the spectrum of HELMS RED 4 we detect two lines, unambiguously identified as the CO( 5-4 ) and CO( 6-5 ) transitions. These correspond to a redshift of z=5.162 , which is in very good agreement with the photo- z estimate of 5.27 .

Based on the redshift measurement of z = 5.162 and the observed temperature T_ obs = 10.9 0.9 K, we infer that HELMS RED 4 has a dust temperature of T_ d = 67 6 K, which is similar to the higher than average dust temperatures of other red sources mentioned in Section sec:tobs .

gmw_00009535.name: HELMS RED 6
gmw_00009535.overview: HELMS RED 6 is a high-redshift infrared galaxy that is bright at 500 microns [arXiv:1601.02665]. Photometric observations of the source yield an observed modified-blackbody temperature of 10.79 K [arXiv:1601.02665].

sources.summary: The paper treats HELMS RED 6 as a 500-micron-bright HeLMS red source; CSO/MUSIC and SPIRE photometry gave acceptable modified-blackbody fits and an observed temperature of 10.79 K.

quotes.quote: We observed four of our 500-micron-bright sources (HELMS RED 3, HELMS RED 4, HELMS RED 6 and HELMS RED 7) at the Caltech Submillimeter Observatory using the Multiwavelength Submillimeter Inductance Camera MUSIC, sayers2014 .

The results of the measurements are listed in Table tbl:music . In Fig. fig:musicfits we show modified blackbody fits to the SPIRE and MUSIC photometry measurements for these four sources. We apply Gaussian priors on and _0(1+z) , as discussed in Section sec:tobs , thus we effectively fit for two parameters, T_ obs and S_ 500 (except in the case of HELMS RED 3, where attaching an S_ ^ power law onto the short wavelength side of the peak results in a better fit to our flux densities). The ^2 values are acceptable for all fits, and the observed temperatures are 11.10 1.18 K , 10.85 0.95 K , 10.79 1.32 K and 11.28 0.80 K for HELMS RED 3, HELMS RED 4, HELMS RED 6, and HELMS RED 7, respectively.

gmw_00009536.name: HELMS RED 7
gmw_00009536.overview: HELMS RED 7 is a high-redshift infrared galaxy [arXiv:1601.02665]. It is classified as a 500-micron-bright source with an observed modified-blackbody temperature of 11.28 K [arXiv:1601.02665].

sources.summary: The paper treats HELMS RED 7 as a 500-micron-bright HeLMS red source; CSO/MUSIC and SPIRE photometry gave acceptable modified-blackbody fits and an observed temperature of 11.28 K.

quotes.quote: We observed four of our 500-micron-bright sources (HELMS RED 3, HELMS RED 4, HELMS RED 6 and HELMS RED 7) at the Caltech Submillimeter Observatory using the Multiwavelength Submillimeter Inductance Camera MUSIC, sayers2014 .

The results of the measurements are listed in Table tbl:music . In Fig. fig:musicfits we show modified blackbody fits to the SPIRE and MUSIC photometry measurements for these four sources. We apply Gaussian priors on and _0(1+z) , as discussed in Section sec:tobs , thus we effectively fit for two parameters, T_ obs and S_ 500 (except in the case of HELMS RED 3, where attaching an S_ ^ power law onto the short wavelength side of the peak results in a better fit to our flux densities). The ^2 values are acceptable for all fits, and the observed temperatures are 11.10 1.18 K , 10.85 0.95 K , 10.79 1.32 K and 11.28 0.80 K for HELMS RED 3, HELMS RED 4, HELMS RED 6, and HELMS RED 7, respectively.

gmw_00009537.name: HELMS RED 31
gmw_00009537.overview: HELMS RED 31 is a high-redshift infrared galaxy [arXiv:1601.02665]. Spectroscopic observations with the Atacama Large Millimeter/submillimeter Array and the Karl G. Jansky Very Large Array have confirmed its redshift to be z=3.798 [arXiv:1601.02665].

sources.summary: The paper discusses HELMS RED 31 as a HeLMS red source with ALMA spectroscopy, evaluating CO line identifications and noting later VLA confirmation of the z=3.798 solution.

quotes.quote: We carried out spectroscopic observations for two of our red sources, HELMS RED 4 and HELMS RED 31, using the Atacama Large Millimeter/submillimeter Array (ALMA) during the Cycle 2 operational phase, to determine their redshifts. These two objects have photometric redshift estimates of z_ phot =5.27 0.12 and z_ phot =5.28 0.27 , respectively, based on the SPIRE flux densities and additional millimetre-wave interferometric follow-up observations (the photo- z estimation method will be presented in Clements et al. in prep.).

Only a single strong line is seen in the spectrum of HELMS RED 31, and there are several spectral lines that could fall into this region. We can discard the possibility that the observed line is CO( 6-5 ) or a higher transition, since then we would always detect other CO rotational lines in the observed spectrum.

If we identify the detected line as the CO( 5-4 ) transition then the redshift of this source is z=4.997 and we would not see any other lines in the observed spectral range; this is consistent with the photo- z estimate. If, on the other hand, the detected line corresponds to the CO( 4-3 ) transition then the redshift is z=3.798 and we should be able to detect a [ CI ] line at 102.6 GHz. There is a possible low signal-to-noise peak near to this frequency, but the spectral feature in question is not larger than a dozen or so other spikes in the observed spectrum, and hence the identification is not definite from this measurement alone. We note that recent observations with the Karl G. Jansky Very Large Array (VLA) confirm the z=3.798 redshift value (Riechers et al. in prep.).

gmw_00009538.name: E
gmw_00009538.overview: Component E is a dusty, gas-rich galaxy that is a member of a protocluster [arXiv:1908.03199]. It is an intensely star-forming system with a broad CO emission line, which may indicate either a rotating gas disk or two distinct gas clumps [arXiv:1908.03199].

sources.summary: Component E is identified as a newly confirmed dusty, gas-rich protocluster member, red in IRAC and CO, broad-lined, possibly clumpy or rotating, and intensely star-forming.

quotes.quote: We do this to illustrate that a band-6 continuum source, detected at 6 , which we denote component `E', which lies 12 arcsec to the north of component T, can now be seen to be coincident with a very red galaxy that was detected by IRAC, as well as being detected in CO, also at 6 , but which is not seen in the F110W HST image. E lies in a confused region of the maps obtained by the Wide-field Infrared Survey Explorer WISE - wright10 and a limit cannot be set that is both meaningful and useful. Component E can thus be added to the inventory of dusty, gas-rich galaxies that form part of the protocluster associated with , making five in total, covering 15 arcsec or 120 kpc.

E lies close to the redshifts of M and C, somewhat redward of W and T, and thus further helps to explain the broad line seen originally by the 22 arcsec primary beam of GBT harris12 . The line is considerably broader than those of the other cluster galaxies, 1,380 km s ^ 1 FWHM, albeit with a large uncertainty. The signal to noise is too low for us to be fully confident, but two gas clumps may be involved, distinct both spatially and in redshift, or there could be a rotating gas disk, as with W and T, this time along PA 45^ , with the reddest component to the north east.

Scaling from the band-6 flux density and SED of component W, component E has an IR luminosity of approximately 6 10^ 12 L _ . Following kennicutt98 , this implies an SFR of 650 M _ yr ^ 1 for a chabrier03 stellar initial mass function (IMF), or considerably less for the IMF observed in distant, dusty starbursts by zhang18 .

gmw_00009539.name: SDSS J161422.13+355501.3
gmw_00009539.overview: SDSS J161422.13+355501.3 is a galaxy that hosted the supernova SN 2018bcc [arXiv:1910.06016]. Based on ultraviolet observations, the galaxy has an estimated star formation rate of approximately 0.2 solar masses per year and exhibits subsolar metallicity at the supernova's location [arXiv:1910.06016].

sources.summary: The paper identifies SDSS J161422.13+355501.3 as SN 2018bcc's host, gives its photometry, estimates low metallicity at the SN site, and derives a GALEX-based SFR.

quotes.quote: The host of SN 2018bcc is SDSS J161422.13+355501.3 and does not have a known redshift. Therefore, we used the redshift measured from the SN spectrum (Sect. sec:specID ). This galaxy has the following measured SDSS magnitudes: u=19.70 0.07, g=18.66 0.01, =18.32 0.01, i=18.07 0.01, z=17.94 0.04 .

Assuming a negative metallicity gradient in the galaxy of 0.47 dex R ^ 1 as in Taddia2015 , we derived a metallicity [ log(O/H)+12 ] at the position of the SN of 7.9 dex, which is significantly subsolar (Z = 0.16 Z _ ). This estimate would be even lower if there were projection effects. SDSS J161422.13+355501.3 is also a source in GALEX General Release 6 Bianchi2013 , with UV fluxes in the far- and near-UV bands (FUV and NUV, respectively).
We used the extinction corrected FUV band flux to estimate the star formation rate using the calibration from Salim2007 , and obtained SFR 0.2 M _ yr ^ 1 (for a Salpeter IMF).

gmw_00009540.name: NGC 5595
gmw_00009540.overview: NGC 5595 is a spiral disk galaxy that forms a gravitationally bound pair with its neighbor, NGC 5597 [arXiv:2206.05417]. Neutral hydrogen observations reveal extended northeast and southwest gas streamers, which likely formed during a recent tidal interaction between the two galaxies [arXiv:2304.06272].

sources.summary: The paper presents VLA H I observations of NGC 5595, detecting NE and SW streamers, central H I absence, differential rotation, and likely tidal structures from interaction with NGC 5597.

quotes.quote: At the angular resolution of the observations, 7 1 4 2 , while most of the \ 21 cm in NGC 5595 and in NGC 5597 has the same extent as the optical disk, we have detected for the first time extended structures (streamers) to the north-east (NE), and south-west (SW) of NGC 5595 with no counterparts in blue, red optical (continuum), 20 cm radio continuum, or H spectral-line emission. One structure is extended by 45'' to the NE with blue-shifted velocities, and the other by 20'' to the SW with red-shifted velocities with respect to the systemic velocity. No \ 21 cm emission is detected from the innermost central (nuclear) regions of either galaxy.

Figure 10 shows the VLA B-configuration \ 21 cm velocity integrated Moment 0 map from NGC 5595 in contours superimposed on itself on grey scale. Most of the emission originates from giant \ 21 cm clouds in normal differential rotation around the nucleus of the disk galaxy. The spatial extent of the \ 21 cm emission spans R.A. 18^s 09^s and Dec 16 42'15'' 16 44'10'' .
The NE and SW \ 21 cm elongated emissions may be filamentary gas structures that are most likely a result of a recent gravitational tidal interaction with its neighbor NGC 5597.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 5595 as the northwestern Sc disk companion of NGC 5597, sharing similar systemic velocity and distance but showing no optical bridge to the pair member.

quotes.quote: The disk galaxies NGC 5595 and NGC 5597 are in the Southern Virgo - Libra Cloud of galaxies, Tully's group 41-14(+14), with a very low galaxy volume density of only 0.16 galaxies Mpc ^ 3 ; see their spatial location at the galactic coordinates l^ II 332 . 8 , b^ II +40 . 7 in Plates 1 and 5 in tul87 , and in and in Fig. 2 of tam85 . Since these two galaxies are separated by 13 . ^s 54 , 138 , or about 3 . ' 97 on the plane of the sky while having similar systemic velocities (see Table 1), we may confidently say that they form a physically isolated and gravitationally bound pair of disk galaxies. Therefore, in this paper, we adopt a Hubble spectroscopic distance of D_ pair = 38.6 Mpc tul88 giving an angular-to-linear scale conversion of 1 = 187.14 pc.

Figure 1 shows a reproduction of the optical blue continuum 103aO emission from the pair of disk galaxies NGC 5597 (south-east) and NGC 5595 (north-west) in grey scale (relative units) taken with the OAN-SPM 2.1 m optical telescope in Mexico dia09 . In this blue optical continuum image, there are no bridges or structures connecting the two galaxies.

gmw_00009541.name: NGC 5597
gmw_00009541.overview: NGC 5597 is a barred disk galaxy [arXiv:2206.05417] that forms an interacting physical pair with NGC 5595 [arXiv:1501.02022]. The galaxy is tidally disturbed [arXiv:1501.02022], featuring a nuclear region with bar-like star formation [arXiv:1501.02022] and a central area that lacks atomic hydrogen emission [arXiv:2304.06272].

sources.summary: The paper analyzes NGC 5597 in H I, finding its spectrum and rotation curve, a central absence of H I, and disturbed regions near the bar.

quotes.quote: Figure 3-Left shows our VLA B-configuration \ 21 cm spectrum for NGC 5597. Its overall shape is similar only to the previous Nan ay spectrum, because it has the approximate east - west angular resolution to isolate the emission from NGC 5597 pat03 . The full width of the \ emission line at 20 of the peak, seen in our VLA B-configuration spectrum of NGC 5597, is V_ 20 239.25 km s ^ 1 while the full width at 50 of the peak is V_ 50 211.40 km s ^ 1 (see Figure 3-left).

Remarkably in NGC 5597 there is no \ emission from the central region that includes the nucleus and the circumnuclear area. Another area that lacks \ emission is located to the SW of the optical stellar bar and coincides with the odd SW optical spiral arm labeled 2 in Figure 2. It is worth noting that in other disk galaxies (e.g., M31) \ holes are associated with \ regions com02 , with hot gas of T 10^4 K spi78 , or regions with newly formed stars. Furthermore, there is no \ cold gas emission from the position of the optical nucleus, (J2000) = 14^ h 24^ m 27 . ^ s 49 , (J2000) = 16 45'45 9 , nor from the innermost circumnuclear region where the gas is hot and ionized as indicated by the H continuum-free gar96 and 20 cm radio continuum emission from that region con90 and our new VLA B-configuration 20 cm continuum image (see following sections).

sources.summary: NGC 5597 is described as a tidally disturbed interacting pair member with bar-like nuclear star formation, young stellar continuum, WR features, and other H II regions.

quotes.quote: NGC 5597: This somewhat tidally-disturbed galaxy forms an interacting physical pair with NGC 5595. The nuclear region forms a bar of star formation imaged with HST in the red continuum by Malkan98 . The nuclear continuum spectrum is dominated by young stars, and a broad He 2 4686 feature from a WR population is faintly visible. A number of other \ regions are visible in the general field.

sources.summary: The paper reports VLA 21 cm observations of barred disk galaxy NGC 5597 and estimates its stellar bar pattern speed and possible outer m=4 resonance.

quotes.quote: We report Very Large Array B-configuration observations of the atomic hydrogen 21 cm line emission from the barred disk galaxy NGC 5597 at an angular resolution of 7 1 4 2 . Using the resonance method, and assuming the ratio of the corotation radius to the semi-major axis of the stellar bar is unity ( R R_ CR /a_ bar = 1 ), we estimate the angular pattern speed of the stellar bar to be, _ bar 15.3 km s ^ 1 kpc ^ 1 . This constant value for _ bar crosses _ gas + (R)/4 at a distance 6.73 kpc which would correspond to the spatial location of the north spiral structure near an outer m=4 resonance. This value of _ bar is similar to the values estimated for other bright nearby barred galaxies that exhibit circumnuclear rings (near ILR) or outer rings (near OLR).

gmw_00009542.name: system 1
gmw_00009542.overview: System 1 is a background galaxy located at a secure spectroscopic redshift of z = 3.93 [arXiv:1810.08122]. It is gravitationally lensed by the northeast clump of the cluster RXJ0152 into multiple images [arXiv:1810.08122].

sources.summary: The paper describes system 1 as a z=3.93 background galaxy lensed by RXJ0152, with multiple images, a secure spectroscopic redshift, and use as a model constraint.

quotes.quote: In the central region of the cluster, where the SL features are seen, Umetsu2005 found the first multiple-image system, a z=3.93 galaxy lensed by the NE clump into three multiple images which allowed them to study the lensed galaxy in detail and construct a simple symmetric mass model for the NE clump with some priors drawn from previous mass estimates from WL by Jee2005 . However, having only one multiple-image system usually allows one to only assess the enclosed mass within the system's effective Einstein radius, rather than to actually constrain the overall mass distribution and profile of the cluster.

We measure two secure redshifts in this field, both from detection of Ly- . An image of system 1 at 1:52:45.358, -13:57:07.75, confirming the redshift previously measured by Umetsu2005 , z_ spec = 3.930 , and a galaxy at 1:52:39.566, -13:58:37.11, z_ spec = 3.611 .

The first multiply-imaged system used to constrain the mass model was reported by Umetsu2005 who measured a spectroscopic redshift of 3.93 with the Faint Object Camera And Spectrograph on the Subaru telescope (FOCAS). This background galaxy is lensed by the NE clump into three multiple images appearing as a greenish galaxy on the HST composite image in Figure macs0308cc . We report however an additional image in the system, image 1.4, which lies next to a nearby cluster member.

gmw_00009543.name: System 5
gmw_00009543.overview: System 5 is a high-redshift galaxy that is strongly gravitationally lensed [arXiv:1810.08122]. The system consists of three multiple images, including two arc-shaped images with bright emission knots and a candidate counter-image [arXiv:1810.08122].

sources.summary: The paper identifies System 5 as a multiply imaged lensed source with arc-like images, bright emission knots, a candidate counter-image, and a model-preferred redshift range of 2.5-3.1.

quotes.quote: System 5 consists of three multiple images, images 5.1 and 5.2 are stretched into an arc shape, with two, bright emission knots, appearing light green in a composite color image as in Figure macs0308cc . A candidate counter-image c5.3 sits on the other side of the opposite critical curve. As seen in Table table:0308 , the redshift of this system is not well constrained with the LTM optimization. We use a predicting tool to de-lens one image of the system to the source plane and back to the image plane to compare the model-predicted and observed location and orientation reproduction. Our best-fit LTM model for prefers a higher redhsift (in the redshift range 2.5-3.1 ), in good agreement with the Lenstool and BPZ estimations.

gmw_00009544.name: KPG64A
gmw_00009544.overview: KPG64A is a spiral galaxy classified as either SA(s)b pec or Sc pec [arXiv:astro-ph/0109231]. It features a bright, small nucleus and two centrally symmetric spiral arms that bifurcate at their outer parts and contain well-defined knotty structures [arXiv:astro-ph/0109231].

sources.summary: The paper gives image-based morphology notes for KPG64A, including classification, color information, structural features, and its role within a binary-galaxy pair.

quotes.quote: KPG64A . The galaxy is classified as SA(s)b pec. Our (B-I) colour map shows two centrally symmetric spiral arms that become bifurcated at the outer parts. The arms are sharp-defined and the B-band sharp/filtered image can show some well define knotty structures along them. A bright small nucleus can also be appreciated. We classify this galaxy as Sc pec.

gmw_00009545.name: KPG64B
gmw_00009545.overview: KPG64B is a peculiar spiral galaxy classified as SB(s)a pec [arXiv:astro-ph/0109231]. Its outer spiral structure resembles an integral sign, which may be tidally generated by an interaction, and its arms exhibit knotty and bifurcated structures [arXiv:astro-ph/0109231].

sources.summary: The paper gives image-based morphology notes for KPG64B, including classification, color information, structural features, and its role within a binary-galaxy pair.

quotes.quote: KPG64B . The galaxy is classified as SB(s)a pec. Our (B-I) colour map show an apparently inclined galaxy that could be simulating a barred structure in the central region of a lower resolution image. The outer spiral structure resembles an integral sign that could be tidally generated by the interaction, on line with simulations by Noguchi ( ). The sharp/filtered image shows a faint bifurcated structure emanating from the eastern arm. There is also evidence of knotty structure along the arms, but no clear evidence of a bar structure.

gmw_00009546.name: KPG68A
gmw_00009546.overview: KPG68A is a spiral galaxy classified as Scd: that overlaps at its southeast with its companion galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0109231]. It features a bright nucleus and multiple arms with a knotty structure along them [arXiv:astro-ph/0109231].

sources.summary: The paper gives image-based morphology notes for KPG68A, including classification, color information, structural features, and its role within a binary-galaxy pair.

quotes.quote: KPG68A . This galaxy is overlapping at the southeast with its companion. Our iterative modeling of the light distribution is poor and caution is needed with the total magnitudes and colours for both galaxies. The galaxy is classified as a Scd:. Our sharp/filtered and B-I colour map images shows a bright nucleus plus multiple arms with knotty structure along them. The total (B-V)_ T ^ 0 colour is representative of Sb-Sbc types.

gmw_00009547.name: KPG68B
gmw_00009547.overview: KPG68B is a barred spiral galaxy classified as SBb: that overlaps at its northern side with its companion galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0109231]. Images and color maps of the galaxy reveal a bar with knotty structures along the arms extending from either end [arXiv:astro-ph/0109231].

sources.summary: The paper gives image-based morphology notes for KPG68B, including classification, color information, structural features, and its role within a binary-galaxy pair.

quotes.quote: KPG68B . This galaxy is overlapping at the north with its companion. The galaxy is classified as a SBb:. Our sharp/filtered and B-I colour map images shows knotty structure along the arms from either end of a prominent bar. The total (B-V)_ T ^ 0 colour is representative of Sb-Sbc types. The colour profiles in this barred galaxy are flat after 5 from its center.

gmw_00009548.name: KPG75A
gmw_00009548.overview: KPG75A is a spiral galaxy classified as type Sab [arXiv:astro-ph/0109231]. It features a bright central nucleus and two diffuse, open spiral arms that form an integral-sign pattern, which may be a byproduct of a galaxy interaction [arXiv:astro-ph/0109231].

sources.summary: The paper gives image-based morphology notes for KPG75A, including classification, color information, structural features, and its role within a binary-galaxy pair.

quotes.quote: KPG75A . The galaxy is classified as E?, but our (B-I) and sharp/filtered images show a bright central nucleus from which two opened and diffuse spiral arms emanate. The spiral pattern shows an integral sign (perhaps as a by product of the interaction). We classify this galaxy as Sab. The (B-V)_ T ^ 0 colour is representative of S0a-Sa types.

gmw_00009549.name: KPG75B
gmw_00009549.overview: KPG75B is a spiral galaxy classified as Sb with a bright nucleus and two wrapped adjacent spiral arms [arXiv:astro-ph/0109231]. Although it has been classified as SB?, its spiral arms may simulate a bar in projection, and its northern arm appears warped [arXiv:astro-ph/0109231].

sources.summary: The paper gives image-based morphology notes for KPG75B, including classification, color information, structural features, and its role within a binary-galaxy pair.

quotes.quote: KPG75B . The galaxy is classified as SB?, and our (B-I) and sharp/filtered images show a bright nucleus and two wrapped adjacent spiral arms that in projection may simulate a bar. We can not clearly appreciate a bar structure. The arm at the north appears warped. We classify this galaxy as Sb. The (B-V)_ T ^ 0 colour is representative of Sab-Sb types.

gmw_00009550.name: KPG88A

The (B-V)_ T ^ 0 colour is redder than that corresponding to its morphological type. Our estimated (B-V) colour is consistent, however, with that reported by Prugniel H eraudeau ( ). The colour profiles tend to be flat after 15 . Our EE class is 12.

We caution the reader about an aparent inconsistency in the results obtained for this pair.

gmw_00009570.name: 1RXSJ185800.9+485020
gmw_00009570.overview: 1RXSJ185800.9+485020 is a narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy located at a redshift of 0.078 [arXiv:1409.0058]. Initially identified as an X-ray source, the galaxy exhibits strong optical variability in its Kepler light curve [arXiv:1409.0058].

sources.summary: The paper presents nine-month reverberation mapping of 1RXSJ185800.9+485020, identifying it as a redshift-0.078 NLS1/Seyfert 1 with strong Kepler variability.

quotes.quote: To this end, we present the results of a nine-month monitoring campaign for the narrow-line Seyfert 1 (NLS1) galaxy 1RXSJ185800.9+485020, also known as , which has redshift z=0.078 and a Galactic extinction of A_V = 0.15 mag schlafly2011 . This object was identified as an X-ray source in the ROSAT All-Sky Bright Source Catalogue rosat . Prior to 2012, there was no published spectrum of in the literature, and an observation from Lick Observatory identified it as a Seyfert 1 galaxy edelsonmalkan2012 . The initial portion of 's Kepler light curve from quarters Q6 and Q7 was published by mushotzky2011 and showed strong optical variability, qualifying it as a prime candidate for reverberation mapping.

gmw_00009571.overview: The galaxy 9872 - 3701 is a star-forming system characterized by a kinematically decoupled core [arXiv:2012.07345]. It exhibits a positive stellar age gradient with its youngest stars located in the inner regions, alongside a relatively shallow metallicity gradient [arXiv:2012.07345].

sources.summary: The paper identifies 9872 - 3701 as a star-forming KDC galaxy with positive age gradients, young inner-region stars, and relatively shallow metallicity gradients.

quotes.quote: Two of the three star-forming galaxies, 9872 - 3701 and 8615 - 1902, show positive age gradients, whereas the third, 9027 - 3703, show a negative age gradient. These gradients are more significant than the ones found in the AGN-hosts. These gradients are not consistent with the age gradients found in 2017MNRAS.466.4731G . However, the SDSS morphological classification for 9027 - 3703 is a spiral galaxy, in such case the gradient is consistent with those of late-type galaxies in 2017MNRAS.466.4731G

For the star-forming galaxies, two of the three galaxies, 9872 - 3701 and 8615 - 1902, show a relatively sharp positive age gradient, with the youngest stars in the core regions, and a shallower metallicity gradient compared to the AGN-hosts. The stellar age gradients are consistent with the radial profiles obtained using the Kauffmann diagnostic, where the same two galaxies show the youngest stars in the inner regions and 9027 - 3703 showing the youngest stars in the outer regions. The positive gradients are similar to what was seen in previous studies such as Coccato_2011 and Coccato_2012 , however the shallow metallicity gradients do not seem to match what was found in these studies.

gmw_00009572.overview: The galaxy 8615 - 1902 is a star-forming system that hosts a kinematically decoupled core [arXiv:2012.07345]. It exhibits a positive stellar age gradient, featuring young stellar populations and evidence of recent star formation in its core region [arXiv:2012.07345].

sources.summary: The paper identifies 8615 - 1902 as a star-forming KDC galaxy with positive age gradients, young core populations, high core H_A, and evidence for recent central star formation.

quotes.quote: We see that 9872 - 3701 and 8615 - 1902 have increasing D _ n 4000 radially outward, with the core showing D _ n 4000 < 1.5 , indicating young stellar populations in the core compared to the outskirts. The rest of the sample show D _ n 4000 decreasing radially outward, with the core having D _ n 4000 >1.5 , denoting old stellar populations. The outskirts of the AGN-hosting galaxies show younger stellar populations. We also see that 9027--3703 and 8615-1902 have a high H _ A in the core region, and lower values in the outskirts. This indicates that recent star formation occurred in the galaxy core.

For the star-forming galaxies, two of the three galaxies, 9872 - 3701 and 8615 - 1902, show a relatively sharp positive age gradient, with the youngest stars in the core regions, and a shallower metallicity gradient compared to the AGN-hosts. The stellar age gradients are consistent with the radial profiles obtained using the Kauffmann diagnostic, where the same two galaxies show the youngest stars in the inner regions and 9027 - 3703 showing the youngest stars in the outer regions. The positive gradients are similar to what was seen in previous studies such as Coccato_2011 and Coccato_2012 , however the shallow metallicity gradients do not seem to match what was found in these studies.

gmw_00009573.overview: The galaxy 9027 - 3703 is a star-forming spiral galaxy [arXiv:2012.07345]. It features a negative stellar age gradient with its youngest stars located in its outer regions, while its most recent star formation occurred in the core [arXiv:2012.07345].

sources.summary: The paper describes 9027 - 3703 as a star-forming spiral KDC galaxy with a negative age gradient, young outer-region stars, and core recent-star-formation indicators.

quotes.quote: Two of the three star-forming galaxies, 9872 - 3701 and 8615 - 1902, show positive age gradients, whereas the third, 9027 - 3703, show a negative age gradient. These gradients are more significant than the ones found in the AGN-hosts. These gradients are not consistent with the age gradients found in 2017MNRAS.466.4731G . However, the SDSS morphological classification for 9027 - 3703 is a spiral galaxy, in such case the gradient is consistent with those of late-type galaxies in 2017MNRAS.466.4731G

The radial gradients for two of the three, 9027 - 3703 and 8615 - 1902 indicate that the most recent star formation occurred in the core. The gaseous velocity maps show that the gas is counter-rotating with the main stellar body, it is possible that the accreted counter-rotating gas, which is the dominating system for star-forming galaxies, is still present and forming stars.

The stellar age gradients are consistent with the radial profiles obtained using the Kauffmann diagnostic, where the same two galaxies show the youngest stars in the inner regions and 9027 - 3703 showing the youngest stars in the outer regions.

gmw_00009574.name: host of GRB 031203
gmw_00009574.overview: The host galaxy of GRB 031203 is a dwarf starburst galaxy [arXiv:1808.09228]. It contains molecular gas, with an estimated molecular hydrogen gas mass of approximately 1.7 to 2.3 billion solar masses [arXiv:1808.09228].

sources.summary: The paper detects a candidate H2 ro-vibrational line in the GRB 031203 host, reanalyzes Spitzer spectra, and estimates a molecular gas mass of a few billion solar masses.

quotes.quote: Searching the data of four host galaxies, we detect a single significant rotation-vibrational H _2 line candidate, in the host of GRB 031203. Re-analysis of Spitzer mid-infrared spectra of the same GRB host gives a single low significance rotational line candidate.

In the Xshooter spectrum of the host of GRB 031203, we detect a single \ transition, 1--0 S(3), with flux significance of 4 (Figure fig:031203detection ), and with the expected centre wavelength and line shape. We estimate its flux at 1.0 0.24 10^ 17 erg s ^ 1 cm ^ 2 , with the caveats applying to the line fluxes as listed above.

Using equation 9 of Togi16 and assuming a value of n = 4.5 , T_ l = 50 K, T_ u = 2000 K, with the S(7) line flux estimate above, the calculated H _ 2 gas mass is 1.7 10 ^ 9 M _ in the host of GRB 031203 (2.3 10 ^ 9 M _ , including He and heavy element mass corrections). The variation in n from 4.0--5.0 can change the gas mass in the range (0.37--7.5) 10 ^ 9 M _ (and (0.5--10) 10 ^ 9 M _ with He and heavy element correction). The upper dust mass limit estimated is 10 ^ 8 M _ in this host Michalowski100316D . Using a gas-to-dust ratio of 500, using the scaling relation for the metallicity value of 8.2 for GRB 031203, the limit on the gas mass is <5.0 10^ 10 M _ , consistent with our estimates.

gmw_00009575.name: Q2059-360
gmw_00009575.overview: Q2059-360 is a radio-quiet quasar located at a redshift of z=3.08 [arXiv:1705.05728]. The quasar is situated close to a small Lyman-alpha blob and is absorbed by a proximate damped Lyman-alpha system [arXiv:1705.05728].

sources.summary: The paper presents MUSE observations of the radio-quiet quasar Q2059-360, redetermines its redshift, and studies its association with a nearby Lyman-alpha blob and proximate damped absorber.

quotes.quote: The radio-quiet quasar Q2059-360 at redshift z=3.08 is known to be close to a small Lyman blob (LAB) and to be absorbed by a proximate damped Ly (PDLA) system. Here, we present the Multi Unit Spectroscopic Explorer (MUSE) integral field spectroscopy follow-up of this quasi-stellar object (QSO). Our primary goal is to characterize this LAB in detail by mapping it both spatially and spectrally using the Ly line, and by looking for high-ionization lines to constrain the emission mechanism.

We compared the QSO spectrum extracted from the MUSE datacube with the template QSO spectrum built by VBRB01 on the basis of SDSS spectra. The latter have about the same resolution as MUSE, making them appropriate for the task. The most useful emission line for redshift determination is C iv 1549 , although other features such as Si iv 1396 / O iv ] 1402 and C iii ] 1908 / Fe iii can also help.

gmw_00009576.name: LAE1
gmw_00009576.overview: LAE1 is a probable Lyman-alpha emitting galaxy located at a projected distance of approximately 265 pkpc from the quasar Q2059-360 [arXiv:1705.05728]. The galaxy exhibits a two-peaked Lyman-alpha emission profile and has an extended diameter of about 34 to 50 pkpc [arXiv:1705.05728].

sources.summary: LAE1 is described as a probable Lyman-alpha emitter near Q2059-360, 265 pkpc away, extended over tens of pkpc, with a two-peaked Ly-alpha emission profile.

quotes.quote: The brighter feature (LAE1) lies 34 \ away from the QSO, which corresponds to a projected distance of about 265  pkpc, and it emits within the range 4960 \ to 4975 . The vicinity to the QSO and the emission at wavelengths identical to the central LAB suggest that this object is emitting in the Ly line. Figure  fig:LAE1 (left panel) shows the extension of this emitter as found with CubEx, which is about 4.3 \ or 34  pkpc in diameter; if the eastern extension is real and physically belongs to the LAE, then the major axis of the LAE would reach 6.4 \ or 50  pkpc. This appears very large, but is consistent with the finding of WBB16 , who found many examples of LAEs with Ly emission extending to 2 \ radius at similar redshift. The emission feature of this nebula consists of two peaks, as shown in the right panel of Fig.  fig:LAE1 .

gmw_00009577.name: LAE2
gmw_00009577.overview: LAE2 is a high-redshift Lyman-alpha emitting galaxy [arXiv:1705.05728]. It is located at a projected distance of 207 kiloparsecs from the quasar Q2059-360, sharing the same redshift as the surrounding Lyman-alpha blob [arXiv:1705.05728].

sources.summary: LAE2 is described as a fainter, less extended Ly-alpha emitter near Q2059-360, sharing the LAB redshift, with measured Ly-alpha flux, luminosity, and equivalent width.

quotes.quote: The second object, LAE2, appears very similar to LAE1 although it is much fainter and less extended (Fig.  fig:LAE2 , left panel). The blue component of the emission line seems brighter compared to the red component than it is in LAE1 (see Fig.  fig:LAE2 , right panel), although it is also more affected by the noise The spectrum is drawn here from a datacube from which continuum sources were subtracted, thereby avoiding contamination by galaxies close to the line of sight. . LAE2 lies 26.5 \ away from the QSO, representing a projected distance of 207  pkpc and also shares the same redshift as the LAB one. Its coordinates and characteristics are given in Table  table:LAE_prop . Its total Ly flux within a 1.0 \ radius is F_ Ly = (12.0 1.2) 10^ 18 erg s ^ 1 cm ^ 2 For the luminosity distance corresponding to the average redshift z=3.0869 , we obtain the luminosity L_ Ly = (1.0 0.1) 10^ 42 erg s ^ 1 .

gmw_00009578.name: emission line galaxy at z=0.33
aliases_external: starburst/emission-line galaxy at z=0.33 near Q2059-360
gmw_00009578.overview: This emission line galaxy is located at a redshift of z=0.33005 [arXiv:1705.05728]. Its line-ratio properties indicate a relatively high excitation typical of a star-forming galaxy, with a Balmer decrement that suggests no dust reddening [arXiv:1705.05728].

sources.summary: The paper detects an emission-line galaxy near the Q2059-360 sightline, measures z=0.33005, identifies optical lines, and finds Balmer and line-ratio properties typical of a star-forming galaxy.

quotes.quote: An emission line galaxy is detected about 5.5 \ to the NW of the QSO. The H _ , H _ , [ O II ] 3727 , [ O III ] 4959, 5007 lines are clearly identified, while the [ N II ] 6584 line marginally so. The five stronger lines allow us to determine the redshift of this galaxy at z=0.33005 0.00009 .

We now focus on other properties of this galaxy. The Balmer decrement can be estimated through the ratio H _ /H _ 2.71 : it is slightly lower than the usually adopted value of 2.86 , suggesting no significant dust reddening. The [ N II ] 6584 /H _ and [ O III ] 5007 /H _ ratios point to a rather typical star-forming galaxy see e.g. Fig. 1 LMC04 . It has a relatively high excitation: with a ratio [ O III ] 5007 / [ O II ] 3727=3.46 , it resembles the galaxies of the DR7 sample of SIM15 , although the [ O III ] 4363 line is not clearly detected.

gmw_00009579.name: J0807+2102
gmw_00009579.overview: J0807+2102 is an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2011.08872]. It exhibits a rising flux at high radio frequencies, with a slight possible increase in its X-band flux density observed over a period of 58 days [arXiv:2011.08872].

sources.summary: The paper treats J0807+2102 as one of three pilot variable AGN with rising high-frequency fluxes; follow-up found roughly steady X-band flux over weeks with a slight possible increase.

quotes.quote: Because a preliminary analysis of our first three pilot sources (J0807+2102, J0832+2302, and J0950+5128) showed rising fluxes at high frequencies, we modified our observing strategy to add higher frequency Ku band observations (12--18 GHz) for the remaining sample, and returned later to our three pilot sources on September 19, 2019 to repeat the X band observations and add Ku band. We discuss the X-band variability on weeks timescale for these three sources in Section  sec:variability_timescale .

At X band, the two observing epochs shown in Figure  fig:timeseries for J0807+2102, J0832+2302, and J0950+5128 span an even narrower range of time ( weeks) compared to the variability timescale constraints at L and S band. The X band flux densities are roughly constant, although all 3 sources exhibit a slight increase in flux of 10 over a period of 58 days. Although an increase in flux density of this magnitude could be due to the evolution of a nascent jet (see Section  sec:origin ), the presence of residual errors in the absolute flux scale of similar magnitude cannot be entirely ruled out.

gmw_00009580.name: J0832+2302
gmw_00009580.overview: J0832+2302 is a variable active galactic nucleus that exhibits a rising flux at high frequencies [arXiv:2011.08872]. Over a period of 58 days, its X-band flux density remained roughly constant with only a slight increase [arXiv:2011.08872].

sources.summary: The paper treats J0832+2302 as one of three pilot variable AGN with rising high-frequency fluxes; follow-up found roughly steady X-band flux over weeks with a slight possible increase.

quotes.quote: Because a preliminary analysis of our first three pilot sources (J0807+2102, J0832+2302, and J0950+5128) showed rising fluxes at high frequencies, we modified our observing strategy to add higher frequency Ku band observations (12--18 GHz) for the remaining sample, and returned later to our three pilot sources on September 19, 2019 to repeat the X band observations and add Ku band. We discuss the X-band variability on weeks timescale for these three sources in Section  sec:variability_timescale .

At X band, the two observing epochs shown in Figure  fig:timeseries for J0807+2102, J0832+2302, and J0950+5128 span an even narrower range of time ( weeks) compared to the variability timescale constraints at L and S band. The X band flux densities are roughly constant, although all 3 sources exhibit a slight increase in flux of 10 over a period of 58 days. Although an increase in flux density of this magnitude could be due to the evolution of a nascent jet (see Section  sec:origin ), the presence of residual errors in the absolute flux scale of similar magnitude cannot be entirely ruled out.

gmw_00009581.name: BCG
gmw_00009581.overview: The BCG is a red-sequence galaxy that exhibits a double-object morphology consisting of two distinct components [arXiv:1106.5784]. This structure indicates an active mass assembly process, which is likely occurring via a dry merger [arXiv:1106.5784].

sources.summary: The paper identifies the cluster BCG as a red-sequence galaxy with two components, a tentative companion redshift, an X-ray-center offset, and likely dry-merger mass assembly.

quotes.quote: For the BCG, we extracted the spectra of both components ID:1-A and ID:1-B (Fig. fig:BCG , right), distinguishable in the 0.86 seeing z-band preimage of Fig. fig:BCG (left). For the second component (ID:1-B) only a tentative redshift measurement of z = 1.5001 can be obtained owing to its low signal-to-noise ratio.

On the red sequence we find a magnitude gap between the BCG and the second ranked member of m_ 12 0.8 mag. From the study of in the local universe, this points towards a dynamically evolved cluster, while the apparent offset of the BCG with respect to the center of X-ray emission ( D ^ BCG _ proj 175 kpc) is a typical sign of a dynamically young clusters Haarsma2010 . This is in line with the fact that the cluster shows indications of asymmetric X-ray emission with a low central surface brightness. A close-up look at the BCG, shown in Fig. fig:BCG (left), shows a double-object morphology, indicating an active mass assembly process via a merger event (implying that the relatively large magnitude gap is a very recent feature). Because of the lack of detectable emission lines in ID:1-A and 1-B spectra, we can state that this coalescence is likely a dry merger.

gmw_00009582.name: SDSS J120251.71+441527.4
gmw_00009582.overview: SDSS J120251.71+441527.4 is a faint host galaxy located at a redshift of z = 0.102 [arXiv:1611.10207]. It exhibits narrow Balmer and [O III] emission lines with line ratios typical of type Ic superluminous supernova hosts [arXiv:1611.10207].

sources.summary: The paper identifies SDSS J120251.71+441527.4 as Gaia16apd's faint host galaxy, deriving its redshift from narrow lines and finding line ratios typical of type Ic SLSN hosts.

quotes.quote: According to SDSS Data Release 13 sdss13 , the g band magnitude of the host galaxy SDSS J120251.71+441527.4 is 21.73 0.06 mag. Narrow, persistent Balmer emission lines and [ O iii ] 4959,5007 attributed to the host galaxy are visible in each spectrum of Gaia16apd (see Section sec:spec ). Using these narrow lines, we derived a redshift of z = 0.102 0.001 , which was adopted for Gaia16apd.

We estimated an upper limit for the [ N ii ] 6583 / H ratio as log [ N ii ] 6583 /H 0.80 dex. The measured average [ O iii ] 5007 / H ratio is log [ O iii ] 5007/H = 0.68 0.05 dex. These values are again consistent with other type I SLSN host galaxies. Thus the host galaxy of Gaia16apd seems typical for a type Ic SLSN.

gmw_00009583.name: GN15
gmw_00009583.overview: GN15, also known as HDF850.2, is a faint submillimeter source with a very low 850-micrometer flux [arXiv:astro-ph/0502526]. Its optical identification remains insecure, though a tentative counterpart is an X-ray detected Lyman-break galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0502526].

sources.summary: The paper treats GN15/HDF850.2 as a faint SCUBA source with uncertain optical identification, favoring an X-ray/LBG counterpart affected by confusion and flux boosting.

quotes.quote: GN15, also known as HDF850.2 Hughes98 , was identified in Paper II with a faint radio source to the North. However, an X-ray detected Lyman-Break Galaxy (LBG) to the west was also mentioned as a possibility. Although there is absolutely no detection of the radio source in any of the optical or near-IR bands, the X-ray/LBG pair is coincident with a red ACS galaxy and has a weak radio signal just below the threshold. This source has a very low 850 m flux and it is likely to be affected by confusion and flux boosting. Therefore although we cannot assign a secure optical counterpart, we now suggest the X-ray/LBG as a tentative identification.

gmw_00009584.name: GN23
gmw_00009584.overview: The galaxy GN23 has an ambiguous optical association due to a nearby blue optical galaxy located 1.5 arcseconds from its radio counterpart [arXiv:astro-ph/0502526]. Instead, a faint K-band smudge directly aligned with the radio source is identified as the true counterpart [arXiv:astro-ph/0502526].

sources.summary: GN23 has an ambiguous optical association: a nearby optical galaxy has inconsistent redshift indicators, while a faint K-band smudge on the radio position is preferred.

quotes.quote: The closest optical galaxy to the radio counterpart of GN23 is 1.5 arcsec away so it is unclear whether this is the same system. The optical photometric redshifts and redshifts estimates from the radio/far-IR correlation (table 5 of Paper II) do not agree, and the colours of the optical galaxy are quite blue. However, there is also a faint smudge in the K_ s band image directly on top of the radio source, below the detection limit, which we identify as the counterpart.

gmw_00009585.name: GN07
gmw_00009585.overview: GN07 is a high-redshift galaxy characterized by multi-peaked radio emission [arXiv:astro-ph/0502526]. Its submillimeter emission is associated with an optically faint radio source rather than a nearby, bluer optical galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0502526].

sources.summary: The paper describes GN07 as a multi-peaked radio source whose submillimeter emission is assigned to an optically faint radio source rather than the brighter blue galaxy.

quotes.quote: One other interesting source worth noting is GN07 which has multi-peaked radio emission within the search radius. The peak of the radio flux is coincident with a very faint ( i_ 775 29 ) optical source. A fainter radio peak ( <5 ) exists 3 arcsec to the west, coincident with a brighter ( i_ 775 24 , z 2 ) optical source with bluer colours. In the sub-mm emission is associated with the brighter optical galaxy, but the colours of this galaxy are not consistent with our other secure optical counterparts at similar redshifts. Sub-mm emission is more likely to trace the radio flux than the optical flux, and therefore we identify the sub-mm emission with the optically faint radio source.

gmw_00009586.name: GN01
gmw_00009586.overview: GN01 is a submillimeter galaxy located at a spectroscopic redshift of 2.4 [arXiv:astro-ph/0502526]. The galaxy is identified with a near-infrared source that also exhibits X-ray emission and tentative radio emission [arXiv:astro-ph/0502526].

sources.summary: GN01 is identified with a near-infrared source that also has X-ray and tentative radio emission, giving the submillimeter galaxy a spectroscopic redshift of 2.4.

quotes.quote: GN01: This source is also detected in the HDF sub-mm study at a similar flux level. There are six optical galaxies within our 7 arcsec search radius. However, only one is detected in the near-IR. This near-IR source is also detected in the X-ray and there is a hint of a detection in the radio at <5 . We identify this galaxy with the sub-mm emission. It has a spectroscopic redshift of 2.4 from .

gmw_00009587.name: GN20
gmw_00009587.overview: GN20 is one of the brightest blank-sky submillimeter sources detected with the Submillimeter Common-User Bolometer Array [arXiv:astro-ph/0502526]. It is undetected in deep radio and X-ray observations, which suggests the galaxy is located at a redshift of at least 2 [arXiv:astro-ph/0502526].

sources.summary: GN20 is presented as an exceptionally bright, robust, radio-undetected SCUBA source whose non-detections imply redshift above 2 and whose counterpart needs interferometric localization.

quotes.quote: GN20: This source is one of the brightest blank-sky sub-mm sources ever detected with SCUBA and is a very convincing example of a radio-undetected SCUBA source. Given its signal-to-noise ratio, the fact that it is separately detected in all 6 chop-throw sub-maps, and the fact that our `poorness-of-fit' statistics (see Appendix B) find nothing unusual at its position, then the chance that this source is spurious must be extremely low. It is also so far above the confusion limit that confusion and flux-boosting cannot play a significant role. This new source is not detected in the 450 m map (although the RMS there is rather poor), nor is it detected in the deep VLA radio images ( 1 RMS of 10 Jy) or the Chandra 2 Msec image. Using the far-IR/radio correlation, these non-detections imply that the sub-mm bright galaxy is at a redshift of at least 2.

The ACS images reveal 13 optical galaxies within the 7 arcsec search radius, 2 of which are detected above 5 in the K band image. If we consider the redshift constraints from the lack of radio flux, then we can eliminate one of these, and our identification technique selects the other red source. We have recently acquired follow-up continuum mapping at 1.3 mm with the IRAM Plateau de Bureau interferometer to localize the sub-mm emission and confirm the correct optical counterpart.

gmw_00009588.name: GN28
gmw_00009588.overview: The submillimeter source GN28 is located in a crowded field surrounded by eleven optical sources and one X-ray source [arXiv:astro-ph/0502526]. Although it has multiple possible optical counterparts, its submillimeter emission is likely associated with the obscured X-ray source [arXiv:astro-ph/0502526].

sources.summary: GN28 lies in a crowded field with many optical sources and one X-ray source; the authors favor an obscured X-ray association but leave multiple counterparts.

quotes.quote: GN28: This source resides in a crowded area surrounded by 11 optical sources and 1 X-ray source. The X-ray source is not coincident with any of the >5 optical sources, although there is a faint smudge in the K band image. None of the optical galaxies stand out with our identification criterion (see Section 5). Therefore, while it is likely that the sub-mm emission is associated with the obscured X-ray source, we list this source as having multiple possible optical counterparts.

gmw_00009589.name: GN37
gmw_00009589.overview: GN37 is a submillimeter source located in the high-redshift universe [arXiv:astro-ph/0502526]. It is tentatively identified as a red galaxy at a spectroscopic redshift of approximately 3.2 [arXiv:astro-ph/0502526].

sources.summary: The paper argues that GN37 is best identified with a red z=3.2 galaxy after rejecting a brighter low-redshift source lacking radio emission.

quotes.quote: GN37: Only 2 of the 14 optical galaxies within 7 arcsec of this sub-mm source have any near-IR emission. One is very bright and has a secure photometric redshift of 0.43, while the other has a spectroscopic redshift of 3.2 Cowie04 . The first source can be eliminated due to the lack of radio emission and we tentatively identify this source with the red z 3.2 galaxy.

gmw_00009590.name: GN20.2
gmw_00009590.overview: GN20.2 is a high-redshift companion galaxy located 20 arcseconds away from the bright source GN20 [arXiv:astro-ph/0502526]. The galaxy has a secure counterpart with a nearby radio source and is well fit by a starburst template [arXiv:astro-ph/0502526].

sources.summary: GN20.2 is described as a distinct companion to GN20 with a nearby radio source, secure counterpart, photometric redshift 3.91, and starburst-template fit.

quotes.quote: GN20.2: This source is the companion to our new bright source. It is 20 arcsec away, which implies that it is a distinct object. However, we should also consider the possibility that GN20 is extended (or has multiple images) due to lensing. GN20.2 does have a radio source nearby and therefore, given our criteria for identification, this is the secure counterpart. It has a photometric redshift of 3.91, and is very well fit to a starburst template.

gmw_00009591.name: J0158
gmw_00009591.overview: The galaxy J0158 is a narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy [arXiv:1707.09087, arXiv:2211.17097] located at a redshift of z=0.0804 [arXiv:2211.17097]. It hosts a low-mass supermassive black hole [arXiv:1707.09087, arXiv:2211.17097] and exhibits a low quiescent luminosity [arXiv:1707.09087].

sources.summary: The paper describes J0158 as the Seyfert 1 host of PS16dtm, compact in WISE imaging, with a low-mass black hole and low quiescent luminosity.

quotes.quote: Recently, Blanchard et al (2017) have claimed that they have discovered a TDE candidate PS16dtm in SDSS J015804.75-005221.8 (hereafter J0158 for short), which is a Seyfert 1 galaxy with low-mass black hole ( 10^6 , Greene Ho 2007; Xiao et al. 2011).
J0158 is a point-like source in all of the \ images and there's no any potential contamination within 20 .
First, the host of PS16dtm before the outburst is a low-luminosity Seyfert 1 galaxy with L_ X =1.2 10^ 42 \ (Pons Watson 2014) and L_ (5100 )/ L_ X =0.5 (Blanchard et al. 2017).

sources.summary: The paper identifies SDSSJ015804.75-005221.8 as PS16dtm's z=0.0804 NLSy1 host with a low-mass SMBH, then uses modeled host photometry and SED corrections for transient analysis.

quotes.quote: The host galaxy of PS16dtm, SDSSJ015804.75-005221.8, is a narrow-line Seyfert 1 (NLSy1) galaxy at z=0.0804 with a SMBH with 10^6M_ , measured by the velocity dispersion method 2011ApJ...739...28X and from the AGN luminosity and the radius of the broad-line region (BLR) (B17).

2021ApJ...910...83H fitted archival multiwavelength photometry from GALEX, 2MASS, SDSS and WISE of the host galaxy and modeled the spectral energy distribution (SED) of the host galaxy, from which they updated the magnitudes in the NUV UVOT filters.

Therefore, the NUV Swift/UVOT magnitudes of the PS16dtm host galaxy are different to those used in B17, leading to an average difference in the host-subtracted photometry of PS16dtm compared to the ones in B17 by 0.58 magnitudes for UVW2 and by 0.02-0.07 magnitudes for U , B , V , UVW1 and UVM2 .

gmw_00009592.name: IRAS 17179+5444
gmw_00009592.overview: IRAS 17179+5444 is a Seyfert 2 galaxy merger system [arXiv:2402.10786] that hosts a hard active galactic nucleus [arXiv:0907.1236, arXiv:1805.00514]. Its complex nuclear region features paired condensations [arXiv:1805.00514], a single nucleus surrounded by tidal material [arXiv:2402.10786], and a faint southern tail [arXiv:1805.00514].

sources.summary: The paper describes IRAS F17179+5444 as a complex nuclear system with paired condensations, a faint tail, clumpy [OIII] emission, and a compact nuclear outflow-dominated source.

quotes.quote: IRAS F17179+5444 The HST continuum image of F17179+5444 shows a complex morphology. Two condensations, separated by 0.47 arcsec ( 1.16 kpc) and connected by a bridge of enhanced continuum emission, are observed in the nuclear region of the galaxy. These condensations are surrounded by a semi-circular halo of diffuse emission, where a series of dust features is clearly visible. In addition, there is a faint tail of continuum emission emerging from the south of the nuclear region and extended 6 arcsec ( 15 kpc) towards the SE of the galaxy. In contrast to the continuum structure, the [ OIII ] emission from the galaxy shows four clumps of ionized gas emission.

The compact northern clump is substantially brighter than the other three, and coincides with the location of the brighter, southern condensation in the nuclear continuum emission. We assume that the latter corresponds to the AGN nucleus. A second region of enhanced [ OIII ] emission emerges 0.4 arcsec to the south of the northern clump, and two additional condensations 0.8 arcsec ( 2.0 kpc) to the SE and SW of the northern clump; the maximum extent of the emission line structures is 1.1 arcsec (2.6 kpc).

sources.summary: The paper highlights IRAS 17179+5444 for an anomalously strong 7.65 micron Ne VI line, interpreting it as genuine evidence of hard nuclear activity.

quotes.quote: The 7.65 m fine-structure line IRAS 17179+5444 exhibits a prominent emission at 7.65 m, blended with the adjacent PAH feature, that we interpret as the unresolved [ Ne vi ] fine-structure line. Due to its ionization energy of 126 eV this feature is connected to the hardest nuclear activity, and indeed it is convincingly observed also in other three Seyfert galaxies (IRAS 05189 2524, IRAS 12072 0444 and IRAS 13454 2956) plus IRAS 04103 2838. Fluxes and equivalent widths are listed in Tab. t4 , and are in good agreement with the values provided by Veilleux et al. (2009) for their positive detections. We note that our identification of this line also in IRAS 04103 2838 represents the first detection involving a non-Seyfert ULIRG. In IRAS 17179+5444 the strength of [ Ne vi ] is anomalously high, as evinced through both a visual inspection and a comparison with the [ Ne v ] lines at longer wavelengths (Farrah et al. 2007). This is a very interesting case, since after a separate check of the single spectra from each nod position the line is confirmed to be genuine.

sources.summary: The paper describes IRAS 17179+5444 as a Seyfert 2 merger with a single nucleus, southwest tidal material, and a GPS radio source whose turnover matches prior modeling.

quotes.quote: IRAS 17179+5444: classified as a Seyfert 2 merger system with a single nucleus surrounded by a tidal material which seems to be extending to the south-west Veilleux02 . This galaxy is known to host a GPS radio source, revealed from the VLBI imaging Vries09 . The SYNAGE modeling by Nandi21 reveals a spectral turnover at 590 MHz, similar to that obtained by us.

gmw_00009593.name: 5BZQJ 0125-0005
gmw_00009593.overview: 5BZQJ 0125-0005 is a known blazar [arXiv:1804.03703, arXiv:2012.01842] that has been firmly detected as a low-TS gamma-ray source [arXiv:1804.03703]. The active galactic nucleus exhibits low-frequency variability on timescales of years [arXiv:2012.01842].

sources.summary: The paper treats 5BZQJ 0125-0005 as a firm low-TS gamma-ray detection whose TS map shows a point-like signature aligned with the blazar position.

quotes.quote: 5BZQJ 0125-0005. This source has been detected with TS 20 when integrating over 7.5 yrs with Pass 8 data. Its light curve does not present strong flaring episodes, but we identify the most relevant bin as July 31 to August 31, 2013, as used to build the TS map. Although the TS value is below 25, we consider it a firm detection since the TS map ( bzq0125 ) clearly shows the \ point-like signature emerge from a low-TS background, and the 68 confinement region is compatible with the blazar position. A likelihood analysis with the 3FGL setup gives TS ^ Pass 7 _ 4 yrs = 12.9 with parameters E _0 = 1000 MeV, N _0 = 2.8 10 ^ 13 ph/cm ^2 /s/MeV, and = 2.68, again in agreement with parameters from Table detectiontab .

sources.summary: The paper reports GLEAM J012528--000557 as a known blazar found variable in this work and another 150 MHz survey, where year-timescale low-frequency variability is expected.

quotes.quote: GLEAM J012528--000557 referred to as J012528+000505 in hajela2019gmrt , is found to be variable in this work and by . This source is a known blazar (Section sec:results_blazarxmatch ) and thus variability at these frequencies on timescales of years is not unexpected.

We compare our variable sources with several notable single frequency variability surveys conducted around 150 MHz and only find two sources which are classified as variable in each, both of which are known blazars: GLEAM J012528--000557 and GLEAM J013243-165444.

gmw_00009594.name: 2WHSPJ 164014.8+685233
gmw_00009594.overview: 2WHSPJ 164014.8+685233 is a high-synchrotron-peaked blazar [arXiv:1804.03703]. It is an active galactic nucleus that has been identified as a new gamma-ray detection with resolved spectral energy distributions [arXiv:1804.03703].

sources.summary: The paper identifies 2WHSPJ 164014.8+685233 as the dominant HSP component in a confused gamma-ray field and presents it as a new detection with resolved SEDs.

quotes.quote: Even though no \ source is reported within 1 ^ of 5BZQJ 1642+6856 in any of the FGL catalogs, our likelihood analysis finds a \ signature matching the position of this source, which is located only 10 arcmin from the HSP blazar 2WHSPJ 164014.8+685233. Indeed, the high-energy E > 1.0 GeV TS map (Fig. 2whsp1640 in top left panel) reveals a dominant \ signature coincident with the 2WHSP source, however extending toward the position of 5BZQJ 1642+6856. We then consider photons in the 0.1-500 GeV band and a likelihood function including two nearby point-like emitters, which gives us an estimate of the \ spectral properties for each source as shown in Table tableFermi2 . Despite the low statistical significance associated with BZQJ 1642+6856 \ signature (TS < 10), we note that the photon spectral index estimated for the pair 5BZQ 2WHSP is consistent with the hypothesis of confusion between a steep and a hard component as expected for nearby LSP and HSP blazars. As a consistency test, we built an additional high-energy TS map (E > 1 GeV) by adding a point-like source at the 2WHSP position modeled as a power-law with the same parameters as from Table solved1 , such that the 2WHSP \ signature is now part of the background model.

Since those \ signatures have not been reported in previous high-energy catalogs, we present 2WHSPJ 164014.8+685233 as a new detection and 5BZQJ 1642+6856 as a relevant signature, whose resolved SEDs are shown in Fig. 2whsp1640 (middle and bottom panels).

gmw_00009595.name: 5BZQJ 0228+6721
gmw_00009595.overview: 5BZQJ 0228+6721 is a blazar located close to the Galactic plane [arXiv:1804.03703]. Radio monitoring of the source has shown signs of variability, with some high-frequency observations suggesting periodicity that is not confirmed by lower-frequency data [arXiv:astro-ph/0401501].

sources.summary: The paper revises the gamma-ray association in a difficult Galactic-plane field, finding the 3FGL emission compatible with blazar 5BZQJ 0228+6721 rather than GB6J 0229+6706.

quotes.quote: Blazar 5BZQJ 0228+6721 had no \ counterpart in previous FGL catalogs, however a \ source at its vicinity was detected (1FGLJ 0233.4+6654) but with no consistent position. In this same region the source 3FGLJ 0228.5+6703 has been associated with a radio source GB6J 0229+6706 GB: from Green Bank 4.85 GHz northern sky survey, GB6radio which is within the 3FGL error circle (dot-dashed line, Fig. bzq0228 top left). This field is difficult to study because it is very close to the Galactic plane (at latitude b 6 ^o ) and the intense low-energy diffuse \ background can hinder both the source localization and detection when integrating over the full energy bandwidth. Since the 3FGL position is only 5.94 arcmin from the blazar, we investigated whether we could improve the \ localization of this source working with E > 500 MeV photons, benefiting from lower background intensity, improved PSF, and longer integration time (from 4.0 to 7.5 years).

We calculated the E > 500 MeV TS map (Fig. bzq0228 , top left) showing that the 68 containment region for the \ signature is compatible with 5BZQJ 0228+6721 (while GB6J 0229+6706 is out of the 99 containment region) and therefore the 3FGL association should be revised. We recalculated the \ SED (Fig. bzq0228 , bottom) assuming a single source with position corresponding to 5BZQJ 0228+6721, and found it is consistent with the end-tail IC bump.

sources.summary: The paper reports 0224+671 as a blazar with Mets ahovi periodicity at 22 and 37 GHz, but UMRAO data give inconsistent, longer periods and do not confirm the high-frequency periodicity.

quotes.quote: We have carried out a multifrequency analysis of the radio variability of blazars, exploiting the data obtained during the extensive monitoring programs carried out at the University of Michigan Radio Astronomy Observatory (UMRAO, at 4.8, 8, and 14.5 GHz) and at the Mets ahovi Radio Observatory (22 and 37 GHz). Two different techniques detect, in the Mets ahovi light curves, evidences of periodicity at both frequencies for 5 sources ( 0224+671 , 0945+408 , 1226+023 , 2200+420 , and 2251+158 ).

On the other hand, the 22 and 37 GHz periodicities of 0224+671 and 0945+408 (which were less extensively monitored at Mets ahovi and for which we get a significant false-alarm probability) are not confirmed by the UMRAO database, where some indications of ill-defined periods about a factor of two longer are retrieved.

In the case of A 0224+671 the analysis of UMRAO data yields significantly different periods at 4.8 GHz compared to 8 and 14.5 GHz; all of them are substantially higher (by roughly a factor of 2) than those found from the Mets ahovi light curves.

gmw_00009596.name: GS9422
gmw_00009596.overview: GS9422 is a high-redshift galaxy located at z=5.943 [arXiv:2404.02333]. Its ionizing radiation is characterized by a combination of young, metal-poor stars and a low-luminosity active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2404.02333].

sources.summary: The paper models GS9422 at z=5.943 and argues its unusual nebular emission can be explained by young metal-poor stars plus a low-luminosity AGN rather than a top-heavy IMF.

quotes.quote: One galaxy recently observed at z=5.943 , GS9422, has nebular line and UV continuum emission that appears to require a high ionizing photon production efficiency. This has been explained with an exotic stellar initial mass function (IMF), 10-30x more top-heavy than a Salpeter IMF Cameron2023d . Here we suggest an alternate explanation to this exotic IMF. We use a new flexible neural net emulator for CLOUDY, , to infer the shape of the ionizing spectrum directly from the observed emission line fluxes.

\ finds that the ionizing radiation from GS9422 can be approximated by a double power law characterized by = -1.5 , which can be interpreted as a combination of young, metal-poor stars and a low-luminosity active galactic nucleus (AGN) with F_ ^ 2 in a 65 /35 ratio. This suggests a significantly lower nebular continuum contribution to the observed UV flux (24 ) than a top-heavy IMF ( 80 ), and hence, necessitates a damped Lyman- absorber (DLA) to explain the continuum turnover bluewards of 1400 .

gmw_00009597.name: J1045+5631
gmw_00009597.overview: J1045+5631 is a remnant radio galaxy candidate with a revised stellar mass [arXiv:2106.13161]. The galaxy exhibits a flattening of its radio spectrum at high frequencies and has a core prominence lower than expected for an evolved active radio source [arXiv:2106.13161].

sources.summary: The paper confirms J1045+5631 as a remnant candidate with uncertain classification, revised stellar mass, high-frequency spectral flattening, and core prominence lower than expected for an evolved active source.

quotes.quote: Here we only re-calculated the stellar mass of the source J1045+5631, due to the more reliable redshift value found in the LH Deep field catalogue.

We found that seven sources have USS already at low frequencies (5 of these were already identified as US by MB17). We also note that sources J1045+5631 and J1047+5550 show a significant flattening of their spectrum at a high frequency (see Sect. sec:discussion ).

Radio spectrum of this source is not curved and the spectral index is not US. In this study, we confirm source J1045+5631 as a remnant based on its low CP compared to its total radio luminosity. We note that in our previous work 2020A&A...638A..34Jurlin the same source was selected as a restarted radio galaxy, based on its high core prominence as a sign of the newly active radio core. In this study we conclude that the CP is although high, considered to be lower than expected if J1045+5631 was an evolved active radio source.

gmw_00009598.name: J1047+5550
gmw_00009598.overview: J1047+5550 is a giant radio galaxy with a linear size exceeding 0.7 megaparsecs [arXiv:2106.13161]. It is classified as a remnant radio galaxy that exhibits ultra-steep spectrum emission at low frequencies and a detected core that may indicate restarted nuclear activity [arXiv:2106.13161].

sources.summary: The paper treats J1047+5550 as a remnant radio galaxy with low-frequency ultra-steep emission, high-frequency flattening, a detected core, and possible restarted nuclear activity.

quotes.quote: We also note that in two sources (J1045+5631 and J1047+5550) we see a flattening of the integrated spectrum towards higher frequencies. As can be seen from Fig. fig:remnants1 , in these sources central emission dominates the total flux density. Source J1047+5550 has USS at low frequencies, confirming its remnant nature, while the flat central emission and detection of the core, both at 6000 MHz, might point to a restart of the nuclear activity.

On the contrary, ultra-steep sources with high levels of CP for their total power might be hinting at a restarting jet activity in the nuclear regions (e.g. sources J1031+5915, J1047+5550, J1054+5505 and J1055+5635, marked with stars above the green line).
The observed linear sizes are in the range [ 214.40, 1385 ] kpc, including 3 giant radio galaxies (linear size > 0.7 Mpc; sources J1031+5915, J1047+5550 and J1052+5636).

gmw_00009599.name: J1052+5636
gmw_00009599.overview: J1052+5636 is an X-shaped remnant radio source that exhibits nuclear radio activity [arXiv:2106.13161]. It is classified as a giant radio galaxy, possessing a linear size greater than 0.7 megaparsecs [arXiv:2106.13161].

sources.summary: The paper describes J1052+5636 as an X-shaped remnant radio source with nuclear radio activity and a giant-radio-galaxy-scale linear size.

quotes.quote: A similar approach was also used for the sources with (ii) amorphous morphology, and (iii) other morphologies (including the X-shaped source J1052+5636 and source J1036+5540). We considered all the optical galaxies within the 3 _ local radio contours to be possible host galaxies.

We confirm that some of the remnants (including some classified based on the USS emission) do show the presence of some level of nuclear radio activity (J1031+5915, J1045+5631, J1047+5550, J1052+5636, J1054+5505 and J1055+5635). The presence of cores (mean core radio luminosity of remnants being log_ 10 (L^ core _ 6000 MHz / W Hz ^ 1 ) = 23) means that not all remnants have a very low CP.
The observed linear sizes are in the range [ 214.40, 1385 ] kpc, including 3 giant radio galaxies (linear size > 0.7 Mpc; sources J1031+5915, J1047+5550 and J1052+5636).

gmw_00009600.name: J1054+5505
gmw_00009600.overview: J1054+5505 is a remnant radio galaxy located in the cluster J105401.2+550611 at a redshift of z = 0.4 [arXiv:2106.13161]. It is associated with the cluster's central galaxy, hosting a detected radio core and showing signs of restarting jet activity [arXiv:2106.13161].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J1054+5505 as a confirmed remnant in a cluster, associated with the central galaxy, with a detected core and possible restarting jet activity.

quotes.quote: The three sources, J1054+5505, J1102+5857 and J1108+5831, belong to the clusters J105401.2+550611 (z = 0.4), J110252.6+585731 ^ +RedMaPPer (z = 0.34; z ^ RedMaPPer = 0.36 0.02) and J110804.3+583141 (z = 0.15), as they are located at the same redshift and have a projected distance from the cluster centre of 2, 124 and 1 kpc, respectively.

On the contrary, ultra-steep sources with high levels of CP for their total power might be hinting at a restarting jet activity in the nuclear regions (e.g. sources J1031+5915, J1047+5550, J1054+5505 and J1055+5635, marked with stars above the green line).

Sources J1054+5505 and J1108+5831 have a distance from the cluster centre of 2 and 1 kpc, respectively, and are therefore associated to the central galaxy of the cluster. The third remnant lies instead at 124 kpc from the cluster centre. We do not find any common properties characterising all our remnant sources located in clusters. For example, source J1054+5505 has its core detected at 6000 MHz, while J1102+5857 and J1108+5831 do not.

gmw_00009601.name: J1055+5635
gmw_00009601.overview: J1055+5635 is a remnant radio galaxy whose optical host remains unidentified [arXiv:2106.13161]. The galaxy exhibits nuclear radio activity and an extremely steep spectral index, which may indicate restarting jet activity in its nuclear regions [arXiv:2106.13161].

sources.summary: The paper highlights J1055+5635 as a remnant with no identified optical host, steep spectral properties supporting a remnant interpretation, nuclear activity, and possible restarting jets.

quotes.quote: One source worth noting is J1055+5635, whose optical host remained unidentified, despite a detection of the core at 6000 MHz.
