For both, Draco and Sculptor, we see larger net enhancement for cuspy density profiles (i.e. the NFW and our sample non-parametric case), since they typically imply deeper potential wells and therefore these halos host colder particle populations at their centers. At the same time, the effect one finds for a given dwarf cannot be rigidly applied to another object, since details of the enhancement depend on the preferred region in the parameter space. In general the larger the halo concentration, the larger the flux increase: e.g., in Draco we found that the fit in case of the NFW profile points to significantly larger r_s and lower _s than for Sculptor, see figure fig:corner_draco_sculpt , while for the Burkert profile the preferred regions in parameter space are closer one to the other; correspondingly we find a smaller relative boost in the NFW versus Burkert comparison for Draco, while it is appreciably larger for Sculptor. As we wish to illustrate now, the choice of phase-space modelling has also a significant impact on the extrapolated enhancements, both when considering an approximation to the isotropic case, such as for the MB model introduced above, and even more drastically when one allows for anisotropic velocity distributions. In figure fig:J_integrand we consider the ratio S (v_ rel ) () / S (v_ rel ) _ E () , comparing the velocity averaged enhancement of a given PSDF to the Eddington's case at the radial distance from the center of halo. The angular and line-of-sight integrals appearing in the definition of J factor, see eq. ( eq:j_factor ), when pointing towards the center of a spherical object, can be reduced to an integral on , while performing the other integrals analytically, see . After rewriting J as J = _0^ r_b dr dJ/dr() , where r_b is the outer truncation radius, the appropriately normalized ratio dJ/dr over J -- assuming the Eddington's PSDF and the Coulomb regime for the Sommerfeld-enhancement --- is plotted in figure fig:J_integrand with thin black lines (with corresponding vertical axis scale at the .h.s. \ of the plots; note that this scale is logarithmic). The three lines in each plot are for three different apertures of the instrument acceptance cone . The curves for the NFW profile peak all at =0 , while for the Burkert profile the largest contribution to J shifts towards r_s ; analogous trends --- but sharper --- would appear also in the resonant regime of the Sommerfeld enhancement. This check provides a visual guidance to the plots, indicating the intervals in over which there is a significant impact on the expected annihilation flux, would a different DM velocity distribution provide a significantly different S (v_ rel ) () . In figure fig:J_integrand thick lines show this quantity as computed for the MB, OM, _c PSDF over the result for the Eddington's case (now the reference vertical scale is on the l.h.s. \ of the plots). From the plot one can see that, for the NFW profile, the Maxwell-Boltzmann approximation gives a systematic (but numerical-friendly) underestimate of the true result in the isotropic case, since the corresponding curve is smaller than 1 at the peak of the contribution to J_1 (and even more so for J_2 ), while it gets marginally above 1 in the radial range less relevant for the J factor; the MB approximation causes instead a much smaller error for the Burkert profile. In the same way one sees that even the very small bias on circular velocity introduced by the model with _ () = -1/2 , impacting on the abundance of slow particles at the center of the systems, is going to significantly boost J_ 1,2- _c for both density profiles. Finally, for what regards Osipkov-Merritt model, J_ 1,2 tend to be smaller then in the isotropic case, which however becomes significant only when the suppression in S (v_ rel ) () , which is maximized at slightly below r_a , gets within the radial range relevant for the computation of J_ 1,2 , namely if we consider r_a close to r_s for Burkert and r_a r_s for NFW profile. With the general trends delineated in figure fig:J_integrand , we can now quantify the Sommerfeld effect for Milky Way classical dSphs. From the samples obtained in the MCMC analysis described in section sec:MCMC , we can actually compute the J factor posterior distributions. Note that evaluation requires the non-trivial computational task of performing multidimensional integration for each of the MCMC events collected. In order to make the demanding numerics feasible, we can resort to the scaling relations explained in greater detail in appendix app:J-factor_scaling . In figure fig:J_histogram we show the J factor p.d.f. for our benchmark galaxies, Draco and Sculptor.

gmw_00009650.name: 5C4.152
gmw_00009650.overview: The radio galaxy 5C4.152 is an active galactic nucleus that exhibits radio lobes and jets [arXiv:1605.08991]. It features rotation measure gradients across both of its lobes, though the gradient across the southern lobe is below statistical significance [arXiv:1605.08991].

sources.summary: The paper describes 5C4.152 as a source with RM gradients across both lobes, but its reconstructed southern-lobe gradients are below statistical significance.

quotes.quote: 5C4.152 .---This source has an intriguing RM distribution, with RM gradients present across both lobes (Fig. 15 of Bonafede et al. 2010). The gradient across the southern lobe seems to be fairly orthogonal to the jet direction, while the gradient across the northern lobe appears to be offset from orthogonality as the jet bends toward the east just before the hot spot. If the gradient across the southern lobe can be taken to be transverse to the jet, its direction is CCW. We reconstructed the previously published RM map in order to estimate the significance of these RM gradients. Our quantative analysis of a series of RM slices taken across the southern lobe indicates that the strongest transverse RM gradients have significances of about 1.5 .

gmw_00009651.name: WISE J030654.88+010833.6
gmw_00009651.overview: WISE J030654.88+010833.6 is a red, extended galaxy with an estimated photometric redshift between 0.28 and 0.31 [arXiv:1908.04280]. It is a candidate ultraluminous infrared galaxy hosting a composite active galactic nucleus and starburst with dust-reprocessed mid-infrared variability [arXiv:1908.04280].

sources.summary: The paper presents WISE J030654.88+010833.6 as a red, extended, MIR-variable AGN in a candidate ULIRG with high star formation and dust-reprocessed variability.

quotes.quote: WISE J030654.88+010833.6 is a red, extended galaxy, which we estimate to be at a photometric redshift of 0.28 z 0.31, based on its optical and near-infrared spectral energy distribution (SED). The factor of two variability over 8 years seen in the WISE 3.4 and 4.6 m wavelength channels is not clearly correlated with optical variability in archival data. Based on our estimation of the physical parameters of the host galaxy, J030654.88+010833.6 is possibly a composite AGN/starburst ULIRG in a phase where high star formation 70 M _ year ^ 1 is occurring. Our estimate of the black hole mass to stellar mass ratio also appears to be consistent with that of broad line AGN in the local universe. The long-term variability of J030654.88+010833.6 as seen in the WISE W1 and W2 light curves is likely due to variations in the accretion rate, with the energy being reprocessed by dust in the vicinity of the AGN.

gmw_00009652.name: IC 65
gmw_00009652.overview: IC 65 is the brightest and most massive galaxy in its group, classified as a barred spiral with a central bulge or short bar [arXiv:0710.1786]. Its optical image exhibits mild asymmetry with a southeastern extension, while its neutral hydrogen envelope extends nearly twice the size of the optical galaxy [arXiv:0710.1786].

sources.summary: The paper presents IC 65 as the group's dominant barred spiral, with blue spiral arms, an older red central region, mild optical asymmetry, and an extended neutral-hydrogen envelope.

quotes.quote: IC 65 is by far the brightest and most massive, i.e. the principal galaxy in this group. Its optical and NIR images as well as various light distribution characteristics are shown in Figs. IC65pics.fig and IC65pics.fig2 . The B band image depicts an oval central bulge and/or a short bar with the long symmetric double-armed spiral pattern starting at its ends. The spiral arms consist of a number of bright H ii regions giving them a filamentary appearance.

The optical image of IC 65 appears slightly asymmetric with an extension to the south-east. This asymmetry can be quantified by means of the decentering degree calculated from the drift of the centre of the fitted free ellipses (Marquez Moles 1999). For the IC 65 the decentering amounts to 8.2 in the radius range of 60'' - 70'' . In contrast, the H i isophotes appear regular within the optical limits of the galaxy (vM83, Fig. 27), but show an extension to the north-west at the lowest measured H i column density level of 2.3 10^ 20 atoms cm ^ 2 , well outside the optical image. The total extent of neutral hydrogen exceeds nearly twice the optical image of the IC 65.

gmw_00009653.name: UGC 622
gmw_00009653.overview: UGC 622 is a late-type galaxy featuring an almost stellar nucleus and faint, flocculent spiral arms [arXiv:0710.1786]. Numerous blue star-forming regions are distributed along these arms, while its light profile exhibits two distinct exponential sections shaped partly by dust extinction [arXiv:0710.1786].

sources.summary: The paper describes UGC 622 as a late-type galaxy with a nucleus, flocculent arms, blue star-forming regions, and a two-component light profile shaped partly by dust extinction.

quotes.quote: The results of the surface photometry of the UGC 622 are summarized in Figs. UGC622pics.fig , UGC622pics.fig2 . This late-type galaxy has an almost stellar nucleus and/or bulge and faint flocculent spiral arms. Numerous star-forming regions, distributed along the arms are outlined with the help of the Laplacian contours. The NIR composite image shows a prominent nucleus and an almost featureless smooth disk.

UGC 622 shows a classical type II light profile (Freeman ), where the central light cusp is followed by two exponential sections with different gradients. The inner exponential in the range of 3'' 15'' has a scale length of h_B 12.1'' in B band. The outer ( 15'' ) exponential has a steeper slope with scale lengths of h_B 5.4'' in B band and h_K = 6.6'' in K band. The optical and NIR light profiles show similar behaviour, but the difference between the inner and outer exponential disk gradients becomes less pronounced at longer wavelengths - an effect of dust extinction in optical passbands.

gmw_00009654.name: UGC 608
gmw_00009654.overview: UGC 608 is a late-type barred spiral galaxy embedded in a low-surface-brightness disk [arXiv:0710.1786]. The galaxy features a young, metal-poor stellar population [arXiv:0710.1786] and is surrounded by an extended neutral hydrogen halo [arXiv:0710.1786].

sources.summary: The paper characterizes UGC 608 as a late-type barred spiral in a low-surface-brightness disk, with blue knots, young metal-poor stellar populations, and an extended H i halo.

quotes.quote: UGC 608 This is a late type SBdm spiral galaxy with a short luminous bar ( 2a 3.2 kpc, b/a = 0.25, P.A. 120^ ) embedded into a LSB disk containing faint and knotty spiral arms (Fig. UGC608pics.fig ). The most prominent, generally blue knots and blobs are delineated with the help of Laplacian contours and labelled from A through E. The B-R colour index image is noisy, and reliable individual colour estimates were obtained only for the brightest features.

Comparing the colours with the stellar population models of Bruzual Charlot ( ) we can conclude that both the centre and the underlying stellar disk of the UGC 608 are populated with young (a few Gyrs) and metal-poor ( Z 0.001) stellar populations which is quite typical of late type spirals. The fuzzy red ( B-R 2.2) non-stellar object A is probably a distant galaxy, seen through the disk of the UGC 608.

The H i map of UGC 608 (vM83, Fig. 30) shows a number of local maxima distributed in a generally regular gaseous disk. Several of these gaseous features coincide with optical star-forming knots (e.g. the triple knot E). The H i halo is 30' (32 kpc) in its largest extent, which is by a factor of about 2.4 larger than the optical diameter of the galaxy at the 25.0 B mag arcsec ^ 2 level.

gmw_00009655.name: PGC 138291
gmw_00009655.overview: PGC 138291 is an edge-on, late-type galaxy with a very flat, smooth, and regular shape [arXiv:0710.1786]. It features a low-surface-brightness disk with a low dust content and regular neutral hydrogen contours, but it lacks a visible bulge [arXiv:0710.1786].

sources.summary: The paper reports new photometry for PGC 138291, an edge-on late-type galaxy with a flat, smooth, regular low-surface-brightness disk and regular H i contours.

quotes.quote: PGC 138291 This galaxy was probably first discussed by vM83 who referred to it as an edge-on galaxy. Because its poor visibility only the position, velocity and H i flux are reported in the literature. We derived its integral magnitudes and light distribution characteristics, using our calibrated DPOSS frames. The most severe problem was proper subtraction of the bright stellar halo.

The galaxy is very flat with an axis ratio of b/a = 0.18, and it has generally a smooth regular shape. Neither the SB profiles nor the optical images prove the presence of a bulge while a tiny bulge may still remain hidden by a dominating disk component, seen edge-on. The mean colours ( B-R 1.0, R-I 0.2 ) could have been explained by a dominating population of very young (a few Gyrs) and metal-poor ( 1/5Z _ ) stars and probably low ISM/dust content in the LSB disk of this galaxy (Bruzual Charlot ). Approximate dimensions of the galaxy were measured on the DPOSS red frame: D_ 25 d_ 25 1.1 0.15 that yields a linear diameter of about 12.5 kpc. The H i contours in this edge-on galaxy as given by vM83 (his Fig. 29) appear regular.

gmw_00009656.name: A 0101+4744
gmw_00009656.overview: A 0101+4744 is a faint, low-surface-brightness dwarf irregular galaxy with a nearly exponential light distribution [arXiv:0710.1786]. It exhibits an irregular head-tail morphology featuring a luminous, blue star-forming knot on its western side and a faint, diffuse curved tail on the opposite side [arXiv:0710.1786].

sources.summary: The paper identifies A 0101+4744 as a confirmed dwarf irregular group member, with a head-tail morphology, blue star-forming knot, exponential light distribution, and matching H i velocity.

quotes.quote: A 0101+4744 This is a faint LSB anonymous galaxy located 5' (56 kpc in projection) to the NE of the IC 65. This galaxy was probably first discussed by vM83 who describes it as ... a barely significant H i detection coinciding with faint optical feature ... . This galaxy has an irregular head-tail shape with a major light concentration in a luminous and blue knot B (Fig. IC65F1Dw ), which is located in the western part of the galaxy, and a faint diffuse curved tail on the opposite side. The very blue colours of the knot B ( B-R = 0.6 0.15 and R-I = 0.1 0.2) may correspond to a recently formed ( 100 Myr) stellar supercluster (Vennik Hopp ).

The light distribution in this LSB galaxy is nearly exponential. The B-R colour index profile (starting from the knot B) shows a marginal radial colour (i.e. stellar population) gradient in the underlying disk and/or in bright knots. Because of the concordant H i radial velocity and the LSB morphology this galaxy could be considered as a certain dwarf irregular member of the group with an absolute blue magnitude of M_B = -15.4, a major axis length of about D_ 25 5 kpc and the exponential scale length of h_B 0.7 kpc.

sources.summary: The paper presents A 0101+4752 as a faint irregular galaxy with smooth low-surface-brightness light, faint resolved knots, arc-like features, and a very blue starburst-like knot.

quotes.quote: A 0101+4752 This is a faint irregular galaxy located 14 ' to the NE of IC 65, with a smooth LSB underlying light distribution and several faint resolved knots, labelled with A and B in Fig F6Dw , embedded in it. Faint arc-like features are evident on the northern and southern periphery of this galaxy, both on our CCD frames and on deeper DPOSS images. The bright non-stellar knot B is extremely blue ( B-R 0.24) when compared to the colours of another, but a semi-stellar knot A ( B-R 1.2) or to the colours of two 22 ^ nd B magnitude Galactic stars (labelled with 1 and 2) of B-R 2.0 0.15. The blue colour of the knot B is typical of a very young and short-lived starburst, which is not unusual in a dIrr galaxy.

gmw_00009657.name: A 0100+4756
gmw_00009657.overview: A 0100+4756 is a faint low-surface-brightness galaxy with an amorphous light distribution and a nearly exponential surface brightness profile [arXiv:0710.1786]. It is considered a possible dwarf member of the IC 65 group of galaxies [arXiv:0710.1786].

sources.summary: The paper treats A 0100+4756 as a possible new dwarf member near UGC 622, citing its amorphous low-surface-brightness morphology, exponential profile, blue colors, and inferred dwarf scale.

quotes.quote: A 0100+4756 This is a very faint LSB object located 5' (56 kpc in projection) to the SW of the UGC 622. The image (Fig. F2Dw ) has an amorphous light distribution with a maximal blue SB of 23.9 0.3 mag arcsec ^ 2 and without any luminous knots. The derived SB profiles are nearly exponential and the B-R and R-I colour-index profiles are essentially flat, showing that nearly homogeneous stellar populations are distributed throughout the LSB disk. The de-reddened mean colours B-R = 0.76 0.2 and R-I = 0.15 0.2 are nearly consistent with a dominating population of metal-poor ( 1/20Z _ ) and young stars (Bruzual Charlot ).

Its LSB dwarfish morphology, nearly exponential SB profile and, particularly, its blue colours very similar to those of the confirmed member A 0101+4744, and other two studied dwarf galaxies, as well as its location close to UGC 622 encourage us to consider it as a possible new dwarf member of the IC 65 group of galaxies. If it is true, then its absolute magnitude is M_ B = -14.4 (but M^ exp _ B 15.0), the diameter is D 3.6 kpc, and the scale length h 1.0 kpc.

sources.summary: The paper describes A 0100+4734 as an anonymous irregular galaxy with a head-tail appearance, multiple blue knots, diffuse low-surface-brightness component, and exponential light profiles.

quotes.quote: A 0100+4734 This is an anonymous irregular galaxy located 10.3' (115 kpc in projection) to the SW of the principal galaxy IC 65. It contains a number of bright resolved knots labelled A, B, C, and D (Fig. F5Dw ) with the brightest knot D located at the eastern periphery and, therefore, giving the galaxy a head-tail appearance. The luminous blue knots which are probably centres of active star formation, are embedded into the diffuse LSB underlying component of the size of D d 49'' 19'' . The radial SB profiles are well approximated by a simple exponential disk model.

gmw_00009658.name: GP1133 + 6514
gmw_00009658.overview: GP1133 + 6514 is a Green Pea galaxy characterized by perpendicular bi-conical outflows [arXiv:2011.02549]. It exhibits a high Lyman-alpha escape fraction [arXiv:2011.02549], a strong blue peak in its Lyman-alpha velocity profile [arXiv:1610.05767], and is predicted to be a Lyman continuum emitter [arXiv:2011.02549].

sources.summary: The paper identifies 1133+6514 as a Green Pea with Milky Way-contaminated spectra, perpendicular bi-conical outflows, high Ly escape, and predicted LyC emission.

quotes.quote: We fit the SALT model to the 1190.42 , 1193.28 \ \ doublet ( 1185 1201 ) for all of the Green Peas except galaxies 1133+6514 and 1219+1526 whose spectra suffer contamination from Milky Way absorptions. The best fit for each galaxy is provided in Figure f3 and the associated parameters have been recorded in Table tab:best_fits_SiII . For galaxies 1133+6514 , 1219+1526 , and 1054+5238 best fit parameters were extrapolated from the ( 1260.42 ) \ transitions, and are highlighted in green in Figure f3 .

Galaxies 0911+1831 , 1054+5238 , 1137+3524 , 1244+0216 , 1249+1234 , and 1424+4217 have spherical outflows in Si II, while galaxies 1133+6514 and 1219+1526 have perpendicular bi-conical outflows. Galaxy 0926+4427 is our only galaxy with a bi-conical outflow observed edge-on. (Even though > in galaxy 0926+4427 we have chosen to include 0926+4427 in this group since within our uncertainties.) Lastly, galaxy 0303-0759 is our only galaxy with a bi-conical outflow oriented parallel to the line of sight. Members of these groups are circled in blue, red, violet, and green, respectively, in Figures f8 and f9 . Because \ photons scatter multiple times in the \ medium the geometry of the gas plays a crucial role. Specifically, \ photons will escape toward directions with the minimum \ column density. In the case of bi-conical geometry, photons will encounter the lowest \ column density in the direction perpendicular to the outflow. The orientation of the outflow with respect to the line of sight, then, will determine the visibility of \ photons by an observer. This seems to be confirmed with the data: galaxies 1133+6514 and 1219+1526 , with outflows oriented perpendicular to the line of sight, have the first and third largest values of the escape fraction out of our 10 Green Pea galaxies, while galaxy 0303-0759 , our only galaxy with a bi-conical outflow oriented along the line of sight, has the lowest \ with respect to all galaxies.

Given their outflow geometries, we predict galaxies 1133+6514 and 1219+1526 (and possibly 0926+4427 ) to be LyC emitters. Recall that the outflow geometries of these galaxies leave a portion of the galaxy uncovered with respect to the line of sight. Moreover, the Si II lines of these galaxies have weak absorption equivalent widths and low values of the kinematic quantity, v_ 90 /v_ cen , where v_ 90 is the velocity at which equivalent width reaches 90 of the continuum, and v_ cen is the velocity at 50 the equivalent width. These characteristics were suggested by as a proxy for LyC leakage. 0926+4427 has a bi-conical outflow observed edge-on and may be capable of leaking LyC radiation in the direction of the observer, but our predictions suggest it won't be as strong of an emitter as the other two galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper reports GP1133 + 6514 has similar blue- and red-part emission profiles, the strongest blue peak in the sample, and residual near-zero-velocity emission.

quotes.quote: In the other four cases (GP1244 + 0216, GP1133 + 6514, GP1429 + 0643, and GP1219 + 1526), the blue-part and red-part \ emission are very similar. We also noticed that the \ spatial profiles show a relation with the \ velocity profiles -- the objects with weaker blue peak in \ velocity profile (i.e. small flux ratio of blue-part to red-part \ emission), such as GP1137 + 3524, GP0911 + 1831, and GP0926 + 4428, also have broader blue-part spatial profiles. On the other hand, GP1133 + 6514, which has the strongest blue peak in \ velocity profile, seems to show slightly more compact blue-part \ emission than the red-part \ emission.

In four Green Peas (GP1219 + 1526, GP1133 + 6514, GP0926 + 4428, and GP1429 + 0643), the \ velocity profiles show large residual emission at velocity near zero. From their 2D spectra (figure 1), we find that the \ emission at velocity near zero seems to have more extended \ emission than the \ emission at other velocities.

gmw_00009659.name: A2744-YD4
gmw_00009659.overview: A2744-YD4 is a gravitationally lensed galaxy located at a spectroscopic redshift of approximately 7.88 [arXiv:2403.03977, arXiv:2305.04741]. It resides within a highly overdense proto-cluster [arXiv:2403.03977] and contains a detected dust continuum spatially coincident with the galaxy [arXiv:2305.04741].

sources.summary: The paper describes A2744-YD4/YD4 as a lensed z≈7.88 proto-cluster source with optical-line redshift confirmation, stellar mass and SFR estimates, and one of the highest-redshift metallicity-gradient measurements.

quotes.quote: A2744-YD4 is part of the proto-cluster A2744-z7p9OD at z 7.883 located behind the strong lensing cluster Abell 2744. The proto-cluster includes 22 sources at 7 < z < 9 identified through combined deep HST and Spitzer IRAC photometry Laporte2014,Zheng2014,Atek2015,Ishigaki2016 as part of the Hubble Frontier Fields program Lotz2017 . Seven of them were recently spectroscopically confirmed to be at z = 7.88 through JWST NIRSpec MSA spectroscopy Morishita2023 . This makes A2744-z7p9OD the most distant proto-cluster known so far. The system is highly over-dense, with an excess of surface number density from the field average = (n- n )/ n 130 ^ +66 _ 51 Ishigaki2016 . The 3 '' 3'' IFU FOV of our observations includes five sources of the proto-cluster known as the `quintet', namely YD1, YD4, YD6, YD7, and ZD1, as well as a new source identified by and named s1 (Fig. fig:yd4_maps , top-left). We only partially cover the East component of YD7 (named YD7-E), while its emission toward to West lies outside the FOV. The lensing magnification factor of the source in the FOV is 2.0 Morishita2023, Bergamini2023 . YD4, at the centre of our IFU observation, was reported to be at z = 8.38 based on Ly and ALMA \ 158 m and \ 88 m line emission Laporte2017,Laporte2019,Carniani2020 . Recently, measured z = 7.88 for YD4 based on the detection of high-S/N 4959,5007 and emission lines in high-spectral resolution ( R 2700) NIRSpec MSA observations, thus ruling out the previous redshift measurement.

Fig. fig:yd4_maps , bottom panel, illustrates the integrated spectrum (aperture radius = 0.15 '' ) associated with the main component of the 4 system, that is, YD4 itself. The rest-frame optical lines , , and are detected with high S/N ( 6 on the peak). The spectroscopic redshift based on Lyman break and optical lines ( z 7.88) is consistent with that found by and from the NIRSpec R 2700 data. We find a stellar mass of log( M_*/M_ ) 8.7 from the SED fitting and a SFR 2 M_ yr ^ 1 from for YD4 (Table tab:integr_meas ).
In particular, YD4 (at z 8) constitutes the highest- z source in which a metallicity gradient has been probed so far.

sources.summary: The paper confirms YD4 as a z=7.8753 member galaxy, detects dust continuum spatially coincident with it, and derives infrared luminosity and dust mass.

quotes.quote: In addition to the three known member galaxies, A2744-YD1, YD4, and the western component of YD7 (YD7-W), we serendipitously identified a new galaxy, A2744-s1, that was not recognized as a z 8 candidate in the previous works based on the HST and Spitzer data.

We obtained the spectroscopic redshifts ranging from 7.8733 to 7.8790 , corresponding to the maximum velocity offset of 152 10 km s ^ 1 . The line FWHM values are measured to be 118-183 km s ^ 1 after correction by the instrumental broadening (FWHM _ inst = 100 km s ^ 1 ; Jakobsen2022 ).

The dust continuum contours show a spatial coincidence in A2744-YD1, YD4, and YD7, within positional uncertainties, at a significance level >3 . The continuum flux densities are 83 34 , 60 25 , and 53 25 Jy in A2744-YD1, YD4, and YD7, respectively, without lensing magnification correction. In A2744-ZD1 and s1, we place a 3 upper limit on the flux density to be 20 Jy. We estimate the total infrared luminosity, L_ IR , by integrating the modified black-body radiation over 8-1000 . Because dust temperature and emissivity index values cannot be constrained with the current data, we assume a dust temperature of T_ d = 50 K and an emissivity index of _ d =1.5 (e.g., Bakx2021 ). We obtain L_ IR ( 1.4 0.5 ), ( 1.0 0.4 ), and ( 0.8 0.4 ) 10^ 11 \ for A2744-YD1, YD4, and YD7, respectively, after correcting for the cosmic microwave background effects ( da_Cunha2013 ). The dust mass is estimated to be M_ d = ( 2.7 1.1 ), ( 2.0 0.8 ), and ( 1.8 0.8 ) 10^ 6 , for A2744-YD1, YD4, and YD7, respectively, with a dust mass absorption coefficient = _ 0 ( / _ 0 )^ _ d , where _ 0 = 10 cm ^ 2 g ^ 1 at 250 \ ( hildebrand1983 ).

sources.summary: The paper treats A2744-ZD1 as a faint z=7.88 quintet candidate, reporting non-detections in spectra and dust continuum plus SED fitting assumptions.

quotes.quote: Among these, particularly interesting is the ``quintet'' galaxies at z 8 (YD1, YD4, YD6, YD7, and ZD1) in a distance of 3 3 , as shown in the left panel of Figure fig:summary .

A2744-ZD1 was not identified in our spectra, which indicates its faint nature in . Table tab:SEDresults summarizes the line properties. We measured the spectroscopic redshift, line FWHM, and flux by applying a Gaussian fit to the \ 5008 \ lines. To obtain uncertainties, we first measured the noise level per spectral bin at = 4.2-4.4 and 4.6-4.8 , and adopted its standard deviation as the 1 value. The 1 value was then considered in the Gaussian fitting. We obtained the spectroscopic redshifts ranging from 7.8733 to 7.8790 , corresponding to the maximum velocity offset of 152 10 km s ^ 1 . The line FWHM values are measured to be 118-183 km s ^ 1 after correction by the instrumental broadening (FWHM _ inst = 100 km s ^ 1 ; Jakobsen2022 ). In A2744-ZD1, we place a 3 upper limit on the \ 5008 \ flux based on a spectrum extracted from a circular aperture with a radius of 0 2 . In this calculation, we assumed the z=7.88 and line FWHM of 200 km s ^ 1 .

In A2744-ZD1 and s1, we place a 3 upper limit on the flux density to be 20 Jy. We estimate the total infrared luminosity, L_ IR , by integrating the modified black-body radiation over 8-1000 . Because dust temperature and emissivity index values cannot be constrained with the current data, we assume a dust temperature of T_ d = 50 K and an emissivity index of _ d =1.5 (e.g., Bakx2021 ). We obtain L_ IR ( 1.4 0.5 ), ( 1.0 0.4 ), and ( 0.8 0.4 ) 10^ 11 \ for A2744-YD1, YD4, and YD7, respectively, after correcting for the cosmic microwave background effects ( da_Cunha2013 ). The dust mass is estimated to be M_ d = ( 2.7 1.1 ), ( 2.0 0.8 ), and ( 1.8 0.8 ) 10^ 6 , for A2744-YD1, YD4, and YD7, respectively, with a dust mass absorption coefficient = _ 0 ( / _ 0 )^ _ d , where _ 0 = 10 cm ^ 2 g ^ 1 at 250 \ ( hildebrand1983 ). Note that these physical quantities are corrected for the magnification factor of 2. To obtain physical properties of the galaxies, we performed SED fitting using photometry obtained from JWST and ALMA observations. We also use the ancillary HST data from the UNCOVER project ( Weaver2023 ) that include six filters, F435W, F606W, F814W, F125W, F140W, and F160W. Details of our methods are presented in Fudamoto2023 , and in the following we summarize essential items. We used the publicly available SED fitting code BAGPIPES Carnall2018 assuming delayed-tau star formation history (SFH). We adopt a dust attenuation law from Calzetti00 . For A2744-ZD1, we assume the redshift of z=7.88 .

gmw_00009660.name: COSMOS-24108
gmw_00009660.overview: COSMOS-24108 is a high-redshift galaxy system at z ~ 6.36 that contains multiple rest-frame ultraviolet continuum components [arXiv:2403.03977]. Its ionized gas emission is more extended than the continuum, revealing additional bright clumps and metallicity variations of up to 0.5 to 0.6 dex across the system [arXiv:2403.03977].

sources.summary: The paper presents COSMOS-24108 as a z=6.36 system with multiple continuum components, extended ionized gas clumps, and 0.5-0.6 dex metallicity differences among its sub-sources.

quotes.quote: COSMOS-24108 ( z 6.36; SFR _ UV 29 M_ /yr, SFR _ dust 6.2 M_ /yr) shows two, or possibly three, spatial components in H-band HST rest-frame UV imaging Pentericci2016 . In Fig. fig:cosmos_maps , left panel, we show the 1--2 m observed continuum ( 0.14--0.27 m rest-frame) from the NIRSpec IFU data. This shows two main spatial components, and a third, fainter one in the SE part of the system, consistent with those seen with HST in a similar spectral band.

The ionised gas line emission (Fig. fig:cosmos_maps , central panel; , , and maps are reported in Fig. fig:cosmos_maps_appdx ) is much more extended than the continuum, revealing two additional bright clumps to the SE of the system, one of the two also tentatively detected in continuum (see contours). We label these as [ OIII ] Ea and [ OIII ] Eb.

The metallicity exhibits differences of up to 0.5--0.6 dex among the different sources in the system. The northernmost of the two main continuum sources, b, appears to have lower metallicity (12 + (O/H) 8.0--8.1) than the southernmost one, a ( 8.2), while the SE minor continuum component, , has larger metallicity ( 8.4). Towards the two ionised gas clumps to the SE, [ OIII ] Ea and [ OIII ] Eb, the metallicity is the lowest, with values of 7.8--7.9.

gmw_00009661.name: SDSS J134736.39+173404.6
gmw_00009661.overview: SDSS J134736.39+173404.6 is a Seyfert 2 galaxy [arXiv:1803.10976, arXiv:1901.05419] that hosts a radio-loud active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1803.10976]. The galaxy is spatially coincident with a pair of galaxies [arXiv:1901.05419] and contains a central black hole with a low mass accretion rate [arXiv:1803.10976].

sources.summary: The paper identifies the X-ray source with the Seyfert 2 galaxy SDSS J134736.39+173404.6 and models its radio-loud AGN, black-hole mass, accretion rate, and QPO behavior.

quotes.quote: Using a Chandra observation of 2008, we evaluate more accurately the position of the X-ray source and show that the new source coordinates coincide with the position of the Seyfert 2 galaxy. We provide a detailed spectral energy distribution of the AGN counterpart using multi-wavelength observations. The AGN is radio-loud and the broadband SED modelling indicates a black hole with a mass of 9.8 10^ 6 M_ , that accretes at an Eddington ratio of 0.047.

It is now clear from Fig. fig_optic that the X-ray source is associated with the Seyfert 2 galaxy, also known as SDSS J134736.39+173404.6, and can't be attributed to a foreground X-ray binary as suggested by .

This is consistent with the source being a Seyfert 2 galaxy. The best-fit model has a black hole mass of 9.8 10^ 6 M_ , and an Eddington ratio of 0.047, so it is a low mass accretion rate AGN. The soft X-ray Comptonisation component has an electron temperature of 0.26 keV and optical depth of 17.3.

sources.summary: The paper associates the QPO X-ray source with the Seyfert 2 galaxy SDSS J134736.39+173404.6 and uses its twin QPOs to constrain an AGN black-hole mass.

quotes.quote: carried out a systematic study of Chandra, Swift, and XMM-Newton observations of the X-ray source , which has been previously found to be spatially coincident with a pair of galaxies including the Seyfert 2 galaxy SDSS J134736.39+173404.6. Using the Chandra observation from 2008, accurately evaluated the position of this X-ray source and showed that its coordinates coincide with the position of the Seyfert 2 galaxy. Within the analysis of a set of 29 Swift observations conducted between February 6 and May 23, 2008, they discovered strong twin-peak quasi-periodic oscillations with periods of 23.82 0.07 h and 71.44 0.57 h. This measurement clearly indicates that R=3.00 0.04 , and it provides very strong evidence for frequency-commensurable QPOs in an AGN BH. The frequency commensurability is crucial for the nonlinear resonance models discussed by Abramowicz, Kluzniak and collaborators e.g., abr-klu:2001,reb:2004:PASJ:,hor:2008:AA:,tor-etal:2005 . Motivated by the observed 3:1 frequency ratio, investigated the mass-spin relations expected for this source in detail from various resonances between disk-oscillation modes that have previously been discussed in the context of QPOs in Galactic microquasars.

gmw_00009662.name: NGC 5582
gmw_00009662.overview: NGC 5582 is a gas-rich early-type galaxy characterized by a neutral hydrogen ring or disk structure [arXiv:2001.08087] and a CO-poor interstellar medium [arXiv:2001.08087]. Its velocity field displays regular rotating features [arXiv:2401.17553], with extended arms located beyond ten effective radii [arXiv:2401.17553].

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 5582 among six gas-rich ETGs with enormous H I ring or disc structures, red dusty inner regions, blue outer H I rings, and is described with the group as CO-poor.

quotes.quote: We note that six rich galaxies (NGC 2859, NGC 3941, NGC 3945, NGC 4036, NGC 4278, and NGC 5582) in our sample have enormous \ ring or disc structures, while they have no \ in their very central regions (red circles in Fig. fig:derivedH ). While these galaxies show very red colours in the inner regions due to dust, they show blue colours in the outer regions where the highest \ column density rings are located 2017MNRAS.464..329Y . We would like to point out that these galaxies are also CO-poor with the exception of NGC 4278. Therefore, we conclude that the presence of a large \ disc or ring in an ETG indicates a dust structure in the centre, even if there is no \ in central regions. It would be interesting to continue to study these objects in future studies.

sources.summary: The paper includes NGC 5582 among H I galaxies with regular rotation, finding lower dark-matter fractions with H I data and masking extended arms beyond 10 Re.

quotes.quote: However, only a subsample of four galaxies shows regular rotating features in their velocity fields in deeper observations with the WSRT: NGC 2685, NGC 4203, NGC 5582 and NGC 6798 (see Appendix sec:HI-fields for these velocity maps).

Among the four galaxies in the subsample, the dark matter fraction of NGC 2685 remains unchanged throughout all radii when include kinematics, while the dark matter fraction of NGC 4203 increases, and the dark matter fractions of NGC 5582 and NGC 6798 decrease, especially at f_ DM (5R_ e ) . There are extended arms in the velocity fields of NGC 4203 and NGC 5582, which are located outside 10 R_ e of each galaxy.

gmw_00009663.name: B0707 359
gmw_00009663.overview: B0707 359 is a giant radio galaxy that exhibits restarted jet activity accompanied by a change in its ejection axis [arXiv:1308.5054]. This interruption of the central engine beams created a relic double radio source, while the new jets show asymmetric orientation [arXiv:1308.5054].

sources.summary: The paper presents B0707 359 as a giant radio galaxy whose radio morphology indicates restarted jet activity, an axis change, asymmetric orientation, fast hotspot advance, and relic double-source structure.

quotes.quote: We present radio observations of the giant radio galaxy B0707 359 whose structure offers an interesting case study of an AGN that may be exhibiting not only inconstancy in AGN output but also inconstancy in direction of ejection axis. Its radio morphology shows evidence for a restarting of the jets accompanied by an axis change. The observed side-to-side asymmetries of this giant radio galaxy suggest that the new jets are not in the plane of the sky. We infer that the hotspot advance velocities are unusually large and of magnitude a few-tenths of the speed of light. The dual-frequency radio images are consistent with a model where the beams from the central engine ceased, creating a relic double radio source; this interruption was accompanied by triggering of a movement of the axis of the central engine at a rate of a few degrees Myr ^ 1 .

gmw_00009664.name: Virgo I
gmw_00009664.overview: Virgo I is an ultra-faint dwarf satellite galaxy of the Milky Way located approximately 87 kpc away [arXiv:1609.04346]. It is characterized by an extended spatial distribution with a half-light radius of 38 pc and an extremely low absolute magnitude of -0.8 [arXiv:1609.04346].

sources.summary: The paper reports Virgo I as an ultra-faint Milky Way dwarf satellite, identified as a significant old stellar overdensity with an extended 38 pc half-light radius and very low luminosity.

quotes.quote: This new satellite, Virgo I, which is located in the constellation of Virgo, has been identified as a statistically significant (5.5 ) spatial overdensity of star-like objects with a well-defined main sequence and red giant branch in their color-magnitude diagram. The significance of this overdensity increases to 10.8 when the relevant isochrone filter is adopted for the search. Based on the distribution of the stars around the likely main sequence turn-off at 24 mag, the distance to Virgo I is estimated as 87 kpc, and its most likely absolute magnitude calculated from a Monte Carlo analysis is M_V = -0.8 0.9 mag. This stellar system has an extended spatial distribution with a half-light radius of 38 ^ +12 _ 11 pc, which clearly distinguishes it from a globular cluster with comparable luminosity. Thus, Virgo I is one of the faintest dwarf satellites known and is located beyond the reach of the Sloan Digital Sky Survey.

gmw_00009665.name: J0304+7727
gmw_00009665.overview: J0304+7727 is a radio galaxy candidate with a spectral peak that likely occurs near 1 GHz [arXiv:1807.05441]. High-resolution imaging of the source at 8.6 GHz resolves a pair of compact components separated by 10 milliarcseconds [arXiv:1807.05441].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J0304+7727 as an EISERS candidate whose sparse radio fluxes allow a near-1 GHz peak and whose VLBA image resolves two compact components.

quotes.quote: For only one source, J0304+7727, the available flux density measurements might not encompass the turnover frequency, though the data in the plot can still be reconciled with the spectral peak occurring near 1 GHz (Fig. fig:spec_all_undetected ).

J0304+7727 The VLBA image at 8.6 GHz shows this source to consist of a pair of compact components separated by 10 mas Sokolovsky2011 . The 3 available spectral measurements fall short of pinning down the turnover frequency, but it is unlikely to be below 0.5 GHz (Fig. fig:spec_all_undetected ).

gmw_00009666.name: J0754+5324
gmw_00009666.overview: J0754+5324 is a radio galaxy with a classical gigahertz-peaked spectrum shape that suggests a compact symmetric object morphology [arXiv:1807.05441]. High-resolution imaging reveals a structure dominated by two hot spots and a jet containing a moving knot [arXiv:1807.05441].

sources.summary: The paper describes J0754+5324 as a GPS-shaped compact symmetric-object candidate with milliarcsecond VLBA structure, hot spots, and a moving jet knot.

quotes.quote: J0754+5324 The classical GPS shape of its radio spectrum (Fig fig:spec_all_detected ) hints at a compact symmetric object (CSO) type morphology Phillips1982, Conway1994, Wilkinson1994 . Indeed, this appears consistent with the 5 GHz VLBA image which shows an overall size of 9 mas Helmboldt2007 . An earlier VLBI image at 8.4 GHz showed the source to be dominated by two hot spots with a strong jet pointing toward the northernmost lobe Peck2000 . The core appears to be a knot in the jet, speeding toward the northwestern hot spot at 0.060 0.026 mas per year Gugliucci2005 .

gmw_00009667.name: J0847+5723
gmw_00009667.overview: J0847+5723 is a radio galaxy with a gigahertz-peaked spectrum [arXiv:1807.05441]. Very Long Baseline Interferometry observations at 5 GHz reveal a triple morphology consistent with a compact symmetric object [arXiv:1807.05441].

sources.summary: The paper reports that J0847+5723 has VLBI triple morphology consistent with a compact symmetric object and a GPS spectrum, plus high-frequency detections implying a compact transparent component.

quotes.quote: J0847+5723 The VLBI image at 5 GHz shows a triple source, consistent with a CSO morphology Xu1995 . Such an interpretation would be in accord with its GPS type spectrum (Fig. fig:spec_all_detected ). The source is also detected at 15 GHz (66.9 6.7 mJy Taylor2005 ) and at 30 GHz (43 04 mJy Lowe2007 ), suggesting a compact component becoming transparent at these frequencies (Fig. fig:spec_all_detected ).

gmw_00009668.name: J1326+5712
gmw_00009668.overview: J1326+5712 is a radio galaxy [arXiv:1807.05441] that exhibits a complex morphology with an overall size of 4.2 milliarcseconds at 5 GHz [arXiv:1807.05441]. The source is unpolarized at 10.5 GHz, and its radio spectrum shows an upturn around 8 GHz that suggests a highly compact high-frequency component [arXiv:1807.05441].

sources.summary: The paper describes J1326+5712 as a complex 5 GHz VLBA source with no 10.5 GHz polarization and a spectral upturn implying a very compact high-frequency component.

quotes.quote: J1326+5712 The 5 GHz VLBA image of this source made under the VIP survey Helmboldt2007 shows a complex morphology with an overall size of 4.2 mas. The source was found to be unpolarised in the 10.5 GHz observations with the Effelsberg telescope Pasetto2016 . The spectrum shows an upturn around 8 GHz (Fig. fig:spec_all_undetected ), which implies the existence of an even more compact component whose spectrum peaks at frequency > 20GHz Snellen1995 .

gmw_00009669.name: J1430+3649
gmw_00009669.overview: The radio galaxy J1430+3649 exhibits a deceptively simple milliarcsecond radio morphology [arXiv:1807.05441]. However, its complex radio spectrum reveals a second, more compact component that dominates above a few gigahertz [arXiv:1807.05441].

sources.summary: The paper says J1430+3649 has a simple milliarcsecond radio morphology but a complex spectrum with a compact component dominating above a few GHz.

quotes.quote: For a few of the sources, namely, J0847+5723, J1326+5712 and J1430+3649, and, possibly, J1549+5038, the available radio spectrum provides evidence for a second, more compact component which becomes transparent (and often, even dominant) above a few GHz (Figs fig:spec_all_detected fig:spec_all_undetected ; see below).

J1430+3649 The 5 GHz VLBA image of this source made under the VIP survey Helmboldt2007 shows it to be single source, with a mean radius of 1.3 mas. However, this deceptively simple radio morphology masks the complexity of its radio spectrum which shows a second even more component which dominates above 2-3 GHz and remains opaque at least upto 15 GHz (Fig. fig:spec_all_detected ).

gmw_00009670.name: J1700+3830
gmw_00009670.overview: J1700+3830 is a radio galaxy displaying a core-jet structure with an eastern jet that potentially terminates in a lobe [arXiv:1807.05441]. The source exhibits a classical gigahertz-peaked spectrum peaking near 0.5 GHz and is the sole object in its sample that may have undergone flux variation near this spectral peak [arXiv:1807.05441].

sources.summary: The paper describes J1700+3830 as a core-jet EISERS candidate with a possible terminal lobe, a GPS spectrum peaking near 0.5 GHz, and possible peak variability.

quotes.quote: J1700+3830 The 5 GHz VLBA image of this source, made under the VIP survey Helmboldt2007 , shows it to be a core with a jet towards the east, which possibly terminates in a lobe 50 mas away from the core. It has a classical GPS spectrum which peaks near 0.5 GHz (Fig. fig:spec_all_detected ).
We note that J1700+3830 is the only source where a significant flux variation (near the spectral peak) may have occurred.

gmw_00009671.name: id85
gmw_00009671.overview: The high-redshift galaxy id85 is a Lyman-break galaxy with a star formation rate of 3.1 solar masses per year based on its ultraviolet luminosity [arXiv:1904.07254]. It features an extended Lyman-alpha halo that reaches far beyond its ultraviolet continuum [arXiv:1904.07254].

sources.summary: The paper identifies galaxy id85 as the brightest J1220+0921 detection, with an extended Ly-alpha halo, high impact parameter, velocity offset, UV-based star formation, and LBG-like spectrum.

quotes.quote: Galaxy id85 is the brightest detection both in \ and the band. Fig. fig:lya_SB shows the \ halo of id85 extends far beyond the UV continuum. This object has an impact parameter of 278.2 kpc (36.4 arcsec), it is offset from the absorption system by +370 20 km s ^ 1 , and is forming stars at a rate of 3.1 0.6 yr ^ 1 based on the UV luminosity. The rest-frame UV spectrum of this object is consistent with an LBG with z_ LBG z_ DLA =3.309 (see Fig. fig:LBG_spec lower panel), the Lyman break is convincingly detected but the spectrum is too noisy to detect the interstellar lines.

gmw_00009672.name: SDSS J002444.11-003221.4
gmw_00009672.overview: SDSS J002444.11-003221.4 is a quasar that exhibits velocity-shift features in its ratio spectra [arXiv:1306.4987]. It is identified as a candidate sub-parsec supermassive black hole binary system [arXiv:1306.4987].

sources.summary: The paper treats SDSS J002444.11-003221.4 as a candidate sub-parsec SMBH binary quasar, showing ratio-spectrum velocity-shift features and retaining it among the three most reliable cross-correlation candidates.

quotes.quote: Since the velocity shifts in the broad lines are small compared with the linewidths, in Fig. fig:ratio we plot the ratio of the spectra at the two epochs for each candidate, at wavelengths around and H (if present). These ratio spectra nicely highlight the velocity shifts via ``bumps" or ``valleys" around the line peaks. We clearly see such features in the ratio spectra of SDSS J032223.02+000803.5, SDSS J002444.11-003221.4, SDSS J095656.42+535023.2, SDSS J161609.50+434146.8 and SDSS J093502.54+433110.7.

As a sanity check, we did another set of cross correlations for the seven candidates over [ 1700 , 3800 ] to exclude Ca H+K absorption lines. Only three of the seven candidates (SDSS J002444.11-003221.4, SDSS J095656.42+535023.2, SDSS J004918.98+002609.4) survive this test. These are our three most reliable candidates.

gmw_00009673.name: SDSS J095656.42+535023.2
gmw_00009673.overview: SDSS J095656.42+535023.2 is a quasar that exhibits velocity-shift features in its ratio spectra [arXiv:1306.4987]. It is identified as a candidate sub-parsec supermassive black hole binary system [arXiv:1306.4987].

sources.summary: The paper treats SDSS J095656.42+535023.2 as a candidate sub-parsec SMBH binary quasar, showing ratio-spectrum velocity-shift features and retaining it among the three most reliable cross-correlation candidates.

quotes.quote: Since the velocity shifts in the broad lines are small compared with the linewidths, in Fig. fig:ratio we plot the ratio of the spectra at the two epochs for each candidate, at wavelengths around and H (if present). These ratio spectra nicely highlight the velocity shifts via ``bumps" or ``valleys" around the line peaks. We clearly see such features in the ratio spectra of SDSS J032223.02+000803.5, SDSS J002444.11-003221.4, SDSS J095656.42+535023.2, SDSS J161609.50+434146.8 and SDSS J093502.54+433110.7.

As a sanity check, we did another set of cross correlations for the seven candidates over [ 1700 , 3800 ] to exclude Ca H+K absorption lines. Only three of the seven candidates (SDSS J002444.11-003221.4, SDSS J095656.42+535023.2, SDSS J004918.98+002609.4) survive this test. These are our three most reliable candidates.

gmw_00009674.name: SPRC-42
gmw_00009674.overview: SPRC-42 is a polar-ring galaxy with a polar structure that is brighter than its host galaxy [arXiv:1302.7273]. The galaxy exhibits a mild offset in its Tully-Fisher relation, and its detected molecular gas mass may represent only a lower limit [arXiv:1302.7273].

sources.summary: The paper identifies SPRC-42 as a detected polar-ring galaxy whose CO beam may miss some gas, whose Tully-Fisher location is mildly offset, and whose polar structure is brighter than its host.

quotes.quote: For the CO(1-0) beam, two detected galaxies, SPRC-42 and SPRC-47, are particularly in this case, where the H _2 mass derived in Table tab:det might only be a lower limit.

In Fig. fig:TF , one of the PRGs falls in the expected range for a spherical potential (SPRC-14), two are mildly offset (SPRC-69 and SPRC-42), and two are very far from the usual relation, with very broad velocities (SPRC-48 and SPRC-47). This large scatter might indicate different formation mechanisms for the systems, and at least four out of the five should have their dark matter halo aligned with the polar plane. The position of the five PRGs in the TF diagram does not appear related to the mass ratio between the polar disk/ring and the host. The galaxies SPRC-42 and SPRC-69 have polar structures brighter than their hosts, while SPRC-14 and SPRC-48 are comparable, and SPRC-47 is weaker than its host.

gmw_00009675.name: SPRC-48
gmw_00009675.overview: SPRC-48 is a polar-ring galaxy that exhibits a double-horn profile, which indicates the presence of a rotating disk or ring [arXiv:1302.7273]. The galaxy features a polar structure of comparable brightness to its host and is inferred to possess a highly flattened dark-matter system [arXiv:1302.7273].

sources.summary: The paper describes SPRC-48 as a detected polar-ring galaxy with a double-horn profile, an extreme Tully-Fisher offset, host-comparable polar structure, and evidence for a very flattened dark-matter system.

quotes.quote: Two of the PRGs, P47 and P48, clearly show double-horn profiles, indicative of rotating disks or rings.

In Fig. fig:TF , one of the PRGs falls in the expected range for a spherical potential (SPRC-14), two are mildly offset (SPRC-69 and SPRC-42), and two are very far from the usual relation, with very broad velocities (SPRC-48 and SPRC-47). This large scatter might indicate different formation mechanisms for the systems, and at least four out of the five should have their dark matter halo aligned with the polar plane. The position of the five PRGs in the TF diagram does not appear related to the mass ratio between the polar disk/ring and the host. The galaxies SPRC-42 and SPRC-69 have polar structures brighter than their hosts, while SPRC-14 and SPRC-48 are comparable, and SPRC-47 is weaker than its host.
There is no obvious relation with the PR spatial extent either. The two most extreme cases (SPRC-47 and SPRC-48) must have a very flattened dark-matter system, as flat as a disk.

gmw_00009676.name: IRAS 13305-1739
gmw_00009676.overview: IRAS 13305-1739 is an ultraluminous infrared galaxy hosting a Seyfert 2 nucleus [arXiv:2402.10786]. The galaxy exhibits slight optical isophotal distortion and is characterized by a single power-law radio spectral energy distribution without a double-lobed radio morphology [arXiv:2402.10786].

sources.summary: The paper describes IRAS 13305-1739 as a Seyfert 2 ULIRG with slight optical isophotal distortion, no double-lobed radio morphology, and a single power-law radio SED.

quotes.quote: IRAS 13305-1739: classified as a Seyfert 2 nucleus with a slight isophotal distortion in optical image Veilleux02 , this galaxy does not show a double-lobed radio morphology Nandi21 . The SYNAGE modeling of this galaxy indicates a turnover frequency at 1.7 GHz Nandi21 which is not corroborated with our analysis where it is best fit with a single PL model. This is most likely due to the inclusion of low frequency (down to 70 MHz from GLEAM) data in the SED modeling by us.

gmw_00009677.name: IRAS 14394+5332
gmw_00009677.overview: IRAS 14394+5332 is an interacting system [arXiv:1805.00514] classified as a triplet [arXiv:2402.10786] or a pair connected by a tidal bridge [arXiv:1805.00514, arXiv:1908.03104]. Its eastern component contains a Seyfert 2 nucleus [arXiv:1805.00514, arXiv:2402.10786] hosting an obscured active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2402.10786] and a compact, high-ionization warm outflow [arXiv:1908.03104].

sources.summary: The paper describes IRAS F14394+5332 as an interacting pair with a Seyfert 2 eastern component, complex morphology, extreme [OIII] kinematics, and a compact uncertain outflow region.

quotes.quote: IRAS F14394+5332 Figure 14394all shows a wide field-of-view image of the continuum emission from the two interacting galaxies that form this system. The nuclear separation between the two galaxies is 28 arcsec ( 56 kpc), and they are connected by a bridge of faint, diffuse emission. The western component shows a spiral morphology, and no [ OIII ] emission is detected from this source. Therefore, it is not shown in Figure HST_frames . A priori, the morphology of the continuum emission from the eastern component in our HST images is consistent with that described in in which the eastern component is a double nucleus galaxy in itself, with the brighter nucleus (labelled E) separated from a fainter nucleus (labelled EE) 2.0 arcsec (3.7 kpc) to the NE along PA67.

Spectroscopically, the E nucleus is classified as a Seyfert 2 galaxy at optical wavelengths veilleux99,yuan10 . RZ13 carried out a spectroscopic study of the nuclear outflows in this nucleus and found that it shows the most spectacular [ OIII ] emission line kinematics of all the objects considered in their study (see their Figure 1). In order to fit the [ OIII ] emission line profile, three narrow (FWHM < 450 km/s) and two broad (FWHM 1300 km/s) components with extreme velocity shifts of up to 1500 km/s were required.

sources.summary: The paper resolves the sub-kpc warm outflow in F14394+5332E, describes the interacting ULIRG system and hidden type II AGN, and finds a compact disturbed outflow in a calmer halo.

quotes.quote: As part of a project to study the AGN outflows in some of the most rapidly evolving galaxies in the local Universe, here we present HST/STIS observations of F14394+5332E that resolve the sub-kpc warm outflow for the first time in a ULIRG. The observations reveal a compact, high ionization outflow region ( r_ max 0.9 kpc) set in a more extensive ( r_ max 1.4 kpc) halo that is kinematically quiescent and has a lower ionization state. A large line width ( 600 < FWHM < 1500 km s ^ 1 ) is measured throughout the outflow region, and the outflowing gas shows a steep velocity gradient with radius, with the magnitude of the blueshifted velocities increasing from 500 to 1800 km s ^ 1 from the inner to the outer part of the outflow.

The F14394+5332 system has a complex structure on a large scale, which includes a spiral galaxy to the west that is connected by a tidal bridge to a more disturbed galaxy 52 kpc to the east kim02,tadhunter18 . Based on near-IR imaging kim02 , this eastern galaxy has two nuclei -- labelled F14394+5332E and F14394+5332EE -- separated by 1.9 arcsec ( 3.5 kpc), but the [ OIII ] emission and AGN are associated with the most westerly of these two nuclei (F14394+5332E). Although the continuum structure of F14394+5332E is complex at optical wavelengths, there are two main continuum condensations bisected by a dust lane, which also bisects the [ OIII ] structure.

Overall, the results are consistent with the those of our previous ground-based spectroscopy and HST imaging studies of F14394+5332E, in the sense that they confirm the presence of a relatively compact outflow region to the north of the nucleus. This outflow is set in a more extensive and kinematically quiescent halo that is only detected in H + [ NII ] in individual rows of the STIS spectra.

sources.summary: The paper describes IRAS 14394+5332 as a triplet with a Seyfert 2 eastern component, close nuclei, a long tidal tail, obscured AGN, and two-component radio SED.

quotes.quote: IRAS 14394+5332: classified as a triplet with an east component having a Seyfert 2 nucleus and two close nuclei at a separation 2.6 kpc and connected to a west component with the long tidal tail of 35 kpc Veilleux02 . The VLA observations conducted by Crawford96 indicate the presence of an obscured AGN contributing the most to the radio continuum in the east component. Its radio SED is best modeled with a two-component model characterized by different nonthermal electron populations and turnovers (C2). The SYNAGE modeling of its radio SED from Nandi21 fits a power-law.

gmw_00009678.name: IRAS 23389+0300
gmw_00009678.overview: IRAS 23389+0300 is a pre-merger system containing a Seyfert 2 nucleus [arXiv:2402.10786]. The northern galaxy of this system hosts a compact, double-lobed gigahertz-peaked spectrum radio active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2402.10786].

sources.summary: The paper describes IRAS 23389+0300 as a Seyfert 2 pre-merger where the northern galaxy hosts a compact double-lobed GPS radio AGN with radio-loud CIGALE support.

quotes.quote: IRAS 23389+0300: classified as a Seyfert 2 nucleus and a pre-merger system Rodriguez-Zaurin09 , the northern galaxy hosts a double-lobed radio AGN of GPS type with a linear size of 830 pc Nagar03 . Its radio SED is best-modeled by the single-component model (C) with a turnover at 0.32 GHz. The SYNAGE modeling by Nandi21 reveals a spectral turnover at 177 MHz, while our analysis recovers it at 320 MHz. This could be due to a better sampled SED used in our analysis. CIGALE estimated radio-loudness is 800, which supports the view that this could be a young radio AGN of GPS type Nandi21 .

gmw_00009679.name: c4
gmw_00009679.overview: The active galactic nucleus c4 is classified as a Fanaroff-Riley II LINER radio galaxy [arXiv:0711.4773]. Its X-ray spectrum exhibits thermal residuals and a possible line-like feature around 6 to 7 keV, but the galaxy shows no variability on short timescales [arXiv:0711.4773].

sources.summary: The paper treats c4 as an FRII LINER radio galaxy whose X-ray spectrum shows thermal residuals, an apparent 6-7 keV feature, and no significant short-timescale variability.

quotes.quote: Two ( \ and ) are FRI and two (3C 88 and c4) FRII galaxies, although the classification of 3C 88 is only morphological.

Large residuals at soft energies are present in , 3C 88, and c4, suggesting the presence of additional (thermal) components. The residuals of c4 \ also suggest the possible presence of a line-like feature around 6--7 keV.
\ is only marginally variable in the soft band, whereas c4 \ does not show significant variability on short timescales.

gmw_00009680.name: PG 2233+134
gmw_00009680.overview: PG 2233+134 is a super-Eddington active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1610.00429] that exhibits the highest bolometric luminosity in its sample [arXiv:1610.00429]. Structural modeling of its host galaxy favors a low-luminosity disk profile [arXiv:astro-ph/0002199].

sources.summary: The paper describes PG 2233+134 as a super-Eddington AGN with the highest bolometric luminosity in the pair and a covering factor that rises in the adjusted fit.

quotes.quote: Super-Eddington sources There are two sources in our sample, KUG 1031+398 (No. 15) and PG 2233+134 (No. 50), which have super-Eddington accretion rates. Among these KUG 1031+398 has the highest value of _ Edd ( 2.64) and lowest black hole mass, whereas PG 2233+134 has the highest value of bolometric luminosity. In these sources, we attempt to fit the data by fixing _ Edd to 1 and letting M _ BH be a free parameter.

In the case of PG 2233+134, M _ BH remained unchanged while ^2 (/dof) increased from 893.6(/173) to 2641.2(/173). In this case also, f_c shows an increase from 0.11 to 0.22. Although the inclination effects are not taken into account, it seems likely that this AGN is indeed a super-Eddington source.

sources.summary: The paper says earlier studies only bounded or partly resolved 2233+134's host, while its modeling favors a low-luminosity disk host unless an elliptical profile is forced.

quotes.quote: Both Smith \ smith and Veron-Cetty Woltjer veron-cetty included this quasar in their samples, but both failed to resolve the host galaxy beyond obtaining upper limits for the luminosity. Hutchings Neff hutchings92 did resolve the host galaxy and found the host to be best-fit by a =4 model, although they did not resolve further information about the galaxy. However, we find that the most probable host has an disk profile and calculate M_K=-24.1 , the lowest luminosity host modelled. If we constrain the host to have an elliptical profile, the best fit luminosity becomes M_K=-25.9 , although the half light radius is very small for this model ( R_ 1/2 =1.5 kpc) which places it a long way from the K band Fundamental Plane of Pahre, Djorgovski de Carvalho pahre . If the host parameters are constrained to lie on this plane, then rerunning the modelling gives a best fit host with M_K=-24.6 .

gmw_00009681.name: ID 10013704
gmw_00009681.overview: ID 10013704 is a high-redshift galaxy at z=5.919 [arXiv:2308.01230] that hosts a low-mass active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2401.07910]. The system exhibits a possible weak tidal tail indicating a recent merger [arXiv:2308.01230], and it displays a broad H-alpha spectral component suggesting a dual active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2308.01230].

sources.summary: The paper presents ID 10013704 as a z=5.919 JADES galaxy whose H profile, weak tidal tail, and second broad component suggest a merging dual AGN.

quotes.quote: The first (and most distant) one is ID 10013704 at z=5.919, in Deep/HST GOODS-S (Fig. fig:clara_gemma ). In addition to the narrow component, having the same width as [ OIII ] (140 km/s), the medium resolution grating shows a clear, broad component of H with a width of 2400 km/s (Fig. fig:clara_gemma , top-left panel). Not surprisingly, such a broad component is nearly lost in the noise in the high resolution spectrum (Fig. fig:clara_gemma , bottom-left panel).

We suggest that the second broad, redshifted bump in ID 10013704 is tracing the BLR of a second, fainter AGN, probably associated with a secondary BH in the process of merging with the BH in the primary galaxy. We note that morphologically ID 10013704 shows the presence of a possible weak tidal tail (Fig fig:images ), indicating that very likely it went through a recent merger, and that the black hole of the secondary galaxy is now approaching the nucleus of the primary galaxy, while accreting gas and being detectable as a secondary AGN We note that the tidal tail cannot be responsible for the intermediate broad component of H , as it is far too broad even for the tidal tails of merging massive galaxies, hence even more unlikely for the host of ID 100133704, which, as we will see, has a mass of <10^9 M_ .

sources.summary: The paper describes J-10013704 as the faintest JADES radio AGN, with low black-hole flux, difficult ngVLA detection, and host H II emission exceeding some forecasts.

quotes.quote: J-10013704 has the lowest radio flux, about 40 nJy at 0.1 GHz, because it has the smallest BH mass.

Our estimates show that BH radio flux densities for most of the sources can be detected with ngVLA with integration times of 10 - 100 hr except for J-10013704 and J-3608. Radio emission from J-73488 and J-954 can be detected with integration times of only 1 hr at 1 - 10 GHz. On the other hand, SKA would require integration times of 100 hr or more at frequencies > 1 GHz to detect these sources except for J-10013704, J-3608 and J-62309, which require even longer times. J-954 and J-73488, the brightest of these AGNs, can be observed with SKA with 10 - 100 hr integration times. Radio emission from H II regions is below that of BHs in almost all cases with SFRs of up to 10 \ yr ^ 1 except for J-10013704, where it exceeds the most pessimistic of the FPs at 1 - 10 GHz.

BH fluxes for = 0.7 in general are lower in the sensitivity range of ngVLA (1-10 GHz) so longer integration times of 10 -100 hr would be required to observe them depending on the BH mass and redshift except for GN-z11 and J-10013704, which still require more than 100 hr integration times.

gmw_00009682.name: ID 73488
gmw_00009682.overview: ID 73488 is a high-redshift active galactic nucleus located at a redshift of z=4.133 [arXiv:2308.01230, arXiv:2401.07910]. The galaxy exhibits a weak asymmetric morphology that likely indicates a recent merger [arXiv:2308.01230], and it is among the brightest active galactic nuclei in the JADES survey [arXiv:2401.07910].

sources.summary: The paper describes ID 73488 as a z=4.133 JADES galaxy whose H components and weak asymmetric morphology support a dual-BLR, merging-black-hole interpretation.

quotes.quote: A similar case is seen in the spectrum of ID 73488 at z=4.133, in the Medium/JWST tier in GOODS-N, as illustrated in Fig. fig:lara_emma . Also in this case there are a clear narrow (68 km/s) and a very broad ( 2400 km/s) component of H . However, the line profiles, especially in the high resolution spectrum, cannot be explained with only two components and reveal the presence of an intermediate component with a width 460 km/s (the results of the double component fitting are shown in Fig. fig:lara_emma and reported in Tab. tab:measures_dual ).

Also in the case of ID 73488 both broad components are detected at very high significance ( >35 ), as reported in Tab. tab:measures_dual . Introducing the second broad component of H the BIC improves by more than 500 ( BIC_ 1B2B =547 , Tab. tab:measures_dual ).

In terms of morphologies, similarly to ID 10013704, both ID 73488 and ID 53757 show the presence of weak asymmetric features, which are likely remnants of recent mergers, hence fitting in the scenario in which these systems may be hosting dual BHs, in the process of merging.

sources.summary: The paper identifies J-73488 as one of the brightest JADES AGNs, with high predicted radio flux and short ngVLA and moderate SKA integration requirements.

quotes.quote: J-954 and J-73488 have the highest flux densities, up to 10^4 nJy because of their large BH masses, high bolometric luminosities, and greater Eddington ratios.
Radio emission from J-73488 and J-954 can be detected with integration times of only 1 hr at 1 - 10 GHz.
J-954 and J-73488, the brightest of these AGNs, can be observed with SKA with 10 - 100 hr integration times.

gmw_00009683.name: ID 53757
gmw_00009683.overview: ID 53757 is a candidate dual broad-line region galaxy that exhibits a nearly symmetric broad H-alpha emission profile [arXiv:2308.01230]. The galaxy displays weak asymmetric morphological features, which are likely remnants of a recent merger [arXiv:2308.01230].

sources.summary: The paper presents ID 53757 as a candidate dual-BLR galaxy with symmetric broad H emission, two fitted BLR components, and merger-remnant morphology.

quotes.quote: Finally, in Fig. fig:laura_julia we show an additional case of candidate dual BLR in the galaxy ID 53757. Unfortunately, in this case we do not have any grating spectrum of [ OIII ] . However, the intensity of the broad component of H (more than two times the flux of the narrow component) makes it very unlikely that it is associated with outflow, as it would imply more gas mass in the outflow than in the host galaxy. Moreover, the nearly symmetric profile of H (if anything slightly redshifted, as discussed below), argues against the outflow interpretation (which generally requires a blueshift of the high velocity gas).

The result of the fit with two BLRs is shown in Fig. fig:laura_julia and the resulting parameters reported in Tab. tab:measures_dual : the second broad component, with width 700 km/s , is detected at >5 , while the broader component, with FWHM 2800 km/s, is detected at 10 . Introducing the second broad component results into a BIC_ 1B2B =39 . In terms of morphologies, similarly to ID 10013704, both ID 73488 and ID 53757 show the presence of weak asymmetric features, which are likely remnants of recent mergers, hence fitting in the scenario in which these systems may be hosting dual BHs, in the process of merging.

gmw_00009684.name: ID 954
gmw_00009684.overview: ID 954 is an active galactic nucleus located at a redshift of z=6.759 [arXiv:2308.01230]. It is characterized by broad hydrogen emission lines [arXiv:2308.01230] and is predicted to have high radio flux densities [arXiv:2401.07910].

sources.summary: The paper identifies ID 954 as the most luminous AGN in the JADES sample, with broad H and H emission and a likely overestimated stellar mass.

quotes.quote: A broad component of H is only detected in the case of ID 954 at z=6.759, which is the most luminous AGN in our sample.

We will discuss that, when compared with the dynamical masses, in the case of ID 954 the stellar mass is probably overestimated (likely because of this is the most luminous AGN in our sample and therefore it is more difficult to disentangle host galaxy and AGN components), while for ID 11836 the stellar mass might be underestimated (but see discussion in Sect. sec:mbh_mdyn ).

ID 954 is also another extreme case in which the inferred dynamical mass is about one order of magnitude lower than the stellar mass. This is the most luminous AGN in our sample, and it is likely that the spectral decomposition struggled to disentangle the stellar component. The only exception is ID 954 (the most luminous AGN in the sample) for which the BLR is seen also for H . h In this appendix we perform a closer analysis of the [ OIII ] profile of ID 10013704.

sources.summary: The paper identifies J-954 as one of the brightest JADES AGNs in predicted radio flux, detectable rapidly by ngVLA and in 10-100 hours by SKA.

quotes.quote: J-954 and J-73488 have the highest flux densities, up to 10^4 nJy because of their large BH masses, high bolometric luminosities, and greater Eddington ratios.
Radio emission from J-73488 and J-954 can be detected with integration times of only 1 hr at 1 - 10 GHz.
J-954 and J-73488, the brightest of these AGNs, can be observed with SKA with 10 - 100 hr integration times.

gmw_00009685.name: ID 11836
gmw_00009685.overview: ID 11836 is a high-redshift active galactic nucleus host galaxy [arXiv:2308.01230]. Its inferred dynamical mass is approximately two orders of magnitude higher than its estimated stellar mass, which may be significantly underestimated due to the difficulty of accounting for the active galactic nucleus contribution [arXiv:2308.01230].

sources.summary: The paper highlights ID 11836 as a remarkable JADES case where dynamical mass exceeds stellar mass by about two orders of magnitude, possibly from underestimated stellar mass.

quotes.quote: We will discuss that, when compared with the dynamical masses, in the case of ID 954 the stellar mass is probably overestimated (likely because of this is the most luminous AGN in our sample and therefore it is more difficult to disentangle host galaxy and AGN components), while for ID 11836 the stellar mass might be underestimated (but see discussion in Sect. sec:mbh_mdyn ).

The specific case of ID 11836 is remarkable, as the inferred dynamical mass is about two orders of magnitude higher than the stellar mass. It might be that in this case the stellar mass is significantly underestimated owing the difficulty to account for the AGN contribution, and indeed not including the power-law component gives a stellar mass that is 15 times higher; however, it is also true that previous studies at z < 4 have found some galaxies with inferred gas masses that are nearly two order of magnitudes higher than the stellar masses Scoville2017,Zhang2021,Liu2019 . h ID 954 is also another extreme case in which the inferred dynamical mass is about one order of magnitude lower than the stellar mass.

gmw_00009686.name: NGC 5614
gmw_00009686.overview: NGC 5614 is an interacting galaxy located within the Arp 178 triplet [arXiv:2401.13181]. The galaxy exhibits highly disturbed ionized-gas kinematics and shows recent star formation concentrated in its central regions [arXiv:2401.13181].

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC 5614 as an EDGE database example, describing it as an interacting Arp 178 triplet galaxy with central recent star formation and disturbed ionized-gas kinematics.

quotes.quote: We display cumulative star formation histories for two EDGE galaxies, NGC 3687 (which has the prominent H ring shown in Figure fig:dotplot ), and NGC 5614 (an interacting galaxy in the Arp 178 triplet) in Figure fig:sfhmass , along with the corresponding radial profiles of specific SFR (sSFR= _ SFR / _* ) based on extinction-corrected H emission. Despite the assumptions involved in recovering the SFH, the radial age trends implied by the SFH profiles are in qualitative agreement with the sSFR profiles, which show high sSFR values (indicative of a recent buildup of stellar mass) at large radii in NGC 3687 and at small radii in NGC 5614. We note that the ionized gas kinematics in NGC 5614 are highly disturbed Barrera:15 , consistent with a recent inflow of gas towards the central regions that was triggered by the interaction.

gmw_00009687.name: MCG-01-12-005
gmw_00009687.overview: MCG-01-12-005 is a central giant elliptical galaxy located in the X-ray cluster EXO 0422-086 [arXiv:1503.04442]. It features two same-redshift absorption-line nuclei, with the southern nucleus likely representing the remnant of a merging satellite galaxy [arXiv:1503.04442].

sources.summary: The paper shows MCG-01-12-005 is a central giant elliptical/cD galaxy with two same-redshift absorption-line nuclei, likely a merger involving a satellite remnant.

quotes.quote: The galaxy MCG-01-12-005 is the giant elliptical in the center of the X-ray cluster EXO 0422-086. A detailed X-ray study of the cluster with Chandra (Belsole et al. 2005) ruled out the presence of an X-ray emitting AGN, showing that the emission is due to the intracluster medium and the interstellar medium of the central elliptical galaxy. Our optical observations showed two clearly separated nuclei 3" that are located apart and are nearly aligned in the N-S direction. The optical spectra of these nuclei are very similar, with no emission lines, showing the absorption features typical of an elliptical galaxy: both sources have the same redshift, z=0.04, consistent with previous data in the literature, which did not mention these two nuclei.

Images from the SDSS confirm the presence of the two nuclei. A morphological study of the isophotes shows that the northern nucleus is located in the light barycenter of the galaxy, suggesting that it is also in the mass center. A radio high-resolution image taken with the VLA (Belsole et al. 2005) shows two small sources separated by few arcsecs, nearly symmetric to the northern optical nucleus. The southern nucleus is most likely the remnant of a satellite galaxy with a velocity vector nearly in the plane of the sky.

gmw_00009688.name: J1101+7225
gmw_00009688.overview: J1101+7225 is a radio-loud quasar located at a redshift of z=1.46 [arXiv:astro-ph/0504552]. Very Long Baseline Interferometry observations reveal that the active nucleus hosts a complex jet structure exhibiting high apparent superluminal motion at large distances from the core [arXiv:astro-ph/0504552].

sources.summary: The paper analyzes VLBI observations of the radio-loud quasar J1101+7225, finding complex jet structure, distant apparent superluminal motion, compact core emission, and unusually broad jet-kinematic behavior.

quotes.quote: Multi-epoch GHz Very Long Baseline Interferometry (VLBI) data of the radio-loud quasar J1101+7225 were analyzed to estimate the proper motion of extended optically thin jet components. Two components separated from the core could be mapped at 1.66 GHz, which is consistent with earlier observations. In one case we found evidence of high apparent superluminal motion ( _ app = 22.5 4 ) at large (deprojected) distances to the core (22 mas 4 kpc, at z= 1.46 ) . Furthermore the Doppler factor, the magnetic field strength, and the angular size of the optically thick core were derived using published X-ray data. Analysis of 5 GHz VLBI data reveals the existence of further jet components within the central 5 mas.

J1101+7225 turns out to be a standard quasar for studying different aspects of radio jet kinematics out to kpc-scales.
